Description/Title: Retain the VCF Automation activity log for the audit window and keep debug output disabled.
Discussion:
VCF Automation's activity log records administrative and tenant actions taken through the platform: who did what, to what, and when. It is the audit trail for changes made through VCF Automation. Log history retention controls how many days entries are kept before they are purged; if it is shorter than the window your investigations and audit obligations require, the record of an action can be gone before anyone looks for it, so set it to cover that window. A separate setting limits only how far back the on-screen view reaches and does not affect retention. Display of debug information adds internal paths and configuration detail to the log output; left disabled, as it ships, the log does not expose that internal detail to whoever can read it.

Not applicable if VCF Automation is not deployed.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Extending activity-log retention keeps the record of administrative and tenant actions available for later investigation, bounded by the storage the log consumes. Too short a retention purges the audit trail before a change can be reviewed. Enabling debug output places internal path and configuration detail into the log, widening what a reader of the log can learn about the system.
Installation Default Value: Log history retained 90 days; debug information display disabled
Baseline Suggested Value: Log history retained to cover the organization's audit-retention requirement (longer than the default); debug information display disabled
Setting Location: UI: VCF Automation Provider interface > Administration > General Settings

Description/Title: Bound VCF Automation OIDC token lifetimes and keep redirect targets HTTPS-only.
Discussion:
When VCF Automation federates authentication to an OpenID Connect (OIDC) identity provider, these settings govern the lifetimes of the tokens it issues and which callback URLs it will return them to. The access token lifetime sets how long an issued access token is accepted; keep it aligned with the access-token and session lifetimes used elsewhere in the environment so a captured access token does not outlive its peers. The refresh token lifetime sets how long a refresh token can be used to obtain new access tokens without re-authenticating, so a longer refresh lifetime means a stolen refresh token keeps yielding fresh access tokens for that period. The redirect URL policy controls which callback URLs the service will return tokens to; restricting it to HTTPS-only targets, as it ships, keeps authorization responses from being sent to an unencrypted endpoint where they could be intercepted.

Not applicable if VCF Automation is not deployed or OIDC federation is not configured.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Shorter access and refresh token lifetimes cause clients to obtain new tokens and re-authenticate more often; set them short enough to limit the reuse window of a captured token but long enough not to disrupt federated logins and integrations. Allowing non-HTTPS redirect URLs, or leaving token lifetimes long, widens the window and the channel through which an OIDC token could be captured and replayed.
Configuration Parameter: Access Token Lifetime, Refresh Token Lifetime, Redirect URL Policy
Installation Default Value: Access token lifetime 3600 seconds; refresh token lifetime 604800 seconds; redirect URL policy HTTPS-only
Baseline Suggested Value: Access token lifetime 900 seconds; refresh token lifetime 86400 seconds; redirect URL policy HTTPS-only
Setting Location: UI: VCF Automation Provider interface > Administration > OIDC Services > General Settings

Description/Title: Configure operation limits in VCF Automation to bound resource-intensive operations.
Discussion:
VCF Automation lets tenants invoke resource-intensive operations (deployments, deletions, large catalog actions) on shared infrastructure. Without explicit per-operation limits, a misbehaving or malicious tenant can saturate the orchestration plane or the downstream targets it manages. That saturation is a denial-of-service against other tenants of the platform. Configure each resource-intensive operation limit to a bounded value rather than leaving it unlimited.

Not applicable if VCF Automation is not deployed.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Legitimate large-scale automation jobs may be slowed or rejected when the operation limit is reached. Tune the limits upward where heavy workloads are expected rather than reverting to unlimited. Leaving operation limits unlimited preserves the saturation risk this control closes.
Baseline Suggested Value: Each Operation Limit set to a bounded value (between 1 and 100000); none configured as unlimited
Setting Location: UI: VCF Automation Provider interface > Administration > General Settings > Operation Limits

Description/Title: Limit the VCF Automation API token lifetime.
Discussion:
VCF Automation issues API tokens for programmatic access, and the API token lifetime caps how long an issued token remains valid. The default is long enough that a token captured from a script, a log, a CI system, or a backup stays usable to an attacker for an extended period, and a value of 0 removes the limit entirely so the token never expires. Set it to a bounded, finite value: the shortest maximum that still meets the needs of the integrations that depend on API tokens, and never 0.

The section's other settings (host refresh frequency, host hung timeout, and transfer session timeout) tune host-health polling and upload-task handling. They are operational timeouts, not security controls, so this control addresses only the API token lifetime.

Not applicable if VCF Automation is not deployed.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Shorter API token lifetimes mean integrations renew their tokens more often; set the value short enough to limit the reuse window of a captured token but long enough not to break automated workflows. A long lifetime, or 0 (unlimited), leaves a captured token usable indefinitely.
Baseline Suggested Value: API token lifetime set to a bounded, finite value (not 0, which is unlimited), reduced to the shortest maximum that meets operational needs
Setting Location: UI: VCF Automation Provider interface > Administration > General Settings > Timeouts

Description/Title: The ESX host must enforce an unlock timeout.
Discussion: This setting controls how long an ESX local account stays locked out after exceeding the failed-login limit. The timeout paces password-guessing attempts (also called brute forcing) without permanently locking out a legitimate user who typoed their password.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Setting this value to 0 prevents accounts from unlocking automatically, requiring administrative intervention to restore access after a lockout. Values below the recommended value shorten the lockout window and reduce the friction the lockout imposes on a password-guessing attempt.

Description/Title: The ESX host must enforce a limit on consecutive invalid logon attempts by a user.
Discussion: After this many consecutive failed login attempts, the ESX local account is temporarily locked, blocking further attempts until the lockout duration expires or an administrator resets it manually. The lockout is the primary defense against online password-guessing (brute forcing). Setting the threshold too low is itself a denial-of-service avenue: automated systems retrying connections, such as SSH from an orchestrator, can trip the lockout and disable accounts that are still in legitimate use. The recommended value limits brute-force attempts while tolerating occasional mistyped passwords by legitimate users.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Lowering the threshold increases accidental lockouts from automation (for example, SSH connection retries) and creates a denial-of-service avenue. Raising it weakens the lockout against password-guessing attempts.

Description/Title: The ESX host must enforce password history for users.
Discussion: Password history is the number of previous passwords ESX remembers and refuses to accept on a password change. The intent is to discourage password reuse: if an old password has been disclosed, history forces the user to choose a different value rather than rotating straight back to the compromised one. The product default satisfies many regulatory compliance requirements.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Setting the value to 0 disables password history and allows immediate reuse, which may conflict with regulatory compliance requirements but is otherwise acceptable. Setting a very high value forces longer chains of distinct passwords, which can push users toward predictable patterns.

Description/Title: The ESX host must not automatically grant administrative permissions to Active Directory groups.
Discussion: When ESX hosts are joined to Active Directory, the "Config.HostAgent.plugins.hostsvc.esxAdminsGroupAutoAdd" setting controls whether membership in the AD group named by "Config.HostAgent.plugins.hostsvc.esxAdminsGroup" automatically receives administrative permissions on ESX. With auto-add enabled, anyone able to add a user to that AD group (an Active Directory administrator, for example) effectively gains root access on every ESX host joined to the domain. Disable auto-add and grant ESX administrative permissions through vCenter's role and permission model instead.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Access must be granted manually through vCenter's role and permission model instead of being inherited automatically from Active Directory group membership. Leaving auto-add enabled means anyone with rights to modify the configured AD group can grant themselves root on every ESX host in the domain.

Description/Title: The ESX host must not use the default "ESX Admins" Active Directory group.
Discussion: When an ESX host is joined to Active Directory, the Active Directory group named in "Config.HostAgent.plugins.hostsvc.esxAdminsGroup" is granted administrative permissions on ESX. The default value "ESX Admins" is a well-known name: an attacker or a careless Active Directory administrator can create or repurpose that group to grant themselves ESX root access. Change the value to a site-specific group whose membership is tightly controlled. ESX hosts not joined to Active Directory should leave this setting empty.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Changing the group name to a site-specific value adds the operational step of provisioning an AD group with appropriate membership before ESX joins the domain. Leaving the default "ESX Admins" group in place lets anyone who can create or modify that AD group obtain root access on every ESX host that joins the domain.
Baseline Suggested Value: Site-Specific Active Directory group, or empty for non-AD-joined hosts

Description/Title: The ESX host must not disable validation of users and groups.
Discussion: When an ESX host is joined to Active Directory, ESX periodically re-validates the membership of any AD groups granted permissions on the host. The interval is set in minutes by the "Config.HostAgent.plugins.vimsvc.authValidateInterval" parameter. Without periodic validation, a user removed from an AD group in Active Directory continues to hold their ESX permissions until ESX next syncs or restarts, opening a window where revoked users still have access. The default is the longest interval recommended; a value of 0 disables validation entirely and should not be used.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Setting the value to 0 disables AD group membership validation, allowing users removed from privileged AD groups to retain ESX access until the host restarts or the setting is re-enabled. Setting a value above the recommended value lengthens the window during which revoked AD users still hold ESX permissions.

Description/Title: The ESX host must configure a session timeout for the vSphere API.
Discussion: The vSphere API (specifically the vmacore SOAP service) handles authenticated requests from PowerCLI, automation, and the host UI. The "Config.HostAgent.vmacore.soap.sessionTimeout" parameter sets the idle timeout for these sessions in minutes (despite the value appearing as a small integer). Without an idle timeout, an unattended SOAP session can stay alive indefinitely, giving anyone with subsequent access to the originating client a usable authenticated session. The recommended idle timeout aligns with common regulatory compliance requirements without disrupting ordinary automation and management workflows.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Setting this value to 0 disables the session idle timeout, allowing SOAP sessions to remain authenticated indefinitely. Very short timeouts (under a few minutes) cause automation that pauses between API calls to re-authenticate frequently, which can fail jobs that don't handle re-auth.

Description/Title: Disable the ESX Customer Experience Improvement Program.
Discussion: The Customer Experience Improvement Program (CEIP) collects product usage and diagnostic data from the ESX host and sends it to the vendor for product improvement; on ESX this is governed by the Host Client CEIP opt-in setting. The data describes how the product is configured and operated; it is not customer workload data. Organizations that restrict outbound data from the management environment, or that operate the management plane in an isolated network, opt out so the host does not originate that traffic. This is a data-handling policy choice that reduces outbound communication from the management plane rather than a control that changes the security state of the host.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling CEIP removes a feedback channel to the vendor for product improvement data; it does not affect ESX function or the supportability of unrelated cases. Leaving it enabled continues that outbound reporting.
Setting Location: UI: ESX Host Client (advanced setting UserVars.HostClientCEIPOptIn)

Description/Title: The ESX host must not suppress warnings about unmitigated hyperthreading vulnerabilities.
Discussion: ESX displays a warning in vCenter when the host runs with hyperthreading (SMT) enabled on a CPU that has known speculative-execution vulnerabilities (such as L1TF/Foreshadow or MDS-class side-channel attacks) without a mitigation applied. The "UserVars.SuppressHyperthreadWarning" parameter lets administrators hide that warning. Suppressing the warning does not change the underlying risk; it only hides the indicator. Leave the warning enabled so operators can see CPUs that need firmware or scheduling-policy remediation. If the residual risk is accepted by the organization, document that decision separately rather than silencing the indicator.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Setting this value to 1 suppresses the hyperthreading vulnerability warning in vCenter. The host's exposure to CPU side-channel attacks is unchanged; only the visibility to operators is affected. Suppressing the warning makes it more likely that unmitigated CPUs go unnoticed and unremediated.

Description/Title: Set a timeout to automatically terminate idle DCUI sessions on the ESX host.
Discussion: The Direct Console User Interface (DCUI) is the text-based console that ESX shows on the host's physical or virtual screen. It accepts local root login and is most often used during initial setup or for emergency recovery. The "UserVars.DcuiTimeOut" parameter sets an idle timeout in seconds for active DCUI sessions. An idle DCUI session left logged in at a console (physical or IPMI/KVM) gives anyone with subsequent console access a privileged shell on the host. An idle timeout closes that window automatically.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Setting this value to 0 disables the idle timeout, leaving DCUI sessions logged in indefinitely after the operator walks away. Setting a very short value forces operators to re-authenticate at the console frequently, which can slow troubleshooting during incident response.

Description/Title: The ESX Managed Object Browser (MOB) must be disabled.
Discussion: The Managed Object Browser (MOB) is a web-based view of the vSphere API object hierarchy on an ESX host. It is primarily a debugging aid for low-level support cases and is not used during normal operations. When enabled, MOB exposes a large surface of internal API objects and methods over HTTPS on the host. Disable MOB unless a support engineer specifically requires it for an active investigation, and re-disable it afterward.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling MOB prevents debugging access to the vSphere API object hierarchy via web browser. Support engineers troubleshooting low-level issues may ask to re-enable it temporarily; re-disable it after the investigation. Leaving MOB enabled exposes a large API surface that authenticated attackers can use to enumerate host configuration and call internal methods.

Description/Title: The ESX Shell must be disabled.
Discussion: The ESX Shell is the local command-line interface to the host (the TSM service), accessible from the DCUI by pressing Alt+F1 or from the host's serial console. It provides root-equivalent access to the underlying VMkernel and POSIX environment, parallel to the vSphere API. Most ongoing administration is better performed through vCenter or PowerCLI, both of which leave more useful audit trails. Keep the shell service stopped and policy set to 'Start and stop manually' so it cannot be auto-started on reboot. Turn it on for the duration of a troubleshooting session and stop it again afterward.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling the ESX Shell removes the local interactive root environment on each host. Troubleshooting that requires shell access (ad-hoc esxcli or esxcfg-* commands, log inspection at the file level) must be performed by enabling the shell for the duration of the work, then stopping it again. Leaving the shell running keeps a long-lived privileged interface on the host that bypasses vCenter's audit trail and remains a potential entry point if host root credentials are ever compromised.
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
Get-VMHostService -VMHost $ESX | Where-Object {$_.Key -eq 'TSM'} | Set-VMHostService -Policy Off
Get-VMHostService -VMHost $ESX | Where-Object {$_.Key -eq 'TSM'} | Stop-VMHostService

Description/Title: The ESX host must disable shell access for the dcui account.
Discussion: The "dcui" account is a system account used internally by the Direct Console User Interface service to perform privilege-separation tasks. It is not meant for interactive use. Granting shell access to this account means that anyone who can authenticate as "dcui" can drop into an ESX shell and run commands as that account. Set the account's shell access to "false" so the dcui account cannot be used as an interactive entry point even if its credentials are obtained.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling shell access for the dcui account does not affect normal ESX operations or the DCUI itself. Leaving shell access enabled gives the system account an interactive entry point that an attacker could use after compromising its credentials.

Description/Title: The ESX host must use sufficient entropy for cryptographic operations.
Discussion: Cryptographic operations on the ESX host (TLS sessions, key generation, random tokens) require a steady supply of unpredictable bytes. Entropy is the term for the unpredictability of those bytes; running short of high-quality entropy weakens any keys or nonces generated during that period. ESX collects entropy from multiple sources, including a hardware random number generator (HWRNG) when the CPU exposes one. The "disableHwrng" kernel option controls whether the HWRNG is used, and "entropySources" selects which combination of sources seeds the entropy pool. Leaving both at their defaults uses the recommended mix of sources.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Setting "disableHwrng" to TRUE removes the hardware entropy source from the pool, leaving ESX dependent on software-only entropy collection, which may be slower to accumulate enough randomness for high-volume cryptographic operations. Changing "entropySources" from the default substitutes a non-default mix of sources, which ESX's certified cryptographic subsystem may not accept; this can cause cryptographic operations to block waiting for entropy or to fail outright.

Description/Title: Configure the login banner for ESX host SSH connections.
Discussion: The "Config.Etc.issue" advanced setting holds the text shown to users connecting to the ESX host via SSH. The banner is a pre-authentication legal notice that communicates terms of use and any monitoring policy in effect; it does not enforce or block anything technically. Many organizations have specific legal text that must appear on management interfaces. Note the case-sensitive lowercase "issue" in the parameter name: ESX and vCenter handle the casing differently, and using lowercase ensures the setting works on both.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Setting or removing the banner has no functional impact on ESX or its workloads. The banner is an informational notice shown before authentication; its absence does not weaken any technical security control. Use text that reflects the organization's policy and consult legal counsel for appropriate language.
Baseline Suggested Value:
Consult your organization's legal advisors for text that is applicable to your environment.

An example is: "Authorized users only. Actual or attempted unauthorized use of this system is prohibited and may result in criminal, civil, security, or administrative proceedings and/or penalties. Use of this information system indicates consent to monitoring and recording, without notice or permission. Users have no expectation of privacy in any use of this system. Any information stored on, or transiting this system, or obtained by monitoring and/or recording, may be disclosed to law enforcement and/or used in accordance with Federal law, State statute, and organization policy. If you are not an authorized user of this system, exit the system at this time."
PowerCLI Command Remediation: Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-AdvancedSetting Config.Etc.issue | Set-AdvancedSetting -Value "****************************************************************************`n* Authorized users only. Actual or attempted unauthorized use of this      *`n* system is prohibited and may result in criminal, civil, security, or     *`n* administrative proceedings and/or penalties. Use of this information     *`n* system indicates consent to monitoring and recording, without notice     *`n* or permission. Users have no expectation of privacy. Any information     *`n* stored on or transiting this system, or obtained by monitoring and/or    *`n* recording, may be disclosed to law enforcement and/or used in accordance *`n* with Federal law, State statute, and organization policy. If you are not *`n* an authorized user of this system, exit the system at this time.         *`n****************************************************************************`n"

Description/Title: The ESX host must configure the firewall to block network traffic by default.
Discussion: ESX has a built-in stateless firewall with per-service rulesets. The "default block" policy controls what happens to traffic that is not matched by any enabled ruleset: when the policy is Block, unmatched traffic is dropped; when it is Allow, unmatched traffic is permitted. With block-by-default, adding or removing services on the host does not silently expose them to the network. Keep the default block policy enabled and grant traffic by enabling specific rulesets for the services that actually need network reachability.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Changing the default policy to Allow opens every service running on the host to inbound network traffic that isn't explicitly blocked by another rule, including services that were never intended to be reachable from outside the host. Keeping the default Block policy means new services must be explicitly allowed via a ruleset, which is the safer default. No workload impact in either direction; the change affects only management-network reachability of host services.

Description/Title: Configure the ESX host firewall to only allow traffic from authorized networks.
Discussion: Each enabled ESX firewall ruleset can be configured to accept connections from a specific list of IP addresses or CIDR ranges. By default, rulesets allow connections from any IP address ("allow all"). Restricting each ruleset to only the management subnets, jump hosts, and infrastructure endpoints that legitimately need to reach the host limits how far an attacker can reach from a compromised network position. Note that the ESX firewall enforces ingress rules only; it does not maintain connection state, so allowed-IP lists must be configured for each service that should be reachable.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Restricting allowed IP addresses on a ruleset blocks traffic from any IP outside the configured list, including legitimate administration sources that were not previously inventoried. Plan the allowed-IP list to include all jump hosts, monitoring tools, automation runners, and backup endpoints that need reachability before applying. Leaving the default "allow all" exposes the service to any reachable network position.
Installation Default Value: Connections allowed from any IP address
Baseline Suggested Value: Connections allowed only from authorized infrastructure and administration workstations
PowerCLI Command Assessment:
$ESXcli = Get-EsxCli -VMHost $ESX -V2
$list = $ESXcli.network.firewall.ruleset.list.Invoke() | Where-Object {($_.AllowedIPconfigurable -eq $true) -and ($_.EnableDisableconfigurable -eq $true)} | Select-Object -ExpandProperty Name

$arguments = $ESXcli.network.firewall.ruleset.allowedip.list.CreateArgs()
foreach ($rule in $list) {
    $arguments.rulesetid = $rule
    $ESXcli.network.firewall.ruleset.allowedip.list.Invoke($arguments)
}
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
# This is an example which you will want to customize!
$ESXcli = Get-EsxCli -VMHost $ESX -V2
# Deactivate firewall temporarily so we don't lose connectivity
$arguments = $ESXcli.network.firewall.set.CreateArgs()
$arguments.enabled = $false
$ESXcli.network.firewall.set.Invoke($arguments)

# Unset the "allow all" flag
$arguments = $ESXcli.network.firewall.ruleset.set.CreateArgs()
$arguments.allowedall = $false
$arguments.rulesetid = "sshServer"
$ESXcli.network.firewall.ruleset.set.Invoke($arguments)

# Add an IP range
$arguments = $ESXcli.network.firewall.ruleset.allowedip.add.CreateArgs()
$arguments.ipaddress = "192.168.0.0/16"
$arguments.rulesetid = "sshServer"
$ESXcli.network.firewall.ruleset.allowedip.add.Invoke($arguments)

# Enable the firewall
$arguments = $ESXcli.network.firewall.set.CreateArgs()
$arguments.enabled = $true
$ESXcli.network.firewall.set.Invoke($arguments)

Description/Title: Leave forensic memory snapshots disabled except on hosts used for deliberate memory capture.
Discussion:
ESX normally declines to take a memory snapshot of a virtual machine that it cannot guarantee it can restore, such as a VM with independent disks. A forensic-snapshot option relaxes that rule so an investigator can capture the live memory state of such a VM for analysis. When the option is off, the host's normal restorability protection stays in force.

Keeping it off avoids two problems. A snapshot taken under this relaxation may not be restorable, so an operator who treats it as a recovery point has a backup that will not restore. Separately, a memory snapshot is a copy of the VM's running memory, which can contain decrypted secrets such as keys, credentials, and in-flight data; allowing these snapshots broadly multiplies the on-disk copies of that sensitive state. Enable the option only on hosts where a deliberate memory-forensics process is in use, and remove the resulting snapshots when the analysis is complete.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Leaving the option disabled does not affect normal snapshots of restorable VMs. Enabling it lets operators create memory snapshots of VMs that may not be restorable, which can be mistaken for valid recovery points and which retain copies of the VM's memory contents.

Description/Title: The environment must use adequately sized persistent, non-SD, non-USB devices for ESX boot volumes.
Discussion: Flash memory wears out over time, with each data write shortening its lifespan. SSDs and NVMe devices have built-in features (wear leveling, over-provisioning) that reduce this wear, making them suitable for long-term use. SD cards and most USB flash drives lack these features and can develop bad sectors silently. To extend SD and USB lifespan, ESX redirects audit and system logs to a RAM disk on those devices rather than writing them to flash. That workaround requires administrators to configure persistent remote log storage and adjust each log destination accordingly. Choosing a reliable, persistent boot device (SSD, NVMe, or a small dedicated boot disk) removes those extra steps and avoids the log-loss and stability risks that come with worn-out boot media. Local persistence also matters during incident response: without a reliable on-host log and state store, forensic investigation depends entirely on whatever telemetry happened to reach a remote collector before the event.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Using SD cards or USB flash drives as ESX boot devices accelerates flash wear and requires the operator to redirect audit and system logs to persistent remote storage. On worn-out boot media, ESX can fail to boot or develop silent corruption in its on-disk state. Using persistent storage (SSD, NVMe, or a small dedicated boot disk) eliminates those concerns.
Baseline Suggested Value: Persistent SSD or NVMe boot device, not SD card or USB

Description/Title: Restrict the server firmware boot order to authorized boot devices.
Discussion:
Server firmware (BIOS/UEFI) decides which devices it will attempt to boot, and in what order. When the boot order permits booting from removable media such as USB or optical drives, from an external network through PXE, or from controller-presented virtual media, an attacker with physical access or with access to the management controller can boot the host into an operating system they control. From there they can bypass the protections of the installed system and read or alter data on the local disks. Restrict the firmware boot order to the authorized internal boot device, and disable booting from removable, optical, and network sources where they are not required.

This control limits the opportunity to boot unauthorized media; UEFI Secure Boot, configured separately, verifies the signatures of whatever does boot, and the two work together. Account for legitimate uses of alternate boot sources before disabling them: hosts that are deployed or run statelessly over the network boot by PXE design, and provisioning or recovery workflows may need removable or virtual media for a time. Where the management controller can present virtual media to the host, restrict that capability as well, because it offers the same alternate-boot path over the network.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Restricting the boot order has no effect on a host that boots normally from its internal device. If removable, optical, or network boot is later needed for provisioning, reimaging, or recovery, an administrator must re-enable the required boot source in firmware first, and hosts that are deployed over the network must keep network boot enabled. Leaving the boot order permissive lets anyone with physical or virtual-media access boot the host from media they control.
Baseline Suggested Value: Boot order restricted to authorized boot devices, booting from removable media, PXE, and virtual media disabled

Description/Title: Enable AMD SEV-ES and SEV-SNP in system firmware where available, and size the SEV ASID pool appropriately.
Discussion: AMD EPYC platforms include two confidential-computing technologies for virtual machines: SEV-ES (Secure Encrypted Virtualization with Encrypted State, which encrypts the VM's CPU register state across each VM exit) and SEV-SNP (Secure Nested Paging, which adds memory-integrity protection and remote attestation, letting a guest VM cryptographically prove its runtime state to an external verifier). Both establish a Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) around the VM: a memory and CPU-state boundary that even the hypervisor and host operating system cannot read in plaintext. VCF can expose these to guest VMs and containers when the hardware features are enabled in system firmware. The Minimum SEV non-ES ASID firmware parameter divides the limited pool of address space identifiers between plain SEV and SEV-ES/SNP; size it for the expected mix of protected VMs.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Once enabled in firmware, SEV-ES and SEV-SNP do not affect VMs that do not opt in. VMs that do enable confidential computing require guest OS support for SEV-ES/SNP and lose access to some VCF features that need special treatment to cross the Trusted Execution Environment boundary, including vMotion of protected VMs, memory snapshots, and certain backup integrations. Plan around the per-VM tradeoffs before designating workloads for confidential mode.
Baseline Suggested Value: SEV-ES and SEV-SNP enabled where available, SEV ASID pool sized appropriately

Description/Title: Enable Intel SGX and TDX in system firmware where available.
Discussion: Intel Xeon Scalable Processor platforms include two confidential-computing technologies: SGX (Software Guard Extensions, which lets an application create encrypted enclaves of memory that even the kernel cannot read) and TDX (Trust Domain Extensions, which extends similar protection to an entire virtual machine, providing memory encryption and CPU state isolation). Both establish Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs): SGX at the application scope (per-process encrypted enclaves), TDX at the VM scope (entire confidential VMs). Both also support remote attestation, letting the protected enclave or VM cryptographically prove its runtime state to an external verifier. VCF supports both inside guest VMs and containers when the hardware features are enabled in system firmware.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Once enabled in firmware, SGX and TDX do not affect VMs or applications that do not opt in. Applications using SGX enclaves and VMs using TDX require guest OS support and lose access to some VCF features that need special treatment to cross the Trusted Execution Environment boundary, including vMotion, memory snapshots, fault tolerance, and suspend/resume. Plan around the per-workload tradeoffs before designating applications or VMs for confidential mode.
Baseline Suggested Value: SGX and TDX enabled where available

Description/Title: Enable Intel TXT in system firmware where available.
Discussion: Intel Trusted Execution Technology (TXT) provides a hardware-anchored way to verify the host's boot chain on Xeon Scalable Processor platforms. At boot, TXT records a cryptographic hash of each boot component (UEFI firmware, bootloader, kernel) into the Trusted Platform Module before passing control to the next stage. vCenter compares those hashes against known-good values to detect tampering with any boot component; this comparison is called attestation. Enabling TXT in firmware unlocks attestation and measured boot without affecting host operation when no tampering is detected.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Once TXT is enabled, attestation alarms in vCenter can fire during operations that legitimately change the boot chain (firmware updates, certain abrupt shutdowns) and clear after the host is cold-booted or its firmware update is completed. See KB 78243 for known scenarios. Leaving TXT disabled means vCenter cannot verify the host's boot chain, so tampering with firmware, bootloader, or kernel goes undetected.

Description/Title: Ensure all system and device firmware is auditable, authentic, and up to date.
Discussion: Firmware (BMC/iLO/iDRAC, BIOS/UEFI, NIC, HBA, NVMe, storage controller) runs below the operating system and survives reboots. A vulnerability in any of these components can give an attacker persistence that ESX itself cannot detect or remove. Apply firmware updates supplied by the hardware vendor on a regular cadence, and verify that downloaded firmware images carry vendor signatures or come from vendor-controlled distribution channels. Keep records of the firmware versions in use so that an inventory check can determine which hosts are exposed to a newly disclosed firmware vulnerability.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Applying a firmware update typically requires a host reboot and brief downtime. Some firmware updates change the measured boot chain and trigger attestation alarms; clear those once the update is confirmed (see esx-9.hardware-cpu-intel-txt impact). For vSAN clusters, ensure both storage device firmware and controller firmware versions remain on the vSAN HCL after updating, to avoid unsupported configurations. Skipping firmware updates leaves disclosed firmware-level vulnerabilities exposed; firmware compromises can persist across OS reinstalls.
Baseline Suggested Value: Current vendor-signed firmware on all components, firmware version inventory maintained

Description/Title: Use strong, isolated identity management on hardware management controllers.
Discussion: Hardware management controllers (BMC, iLO, iDRAC, IPMI) provide out-of-band remote console, power control, and firmware update capabilities for the server hardware. Compromising a management controller gives an attacker bare-metal control over the host, so controllers must be treated as Tier 0 administrative infrastructure: the highest-privilege tier whose compromise yields control of everything below it. Their authentication and authorization should be isolated from the systems the controllers manage and from the organization's general-purpose identity infrastructure. Centralized identity providers (IdPs) are themselves high-value attacker targets, and a single compromise of the IdP yields privileged access to every system that federates to it. Two anti-patterns specifically to avoid: authenticating controller users through an Active Directory that runs on hosts the controllers can power-cycle (a recovery dependency loop), and granting controller access through AD group membership that any AD administrator can modify (extends every AD compromise to bare-metal access). Use local accounts protected by strong unique passwords and, where supported, hardware-token multi-factor authentication. If a centralized identity source is required for these controllers, use a dedicated identity infrastructure that is separate from the organization's main IdP and is not hosted on VCF.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Strong, isolated identity management for hardware management controllers requires per-controller account provisioning and credential rotation, typically scripted through each controller's CLI or REST API. Allowing controllers to authenticate through a centralized identity provider that also serves other systems makes management controllers reachable from any successful attack on that IdP. Allowing controllers to authenticate through the same Active Directory that runs on hosts the controllers manage adds a recovery dependency (an AD outage prevents recovery of the cluster running AD).
Baseline Suggested Value: Strong authentication enabled, identity management isolated from managed systems and general-purpose identity infrastructure

Description/Title: Lock the hardware management controller configuration after provisioning.
Discussion:
Hardware management controllers (BMC, iLO, iDRAC) are fully configurable at any time by default, so anyone who reaches the controller interface, including an attacker who has obtained controller credentials, can change its configuration or push firmware to it. Many controllers offer a configuration lockdown mode that, once a host is provisioned and stable, blocks changes to controller configuration and firmware until an administrator deliberately unlocks it. Enabling this mode after provisioning converts the controller from editable at any time to changeable only through a deliberate unlock, which limits both unintended configuration drift and an attacker's ability to alter the controller or its firmware.

The same mode that blocks an attacker also blocks legitimate firmware updates, and keeping server firmware current is itself an important defense against vulnerabilities that sit below the operating system. Before enabling configuration lock, establish a procedure to unlock the controller, apply firmware and configuration updates, and relock it, so the lock hardens the controller without letting its firmware fall behind.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With configuration lock enabled, firmware updates and configuration changes fail until an administrator unlocks the controller, so firmware-update and maintenance procedures must include an explicit unlock-and-relock step. Without that procedure, the lock can cause server firmware to fall behind on security fixes. Leaving the lock disabled keeps the controller editable at all times, so any access to the controller interface, including through stolen credentials, can change its configuration or firmware.

Description/Title: Forward hardware management controller logs to a central log server.
Discussion: Hardware management controllers (BMC, iLO, iDRAC) maintain their own event log separate from ESX, recording controller logins, power-cycle commands, firmware updates, sensor alerts, and IPMI activity. Without forwarding, these events remain only on the controller and can be wiped or overwritten as the log fills. Configure each controller to send its event log to the central syslog server used for the rest of the infrastructure so that out-of-band administrative activity is visible to security operations alongside in-band activity.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Forwarding BMC, iLO, or iDRAC logs requires a syslog target on the central log server that accepts the format the controller produces (varies by vendor). Without forwarding, an attacker with controller access can wipe the local event log to hide power cycles, firmware tampering, or controller account changes. Centralized retention also lets correlation tools tie out-of-band controller events to in-band ESX events during incident response.
Baseline Suggested Value: Controller event logs forwarded to the central log server

Description/Title: Harden integrated hardware management controllers.
Discussion:
Servers with integrated hardware management controllers (BMC, iLO, iDRAC, IPMI) expose powerful features for monitoring, firmware updates, remote console, and out-of-band management. The same features make controllers attractive targets, so harden each controller across multiple surfaces.

Reduce the service surface: disable unused services and management protocols, including telnet, Serial Over LAN where it is not used, and IPMI over LAN where it is not used. Where IPMI must stay enabled, disable the Cipher 0 option, which permits authentication to be bypassed. Disable the SNMP agent if it is unused; where SNMP is required, use version 3 with authentication and privacy rather than the version 1 or 2c community-string model. Disable USB-driven first-boot and zero-touch provisioning once deployment is complete.

Restrict powerful interactive access paths: disable or restrict the controller's remote console (virtual keyboard, video, and mouse) when it is not in use, because it gives full interactive control of the host along with virtual-media access. Where the remote console is used, limit who can launch it and confirm the session is encrypted. Disable any manufacturer debug or field-service access mode unless support is actively using it, since these modes grant elevated access outside the normal authentication path.

Isolate and restrict network access: the strongest posture is a dedicated physical management interface on a network separate from production traffic, and where that is not available, an isolated management VLAN. Do not bridge the controller onto the same network as host workloads. Restrict the source addresses allowed to reach the management interface using firewalls or ACLs, and enable the controller's login-failure lockout so repeated failed logins temporarily block the source. Secure the controller web and API transport by setting the minimum TLS version to 1.2 or higher and replacing the default self-signed certificate with one issued by a trusted certificate authority.

Secure credentials and physical access: change default account passwords and rotate them per the organization's credential policy, secure external information displays, and protect chassis-mounted power and info buttons against unauthorized use. Enable the controller's built-in alerting for hardware faults and configuration changes if no other monitoring covers those events.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Hardening controllers narrows the management surface, which can affect remote tools that previously relied on default open access. Most hardware management controllers expose CLI and REST API interfaces that can be scripted from a management workstation, reducing the need for vendor-specific management software. Skipping hardening leaves controllers accessible through known default credentials and protocols, which automated scanners regularly probe; a compromised controller gives bare-metal control over the host.
Baseline Suggested Value: Unused controller services and protocols disabled, IPMI Cipher 0 disabled, vendor hardening guidance applied

Description/Title: Synchronize hardware management controller time with organizational reference sources.
Discussion:
Cryptography, audit logging, cluster operations, and incident response all depend on consistent time across infrastructure. Hardware management controllers (BMC, iLO, iDRAC) maintain their own clocks and timestamp their event logs; controller time drift from the rest of the environment makes those logs harder to correlate with vSphere events during an investigation. Configure each controller to use the same NTP sources as the rest of the infrastructure. The recommended NTP topology is one source or three or more; two sources create a split-brain risk in which the controller cannot decide which to trust if the two disagree.

Where the controller supports authenticated NTP, configure a symmetric key so it validates the identity of its time source. This helps prevent an attacker who can reach the management network from feeding the controller a forged time, which could misdate its event logs or move its clock outside the validity window of its certificate.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Synchronizing controller time to the same NTP sources as the rest of the infrastructure has no operational impact on workloads. Controllers with drifted time record event timestamps that do not line up with vSphere logs, which slows or breaks log correlation during incident investigation. Time drift can also cause certificate-validity errors on out-of-band management interfaces if the controller clock falls outside the validity window of its TLS certificate.
Baseline Suggested Value: Controller synchronized to organizational NTP sources, one source or three or more

Description/Title: Disable or physically protect unused external server ports.
Discussion: Unused external ports on physical servers (USB, serial, video, network) can be used by attackers with physical or close-proximity access to attach storage, run keystroke-injection devices, capture console output, or disrupt service. Disable unused ports in the system firmware where possible. Where firmware-level disable is not available, restrict access physically: install solid rack doors, side panels, and floor tiles that block access from outside the rack with the door closed, and route cables so that stiff wires cannot reach exposed buttons through gaps. Where USB ports must remain enabled for occasional administrator use, configure the firmware to accept only HID devices (keyboards) rather than mass storage or network adapters.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling external ports may prevent emergency console access via local USB keyboard or external serial connection. Verify that the chosen approach still permits the recovery scenarios your operations team relies on, including those needed during a complete out-of-band controller failure. Some servers offer dynamically toggleable USB ports that can be enabled briefly when needed and then disabled; that approach preserves both security and recoverability.
Baseline Suggested Value: Unused external ports disabled in firmware or physically protected

Description/Title: Enable UEFI Secure Boot.
Discussion: UEFI Secure Boot uses cryptographic signatures to verify that every component loaded during boot (UEFI bootloader, ESX bootloader, kernel, and signed VIBs) has been issued by a trusted vendor. Components without a valid signature, including malware that has tampered with the boot chain or unsigned modules an attacker has dropped onto the boot disk, are refused. Secure Boot is the first link in the trust chain that the rest of the host's integrity checks depend on; without it, anything else that verifies signatures (TPM attestation, image profile acceptance) is trusting a foundation that cannot be checked.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Enabling Secure Boot causes ESX to refuse to load any boot component or kernel module that is not signed by a key in the host's firmware trust store. VMware VIBs ship signed; third-party drivers may be signed by their vendor, by VMware, or unsigned. Before enabling Secure Boot on existing hosts, run the verification utility (the secureBoot.py script) to confirm every installed VIB has a verifiable signature; an unsigned VIB will block the host from booting. New deployments inherit Secure Boot from the install image and are typically fine.

Description/Title: Install and enable a TPM 2.0 on each ESX host.
Discussion: A Trusted Platform Module (TPM) 2.0 is a hardware chip on the server that provides cryptographic key storage, boot-chain measurement registers (PCRs), and an entropy source. ESX uses the TPM to extend Secure Boot's signature checks into attestation (vCenter verifies the host's boot state from the TPM's measurements), to store keys for the encrypted host configuration, and to seal data so it can only be released when the host has booted into an expected state. Configure the TPM in firmware with SHA-256 hashing and the TIS/FIFO interface; ESX detects the capability automatically once the device is present and enabled.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: A TPM can be added to a deployed host, but it is best specified during procurement. Hosts without a TPM still boot and run workloads normally; they lose access to features that depend on hardware-backed key sealing and attestation (encrypted host configuration, host attestation reports), and an attacker that compromises the host can extract software-only secrets that a TPM would have kept inaccessible.
Baseline Suggested Value: TPM 2.0 installed, enabled. SHA-256 hashing, TIS/FIFO interface

Description/Title: The ESX host must not enable virtual hardware management network interfaces.
Discussion: Some hardware management controllers (BMC, iLO, iDRAC) allow access by the host operating system through a virtual or USB network interface. ESX exposes this capability through the "Net.BMCNetworkEnable" advanced setting. When enabled, the host can communicate with its own management controller over a directly attached virtual network, which third-party tools sometimes use for in-band management. The same path also gives the host's network stack direct access to the management controller, which is a bridge between the data plane (host workloads) and the out-of-band management plane (controller) that should normally stay separated. Disable the virtual hardware management interface unless a specific third-party tool documents a hard requirement for it.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling the virtual hardware management interface prevents the host from talking to its own BMC over the in-band network. Most environments do not use this path, but third-party hardware monitoring agents that run on ESX sometimes depend on it; verify with the agent vendor before disabling on hosts using such tools. Leaving the virtual interface enabled creates a bridge from any compromise of the ESX management plane to the BMC, which exposes the BMC to lateral movement that the physical management network was designed to prevent.

Description/Title: Enable bidirectional/mutual CHAP authentication for iSCSI traffic.
Discussion: CHAP (Challenge-Handshake Authentication Protocol) authenticates iSCSI sessions between the initiator (the ESX host) and the target (the storage array). One-way CHAP has only the initiator prove its identity; mutual CHAP requires both ends to authenticate to each other, which prevents an attacker that controls the network path from impersonating either side during session setup. CHAP authenticates the endpoints but does not encrypt the payload; iSCSI traffic remains in cleartext unless separately protected by network isolation or IPsec. For high-sensitivity storage, also isolate iSCSI traffic onto a dedicated VLAN or use IPsec to provide confidentiality.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Both the ESX initiator and the storage target must support mutual CHAP and be configured with matching credential pairs. Misconfiguration on either side causes the iSCSI session to fail to establish, taking the affected datastores offline. Use a credential-rotation procedure that updates both ends in a maintenance window. Leaving CHAP at the default (none or one-way) lets an attacker on the iSCSI network spoof either side during session setup and gain access to storage or redirect host I/O.
Configuration Parameter: Set iSCSI storage adapter authentication to "Use bidirectional CHAP" and supply credentials
PowerCLI Command Assessment: Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-VMHostHba | Where-Object {$_.Type -eq "Iscsi"} | Select-Object VMHost, Device, ChapType, @{N="CHAPName";E={$_.AuthenticationProperties.ChapName}}

Description/Title: Disable Key Persistence on ESX hosts that do not have controlled physical access.
Discussion:
Key Persistence is a mechanism that caches encryption keys (which would normally be retrieved on demand from an external Key Management System, or KMS) inside the host's encrypted configuration. The encrypted host configuration's own decryption key is sealed by the local Trusted Platform Module (TPM), so it can be decrypted only when the host boots into its expected state. While this setup improves availability (the host can decrypt workloads even when the KMS is unreachable), it changes the encryption risks. The TPM-sealing protects against tampering with the boot chain but not against an attacker who physically removes the host and boots it normally. Under those conditions the TPM unseals the configuration as designed, releasing the cached keys. Therefore, Key Persistence should be used only when the physical security of the hosts is assured.
Key Persistence and Native Key Provider are often conflated because both store encryption data on hosts. However, the Native Key Provider does not use Key Persistence, so turning off Key Persistence won't affect it. Like Key Persistence, the Native Key Provider also requires careful consideration of physical security.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Leaving Key Persistence disabled is the safer default and matches the typical environment using an external Key Management System. Enabling Key Persistence lets the host cache encryption keys locally so it can decrypt workloads without contacting the KMS, useful when the KMS may be unreachable at boot. In environments where an attacker could physically remove a host from the data center, Key Persistence lets the stolen host decrypt the workloads it carried.

Description/Title: Maintain the DCUI.Access list for administrative recovery if Lockdown Mode isolates the host.
Discussion: Lockdown Mode restricts direct host access so that administrators manage the host through vCenter rather than directly. If a host becomes isolated from vCenter while Lockdown Mode is in effect, normal administrative access is blocked. The "DCUI.Access" advanced setting lists the local user accounts that can still log in at the Direct Console User Interface (DCUI) to recover the host in that scenario. Keep this list current so that named operators have a break-glass path to recover an isolated host. The root account cannot be removed from the list, and a separate ESX Shell exception list controls shell access independently. This setting has no effect when the host is in strict lockdown mode, where the DCUI itself is disabled.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Misconfiguring the DCUI.Access list while Lockdown Mode is enabled can leave the host unrecoverable if vCenter is unreachable. Before enabling Lockdown Mode, verify the host is registered with vCenter and that DCUI.Access (and the ESX Shell exception list, if shell recovery is also needed) contains accounts that can be used to recover an isolated host. Note that DCUI.Access is ineffective in strict lockdown mode, where the DCUI itself is disabled.

Description/Title: Keep the Lockdown Mode Exception Users list to the minimum necessary.
Discussion: Lockdown Mode restricts direct host access so that administrators manage the host through vCenter rather than directly. Users on the Exception Users list keep their permissions across the lockdown transition. This is typically used for service accounts such as backup agents or monitoring tools that must continue to authenticate to the host directly even while interactive admin access is restricted. Every account on the list is a deliberate exception to Lockdown Mode, so keep the list minimal: review it periodically and remove entries that no longer have a use. Exception Users and DCUI.Access serve different purposes: Exception Users keep API-level permissions, while DCUI.Access, a separate setting, lists local accounts that can log in at the physical console if the host is isolated from vCenter.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Adding accounts to the Exception Users list extends their privileges through Lockdown Mode, weakening the protection lockdown is meant to provide. Use only for service accounts with a documented operational need; remove entries when the corresponding service is decommissioned. Misconfiguring exception lists or forgetting an essential service account before enabling Lockdown Mode can break automated workflows that depend on direct host access.

Description/Title: The ESX host must enable Lockdown Mode.
Discussion: Enabling Lockdown Mode deactivates direct access to an ESX host so that administration goes through vCenter rather than to the host directly. With direct access blocked, the vCenter roles and access controls cannot be bypassed by logging in to the host, and host-side actions remain auditable through vCenter's audit trail. Some operations, such as backup integrations and low-level troubleshooting, require direct host access; for those, deactivate Lockdown Mode temporarily on the affected host and re-enable it when the task is complete. Before enabling Lockdown Mode, verify the host is registered with vCenter and that the DCUI.Access and Exception Users lists are configured for the operators and service accounts that need a path to the host while lockdown is in effect.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Enabling Lockdown Mode blocks direct access to the host for everyone except accounts on the Exception Users list and the DCUI.Access list. Verify those lists are configured before turning on Lockdown Mode; an incomplete list combined with a vCenter outage can leave the host unreachable. Leaving Lockdown Mode disabled means any operator with host credentials can bypass vCenter's role and audit controls by logging in directly.

Description/Title: The ESX host must transmit audit records to a remote host.
Discussion: ESX maintains an audit log of security-relevant events, including authentication attempts, privilege escalations, and configuration changes. By default this log is kept only on the host. Enabling "Syslog.global.auditRecord.remoteEnable" forwards each audit record to the configured remote syslog destination as it is written, so the same events are available off the host to a central log collector. Without forwarding, an attacker who compromises the host can tamper with or delete local audit records to hide their tracks, and the records also become unavailable for investigation if the host itself is lost or unreachable.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Enabling remote audit-record forwarding sends the same audit events to the central syslog target the host already uses; no workload impact. Leaving forwarding disabled keeps the only copy of the audit log on the host, where an attacker with sufficient access can modify or delete it, and where it is lost along with the host if the host itself is compromised, destroyed, or replaced.

Description/Title: The ESX host must enable audit record logging.
Discussion: ESX maintains an audit log of security-relevant events, including authentication attempts, privilege escalations, and configuration changes. Enabling "Syslog.global.auditRecord.storageEnable" writes those records to local storage on the host so they are persisted between reboots and available for inspection from the ESX shell or via the "/bin/viewAudit" utility. Local audit storage and remote syslog forwarding are complementary: local storage keeps a record on the host even when the network or syslog target is unreachable, while remote forwarding keeps a copy off the host where an attacker with access to the host cannot tamper with it. Both should be enabled.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Enabling local audit-record storage uses host disk space for the audit log. Without local storage enabled, audit records are not persisted on the host, so operators cannot inspect them on the host for troubleshooting, and forensic data is unavailable on-box during an incident.

Description/Title: The ESX host must store audit records for a time period consistent with your records retention policy.
Discussion: ESX writes its audit log to local storage on the host. The "Syslog.global.auditRecord.storageCapacity" setting controls how much storage is reserved for that log. Where remote syslog forwarding is also enabled, local capacity acts as a buffer: if the remote target becomes unreachable, records accumulate locally and are available once the remote target recovers. Size the local capacity to hold the expected backlog for the longest plausible outage of the remote target so that audit records are not lost before they can be forwarded.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Increasing the local audit storage capacity uses host disk space but does not affect workloads. A capacity smaller than the typical backlog during a remote-syslog outage can result in audit records being lost when local storage fills before the remote target becomes reachable again.

Description/Title: Configure a persistent log location for all locally stored audit records on the ESX host.
Discussion:
ESX can be configured to store audit records on an in-memory file system. This occurs when the host's "/scratch" directory is linked to "/tmp/scratch". When this is done only a single day's worth of records are stored at any time. In addition audit records will be reinitialized upon each reboot. This presents a security risk as user activity logged on the host is only stored temporarily and will not persist across reboots. This can also complicate auditing and make it harder to monitor events and diagnose issues. ESX host audit record logging should always be configured to a persistent datastore.

You can detect if the scratch volume is temporary or persistent by querying the "ScratchConfig.CurrentScratchLocation" advanced parameter. If, when queried, it returns "/tmp/scratch" then the volume is temporary and you should remap the audit record storage to a persistent device.

Cannot be a vSAN datastore. If your only local, non-vSAN storage is SD or USB media (which can become unreliable with repeated writes from logs) you might consider leaving the logs in the ramdisk and ensuring that a remote logging host is configured instead. Document the decision and rationale in preparation for future audits.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Routing audit records to a persistent datastore uses host disk space but is the only way to keep records across reboots. If the only available non-vSAN persistent storage is SD or USB media, repeated log writes may shorten its lifespan; leaving logs in the ramdisk and enabling remote syslog forwarding is preferable in that case. Without either persistent local storage or remote forwarding, audit records are lost on every reboot and unavailable for post-incident investigation.
PowerCLI Command Assessment:
$ESXcli = Get-EsxCli -VMHost $ESX -V2
$ESXcli.system.syslog.config.get.Invoke() | Select-Object LocalLogOutput,LocalLogOutputIsPersistent

If your LocalLogOutput is set to a directory in /scratch, and LocalLogOutputIsPersistent is true, that means your boot device is of a type and size that makes /scratch persistent. Verify that your audit storage is also on /scratch, and that /scratch points to a VMFS datastore:

Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-AdvancedSetting ScratchConfig.CurrentScratchLocation
Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-AdvancedSetting Syslog.global.auditRecord.storageDirectory

Description/Title: The ESX host must not enable log filtering.
Discussion: ESX provides a log filtering feature that lets administrators define rules to suppress specific log entries or to limit how often repetitive entries are recorded. While this can reduce log volume, it also removes events from the audit trail that security operations and incident response teams may need. Keep log filtering disabled so all events generated by ESX services reach the log targets unmodified.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Keeping log filtering disabled has no operational impact on workloads; all ESX log events flow to the configured local and remote log targets. Enabling log filtering can drop or rate-limit events that operators and incident responders would otherwise see in the logs, including security-relevant events.

Description/Title: The ESX host must forward system and audit logs to a remote log collection point.
Discussion: By gathering host log files onto a central host, you can more easily monitor all hosts using a single tool. You can also perform aggregate analysis and searching to identify coordinated attacks on multiple hosts. Logging to a secure and centralized log server helps prevent log tampering by attackers with access to a single host and keeps audit records available off the host for long-term retention.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Configuring a remote log destination sends each ESX log message to the syslog server in addition to writing it locally; no workload impact. Without a remote log destination, ESX logs live only on the host, where an attacker with sufficient access can modify or delete them and where they are lost along with the host if the host is compromised, destroyed, or replaced.

Description/Title: The ESX host must verify certificates for TLS remote logging endpoints.
Discussion: When ESX forwards logs to a remote syslog endpoint over TLS, the "Syslog.global.certificate.checkSSLCerts" setting controls whether ESX validates the certificate presented by that endpoint. With certificate checking enabled, ESX confirms the certificate is signed by a trusted certificate authority, matches the configured hostname, and has not expired. Without it, ESX accepts any TLS server's certificate, including a self-signed one presented by an attacker who has positioned themselves on the network between the host and the syslog target.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Certificate validation has no operational impact when the syslog endpoint presents a properly issued certificate. Validation will fail if the syslog server presents a self-signed certificate or a certificate signed by a CA the host does not trust; resolve by issuing a properly signed certificate or adding the CA to the host's trust store. Disabling validation removes that check and lets an attacker on the network path between the host and the syslog target intercept or modify forwarded logs by presenting any TLS certificate.

Description/Title: The ESX host must use strict x509 verification for TLS-enabled remote logging endpoints.
Discussion: With basic certificate verification enabled (controlled by "Syslog.global.certificate.checkSSLCerts"), ESX confirms a TLS syslog endpoint's certificate chains to a trusted CA, matches the hostname, and is unexpired. Strict x509 mode adds further checks defined by RFC 5280, including verification of certificate extensions, basic constraints on intermediate CAs, and key usage requirements. Enabling strict mode reduces the risk that a malformed or misissued certificate is accepted by ESX during syslog forwarding.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Enabling strict x509 mode adds certificate-extension checks during the TLS handshake to the syslog server; if the server's certificate or any CA in its chain has been issued in a non-compliant way, the connection will be refused. Resolve by reissuing a compliant certificate. Leaving strict mode disabled keeps ESX accepting certificates that meet basic verification only, including some that contain extension errors a strict client would catch.

Description/Title: The ESX host must produce audit records containing information to establish what type of events occurred.
Discussion: The "Config.HostAgent.log.level" setting controls the verbosity of events that the ESX host daemon (hostd) records to its log. At the default "info" level, hostd records errors, warnings, and informational events including configuration changes, service starts and stops, user actions, and other operational events relevant to incident investigation. Reducing the level to "warning" or "error" suppresses informational events and removes context that incident responders may need; raising it to "verbose" or higher captures internal diagnostic output that is usually only needed for support cases and generates significantly more log volume.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Keeping the default log level has no impact on workloads. Lowering the level to "warning" or "error" reduces log volume but also drops informational events that operators rely on during troubleshooting and incident investigation. Raising the level to "verbose" or higher generates significantly more output and can fill log storage faster than the operations team expects.

Description/Title: Configure a persistent log location for all locally stored logs on the ESX host.
Discussion:
ESX can be configured to store log files on an in-memory file system. This occurs when the host's "/scratch" directory is linked to "/tmp/scratch". When this is done, only a single day's worth of logs is stored at any time, and log files are reinitialized upon each reboot. This presents a security risk: user activity logged on the host is only stored temporarily and will not persist across reboots. It can also complicate auditing and make it harder to monitor events and diagnose issues. Configure ESX host logging to a persistent datastore.

You can detect if the scratch volume is temporary or persistent by querying the "ScratchConfig.CurrentScratchLocation" advanced parameter. If, when queried, it returns "/tmp/scratch" then the volume is temporary and you should remap the log storage to a persistent device.

Cannot be a vSAN datastore unless "Syslog.global.vsanBacking" is set, which has caveats and dependencies. If your only local, non-vSAN storage is SD or USB media (which can become unreliable with repeated writes from logs) you might consider leaving the logs in the ramdisk and ensuring that a remote logging host is configured instead. Document the decision and rationale in preparation for future audits.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Routing logs to a persistent datastore uses host disk space but is the only way to keep logs across reboots. If the only available non-vSAN persistent storage is SD or USB media, repeated log writes may shorten its lifespan; leaving logs in the ramdisk and enabling remote syslog forwarding is preferable in that case. Without either persistent local storage or remote forwarding, logs are lost on every reboot and unavailable for post-incident investigation.
PowerCLI Command Assessment:
$ESXcli = Get-EsxCli -VMHost $ESX -V2
$ESXcli.system.syslog.config.get.Invoke() | Select-Object LocalLogOutput,LocalLogOutputIsPersistent

If your LocalLogOutput is set to a directory in /scratch, and LocalLogOutputIsPersistent is true, that means your boot device is of a type and size that makes /scratch persistent. Verify that your log storage is also on /scratch, and that /scratch points to a VMFS datastore:

Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-AdvancedSetting ScratchConfig.CurrentScratchLocation
Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-AdvancedSetting Syslog.global.logDir

Description/Title: Configure the ESX host login banner for the DCUI and Host Client.
Discussion: The "Annotations.WelcomeMessage" advanced setting holds the text shown on the ESX Direct Console User Interface (DCUI) and Host Client login screens. The banner is a pre-authentication legal notice that communicates terms of use and any monitoring policy in effect; it does not enforce or block anything technically. Many organizations have specific legal text that must appear on management interfaces.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Setting the banner replaces the default DCUI welcome screen, which normally displays the host's IP address and the F2/F12 key hints. Operators relying on those hints will need a documented alternative such as a runbook. The banner has no impact on workloads or remote management.
Baseline Suggested Value:
Consult your organization's legal advisors for text that is applicable to your environment.

An example is: "Authorized users only. Actual or attempted unauthorized use of this system is prohibited and may result in criminal, civil, security, or administrative proceedings and/or penalties. Use of this information system indicates consent to monitoring and recording, without notice or permission. Users have no expectation of privacy in any use of this system. Any information stored on, or transiting this system, or obtained by monitoring and/or recording, may be disclosed to law enforcement and/or used in accordance with Federal law, State statute, and organization policy. If you are not an authorized user of this system, exit the system at this time."
PowerCLI Command Remediation: Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-AdvancedSetting Annotations.WelcomeMessage | Set-AdvancedSetting -Value "Authorized users only. Actual or attempted unauthorized use of this system is prohibited and may result in criminal, civil, security, or administrative proceedings and/or penalties. Use of this information system indicates consent to monitoring and recording, without notice or permission. Users have no expectation of privacy in any use of this system. Any information stored on, or transiting this system, or obtained by monitoring and/or recording, may be disclosed to law enforcement and/or used in accordance with Federal law, State statute, and organization policy. If you are not an authorized user of this system, exit the system at this time."

Description/Title: The ESX host must enable volatile key destruction.
Discussion:
By default, ESX zeroes out pages allocated for virtual machines (VMs), userspace applications, and kernel threads at the time of allocation. This means no non-zero pages are exposed to VMs or userspace applications, preventing the exposure of cryptographic keys from VMs or userworlds to other clients.

However, if memory is not reused, these keys can remain present in host memory for an extended period. To address this, MemEagerZero can be configured to enforce the zeroing out of userworld and guest memory pages when a userworld process or guest exits. For kernel threads, memory spaces holding keys are zeroed out as soon as the secret is no longer required.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Enabling volatile key destruction adds shutdown time for virtual machines and userworld processes, proportional to the memory they had allocated. Leaving the setting disabled means that pages freed by a VM or process retain their contents until the host reuses them, which can leave cryptographic keys readable in host memory for an extended period after the workload that generated them has exited.

Description/Title: The ESX host should encrypt data written to tiered memory devices.
Discussion: Memory tiering lets ESX extend host memory by using NVMe flash as a slower tier behind DRAM; VM memory pages are transparently moved between tiers based on access patterns. Pages tiered to NVMe are written to persistent storage on the flash device, where they can survive a host reboot or remain readable if the NVMe device is removed. Setting "Mem.EncryptTierNvme" to 1 encrypts those tiered pages at rest. The encryption uses an ephemeral key that is never stored, and the feature is compatible with all other VCF operations including vMotion. Flash memory is difficult to sanitize reliably at end of life, so either encrypt the tiered memory data or plan to securely sanitize or destroy the tiering device when it is decommissioned.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Encrypting tiered memory has a CPU and potential storage-latency cost on hosts using memory tiering; the actual impact depends on the underlying hardware and the workload's tiering pressure. Leaving tiered memory unencrypted means VM data written to the NVMe flash device is readable in cleartext if the device is removed, repurposed, or lost without being thoroughly sanitized.

Description/Title: Enable the Bridge Protocol Data Unit (BPDU) filter on the ESX host.
Discussion:
BPDUs (Bridge Protocol Data Units) are used to transmit Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) information and detect network loops. BPDU Guard and Portfast are commonly enabled on the physical switch directly connected to the ESX host to reduce spanning tree convergence delay.

However, if a BPDU packet is sent from a virtual machine on the ESX host to the configured physical switch, it can result in a cascading lockout of all uplink interfaces from the ESX host. To prevent this type of lockout, BPDU Filter can be enabled on the ESX host to drop any BPDU packets being sent to the physical switch.

Standard and Distributed Virtual Switches do not support STP and do not generate BPDUs themselves; the filter is specifically for BPDU packets originating from guest VM traffic.
