Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Some network-oriented workloads can legitimately generate BPDU packets. The administrator should verify that there are no legitimate BPDU packets generated by virtual machines on the ESX host prior to enabling BPDU Filter. If BPDU Filter is enabled in this situation, enabling Reject Forged Transmits on the virtual switch port group adds protection against Spanning Tree loops.

Description/Title: Use of the dvFilter network APIs must be restricted on the ESX host.
Discussion:
The dvFilter network API lets a virtual appliance (such as VMware NSX or certain third-party security products) attach to a VM's vNIC and inspect or filter its traffic at the host. ESX binds dvFilter agents to the IP address configured in "Net.DVFilterBindIpAddress"; with the default empty value, no dvFilter binding is active.

If you are not using a product that requires dvFilter, leave the binding address empty. Setting an address without an authorized product attached to it creates an entry point that an attacker who can reach that address may use to connect their own filter agent and access traffic destined for VMs on the host. If you are using a product that relies on dvFilter, verify that the binding address matches the product's expected configuration.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Leaving the dvFilter binding address empty has no operational impact on workloads or other ESX features. Setting a binding address when no authorized product is deployed leaves an unguarded entry point that an attacker reaching that address could use to attach a malicious filter agent and intercept VM traffic.

Description/Title: The ESX host must perform reverse path forwarding checks on promiscuous mode port groups.
Discussion:
A port group in promiscuous mode delivers copies of all traffic on that port group to the virtual machines attached to it, rather than only the frames addressed to each machine. Promiscuous mode is used deliberately by traffic-monitoring and intrusion-detection appliances and by nested virtualization, but it widens what a connected workload can observe, so it is normally left disabled and enabled only where a specific function requires it. When the host uses NIC teaming (more than one physical uplink for the same network), a promiscuous port can receive several copies of each broadcast or multicast frame, one arriving over each uplink.

Net.ReversePathFwdCheckPromisc makes the host apply a reverse path forwarding check before delivering such frames to a promiscuous port: a frame is accepted only on the uplink that is the expected path back toward its source, and copies arriving over the other uplinks are dropped. Reverse path forwarding is the same anti-spoofing principle routers use, where traffic is accepted only on the interface by which the source would be reached. The result is that a promiscuous consumer sees one accurate copy of each frame instead of duplicates.

The setting ships enabled and applies only to port groups where promiscuous mode is actually in use, so it is a no-op in environments that reject promiscuous mode (see esx-9.network-standard-reject-promiscuous-mode). Confirm it remains enabled so that environments which do rely on promiscuous mode keep duplicate-frame suppression in place.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling this check causes promiscuous-mode port groups on teamed hosts to receive duplicate broadcast and multicast frames, which adds processing load on monitoring or intrusion-detection appliances and can distort the traffic view they depend on. Where promiscuous mode is not used, the setting has no effect in either state.

Description/Title: Reject forged transmits on standard switches and port groups.
Discussion: A VM's virtual NIC has an assigned MAC address. The guest operating system can send frames with a different source MAC address (a "forged transmit"), which lets the VM impersonate another network device. The "Forged transmits" option on a vSwitch port group controls this behavior: "Accept" allows forged transmits, while "Reject" makes ESX compare each outbound frame's source MAC to the VM's assigned MAC and drop any frame where they do not match. Rejecting forged transmits prevents a compromised VM from impersonating another host on the same Layer 2 segment.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Some workloads, such as clustered applications and network devices or functions, rely on forged transmits as a normal part of their operation. If those workloads are present, create a separate port group that allows forged transmits and connect only the authorized VMs to it.
PowerCLI Command Assessment:
Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-VirtualSwitch -Standard | Get-SecurityPolicy | Select-Object VirtualSwitch,ForgedTransmits
Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-VirtualPortGroup -Standard | Get-SecurityPolicy | Select-Object VirtualPortGroup,ForgedTransmits
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-VirtualSwitch -Standard | Get-SecurityPolicy | Set-SecurityPolicy -ForgedTransmits $false
Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-VirtualPortGroup -Standard | Get-SecurityPolicy | Set-SecurityPolicy -ForgedTransmitsInherited $true

Description/Title: Reject guest MAC address changes on standard switches and port groups.
Discussion: A VM's virtual NIC has both an assigned MAC address (set in VM configuration) and an effective MAC address that the guest operating system is using. A guest can change its effective MAC through OS-level commands. The "MAC address changes" option on a vSwitch port group controls inbound frame handling after such a change: "Accept" delivers frames addressed to the new effective MAC, while "Reject" drops inbound frames addressed to any MAC other than the VM's assigned one. Rejecting MAC changes complements rejecting forged transmits; together they prevent a compromised VM from impersonating another host on the Layer 2 segment in either direction.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Some workloads, such as clustered applications, network devices or functions, applications licensed by MAC address, and vCenter Reduced Downtime Upgrade, rely on MAC changes as a normal part of their operation. If those workloads are present, create a separate port group that allows MAC changes and connect only the authorized VMs to it.
PowerCLI Command Assessment:
Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-VirtualSwitch -Standard | Get-SecurityPolicy | Select-Object VirtualSwitch,MacChanges
Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-VirtualPortGroup -Standard | Get-SecurityPolicy | Select-Object VirtualPortGroup,MacChanges
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-VirtualSwitch -Standard | Get-SecurityPolicy | Set-SecurityPolicy -MacChanges $false
Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-VirtualPortGroup -Standard | Get-SecurityPolicy | Set-SecurityPolicy -MacChangesInherited $true

Description/Title: Reject promiscuous mode on standard switches and port groups.
Discussion: When promiscuous mode is enabled for a port group, all virtual machines connected to that port group can read all packets transmitted across that port group, regardless of the intended recipient. The "Promiscuous mode" option controls this: "Reject" limits each VM to receiving only frames addressed to its MAC, while "Accept" enables the all-traffic behavior. Legitimate use cases for promiscuous mode (such as packet capture tools, intrusion detection sensors, or DHCP server roles) exist but should be confined to specific port groups carrying only the workloads that need it.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Certain workloads and types of work, such as DHCP servers, network devices, and security monitoring, rely on promiscuous mode as a regular part of their operation. If those workloads are present, create a separate port group that allows promiscuous mode and connect only the authorized VMs to it.
PowerCLI Command Assessment:
Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-VirtualSwitch -Standard | Get-SecurityPolicy | Select-Object VirtualSwitch,AllowPromiscuous
Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-VirtualPortGroup -Standard | Get-SecurityPolicy | Select-Object VirtualPortGroup,AllowPromiscuous
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-VirtualSwitch -Standard | Get-SecurityPolicy | Set-SecurityPolicy -AllowPromiscuous $false
Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-VirtualPortGroup -Standard | Get-SecurityPolicy | Set-SecurityPolicy -AllowPromiscuousInherited $true

Description/Title: The ESX host must restrict the use of Virtual Guest Tagging (VGT) on Standard Switches.
Discussion:
When a port group is set to VLAN 4095, the vSwitch passes all network frames to the attached virtual machines (VMs) without modifying the VLAN tags. In vSphere, this is referred to as VGT. The VM must process the VLAN information itself via an 802.1Q driver in the operating system.

VLAN 4095 must only be implemented if the attached VMs have been specifically authorized and are capable of managing VLAN tags themselves. If VLAN 4095 is enabled inappropriately, it may cause denial of service or allow a VM to interact with traffic on an unauthorized VLAN.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Keeping port groups off VLAN 4095 has no operational impact and is the typical setup. A port group set to VLAN 4095 passes all VLAN-tagged traffic to the attached VMs unfiltered; if attached to VMs that are not specifically prepared to handle 802.1Q traffic, this can cause unexpected behavior or denial of service. A VM on a VLAN 4095 port group can also see and send traffic to any VLAN the underlying vSwitch trunk carries, bypassing per-port-group VLAN access controls.

Description/Title: Protect NFS datastore traffic on the ESX host using encryption or network isolation.
Discussion:
NFS traffic between an ESX host and its datastore travels in cleartext by default. Two mechanisms can protect that traffic, and the SCG recommendation is to use at least one of them.

Network isolation: ESX provides controls to assign which vmkernel interface is used for NFS traffic. Isolating NFS traffic onto a dedicated, physically or logically separated network keeps it off any shared fabric where an attacker could observe or modify it.

Kerberos encryption: ESX supports the krb5, krb5i, and krb5p NFS mount options on hosts joined to an Active Directory domain. These mount options instruct NFS to use Kerberos for authentication, data integrity, and privacy. Kerberos NFS protects the traffic on any fabric but has material costs: CPU and I/O on hosts and storage array, lower throughput and higher latency (the actual impact varies with workload, I/O pattern, and hardware). Joining ESX to Active Directory also exposes the host to attack vectors through AD, blurs the lines between storage and virtualization systems in ways that increase the surface for lateral movement, and adds a dependency on AD that complicates restarts and can form dependency loops if AD domain controllers are hosted inside the virtual environment itself.

Pick the path that fits the environment's threat model and constraints. Network isolation is typically the simpler default; Kerberos NFS is appropriate where fabric isolation is impractical and the AD-join surface is acceptable.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V:
Network isolation: requires planning the network topology to give NFS its own vmkernel and physical or logical segment. No CPU or storage performance cost; relies on the isolation being maintained.

Kerberos NFS encryption: consumes CPU and I/O on hosts and storage array and can lower NFS throughput; exact impact varies with workload and hardware. Requires Active Directory connectivity at NFS mount time, so AD outages can disrupt datastore mounting.
Baseline Suggested Value: Kerberos NFS mount options configured, or NFS traffic isolated on a dedicated vmkernel network.

Description/Title: Enforce password complexity on ESX local accounts.
Discussion:
Password complexity rules govern what local user passwords must satisfy before ESX accepts them. In ESX 9.1 the policy is set by the Security.PasswordQualityControlPwquality advanced setting, a raw pam_pwquality option string that takes effect in place of the older Security.PasswordQualityControl unless that older setting is left at the value UNUSED. In the string, minlen sets the minimum length; the credit options dcredit, ucredit, lcredit, and ocredit set to -1 each require at least one numeric, uppercase, lowercase, and special character; difok sets how many characters must differ from the previous password; and enforce_for_root applies the policy to the root account.

Length is the dominant factor in resisting guessing and brute-force attempts, where an attacker systematically tries many candidate passwords. The default already requires one character of each class, so the recommendation keeps those requirements and raises only the minimum length to the SCG standard. This applies to all local ESX users, including root. When the host is joined to an Active Directory domain, password policy for AD users is enforced by AD and is not affected by this setting.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Raising the minimum length applies to newly set passwords; existing passwords keep working until changed. Because the value is a raw pam_pwquality string applied as-is, a malformed entry can block all local password changes, so validate it before saving. Leaving the default permits passwords shorter than the SCG standard.
Installation Default Value: retry=3 dcredit=-1 ucredit=-1 lcredit=-1 ocredit=-1 minlen=7 difok=4 enforce_for_root
Baseline Suggested Value: retry=3 dcredit=-1 ucredit=-1 lcredit=-1 ocredit=-1 minlen=15 difok=4 enforce_for_root
PowerCLI Command Remediation: Get-VMHost -Name $ESX | Get-AdvancedSetting Security.PasswordQualityControlPwquality | Set-AdvancedSetting -Value "retry=3 dcredit=-1 ucredit=-1 lcredit=-1 ocredit=-1 minlen=15 difok=4 enforce_for_root"

Description/Title: The ESX host must be configured with an appropriate maximum password age.
Discussion: Current authentication guidance (NIST SP 800-63B) finds that forced periodic password changes do not improve security when passwords already possess adequate entropy. A very high value is effectively no expiration; the more conventional value of 0 does not disable expiration on ESX. The vSphere Client may require an expiration-alert email address (and a vCenter SMTP server) to be configured before allowing this value to be set via the UI; setting it via PowerCLI bypasses that requirement.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Lowering this value forces users to change ESX local-account passwords on a recurring schedule, which drives users toward predictable patterns and provides little real security gain.

Description/Title: The ESX host must enable Secure Boot enforcement for configuration encryption.
Discussion:
ESX protects its configuration with an encryption key that can be sealed by the host's TPM, so the configuration file is decrypted only when the host boots in a known-good state. The "RequireSecureBoot" setting on the configuration encryption subsystem makes that protection conditional on UEFI Secure Boot also being enabled in the firmware: when set to true, ESX refuses to boot if Secure Boot is not active.

This setting helps mitigate an offline-attack vector. Without it, an attacker with physical access could transfer the ESX install drive to a host where Secure Boot is not enabled and boot the system normally, bypassing the signature checks. With "RequireSecureBoot" enabled, ESX will not boot on such a host without firmware Secure Boot active.

The setting cannot be applied until Secure Boot is enabled in the server's firmware (see esx-9.hardware-secureboot). A reboot is required for the change to take effect.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With "RequireSecureBoot" set to true, ESX will refuse to boot if Secure Boot has been disabled or invalidated in firmware, which can complicate hardware replacement scenarios where the replacement server may default to Secure Boot off. Maintenance that clears or replaces the host's TPM (such as motherboard replacement or some firmware updates) can also prevent the host from booting until the TPM recovery key is used; back up that recovery key before relying on this setting. Setting "RequireSecureBoot" to false leaves the configuration encryption mechanism reliant on Secure Boot being present without enforcing that requirement; an attacker who can boot the host's storage on a Secure-Boot-disabled system bypasses the protection.

Description/Title: The ESX Host Client must enforce an idle session timeout.
Discussion: The "UserVars.HostClientSessionTimeout" setting controls how long an idle session in the ESX Host Client (the host-served UI accessed directly outside of vCenter) stays authenticated before being terminated. The Host Client is normally used only for break-glass administration when vCenter is unreachable; the typical user is highly privileged, so a session left open at a workstation can grant a passerby direct ESX administrative access. The default idle timeout limits that exposure window.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Keeping the default idle timeout has no operational impact on ESX or its workloads. Lowering the value forces Host Client users to re-authenticate more often, which can interrupt break-glass work during incident response. Setting the value to 0 disables the idle timeout, leaving Host Client sessions open indefinitely after the operator walks away from the console.

Description/Title: Configure the inactivity timeout to automatically terminate idle ESX host shells.
Discussion: The "UserVars.ESXiShellInteractiveTimeOut" setting controls how long an interactive ESX shell session (local at the DCUI or remote via SSH) stays active when no input is received. By default, idle shell sessions are not terminated and remain authenticated indefinitely. Set the timeout to a non-zero value so that a shell session forgotten at a workstation or DCUI does not provide an open path to root-level access on the host.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Setting an idle timeout has no operational impact on ordinary shell use. Lowering the value too aggressively can cause active troubleshooting sessions to terminate during long-running diagnostic commands, requiring re-authentication. Leaving the default in place means idle shell sessions remain authenticated indefinitely, leaving an open path to root-level access on the host if a session is left unattended.

Description/Title: Set a timeout to limit how long the ESX Shell and SSH services are allowed to run.
Discussion: The "UserVars.ESXiShellTimeOut" setting controls how long the ESX Shell and SSH services remain running once they have been started. After this number of seconds elapses with no user logged in to the shell, the service is stopped automatically. Setting a non-zero value limits the window during which a forgotten or unmonitored shell service is reachable on the host.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Setting an auto-stop timeout has no operational impact on planned shell or SSH use; the service can be re-enabled when needed. Lowering the value can cause the service to stop while an operator is still preparing to log in (for example, while opening a remote console), forcing them to re-enable it. Leaving the default in place keeps the service running indefinitely once started, expanding the time window during which it can be reached on the network.

Description/Title: The ESX host must not suppress warnings that the local or remote shell is enabled.
Discussion:
When the local ESX Shell or the SSH service is enabled on a host, the vSphere Client and Host Client display a persistent warning banner on the host summary view. The banner is an operator-visible signal that an interactive shell path into the hypervisor is currently available, which is intended to be a short-lived state used only for troubleshooting.

Setting UserVars.SuppressShellWarning to 1 hides that banner without changing whether the shell is actually enabled. The shell remains reachable; only the indicator is gone. Operators who would otherwise notice that a maintenance shell was left on after a support call lose that visual cue, and the host can sit with an interactive shell available for far longer than intended. Leave the value at 0 so the warning surfaces whenever a shell path is open. Deactivating the ESX Shell and SSH themselves is covered separately.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Leaving the value at the default has no operational cost; the warning is informational and only appears when the ESX Shell or SSH is actually enabled. Setting the value to 1 hides the banner, so a shell left enabled after troubleshooting is no longer visible at a glance in the vSphere Client, extending the window before an operator notices and disables it.

Description/Title: The SNMP service on the ESX host must not be configured to start automatically.
Discussion:
ESX includes a built-in SNMP agent (snmpd) that can expose host inventory, configuration, and performance data to a monitoring system. SNMP versions 1 and 2c transmit the community string and all response data in cleartext, so anyone who can reach UDP/161 on a host can read the data and learn the community string by passive capture. SNMPv3 adds user-based authentication and message encryption and is the only version acceptable for an SNMP path that crosses any untrusted network.

The shipped service policy starts the agent automatically with the host, which means the SNMP service is poised to run even on hosts that have no monitoring configured. Setting the service policy to start manually keeps the agent off by default; an operator who needs SNMP must start it deliberately and can configure SNMPv3 at the same time. If SNMP is actively in use, configure SNMPv3 with authentication and stop using v1 and v2c community strings; the v3-required pattern is enforced separately on vCenter through its own SCG entry.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Setting the service policy to manual prevents the SNMP agent from starting at boot; an operator who deploys SNMP-based monitoring on a host must start the service explicitly and configure it for SNMPv3 with authentication. Leaving the policy at the default keeps the agent positioned to run automatically, so a misconfiguration that enables the agent without specifying SNMPv3 can expose host information to anyone reachable on UDP/161.
Installation Default Value: Stopped, Start and stop with host
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
Get-VMHostService -VMHost $ESX | Where-Object {$_.Key -eq 'snmpd'} | Set-VMHostService -Policy Off
Get-VMHostService -VMHost $ESX | Where-Object {$_.Key -eq 'snmpd'} | Stop-VMHostService

Description/Title: The ESX host's SSH service must be deactivated except for temporary diagnostic use.
Discussion:
The ESX host runs an SSH daemon that gives administrators an interactive command-line session into the hypervisor. Anyone who reaches the SSH listener with a valid credential gets a shell that is effectively root on the host, with full visibility into stored secrets, running workloads, and the host's storage. SSH is intended as a break-glass tool for diagnostics and recovery when the vSphere Client, Host Client, or esxcli paths are not enough; it is not the routine management interface.

The SSH service should remain stopped, and its service policy should be set to manual start so it does not come up automatically with the host. An operator who needs SSH for a specific task starts the service for the duration of that task and stops it again afterward. When SSH is intentionally running, the daemon's own configuration hardening is enforced separately by the esx-9.ssh-configuration row, and idle sessions are bounded by the shell timeout rows.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Keeping the service stopped with a manual-start policy has no day-to-day operational cost; routine management uses the vSphere Client, Host Client, and the vCenter API rather than SSH. Setting the service policy to start automatically leaves an interactive root-equivalent shell listening on every host at all times, which is the surface the recommendation removes.
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
Get-VMHostService -VMHost $ESX | Where-Object {$_.Key -eq 'TSM-SSH'} | Set-VMHostService -Policy Off
Get-VMHostService -VMHost $ESX | Where-Object {$_.Key -eq 'TSM-SSH'} | Stop-VMHostService

Description/Title: The ESX host must run a build of ESX that has not reached End of General Support.
Discussion:
Broadcom publishes a lifecycle policy for each ESX release that defines the date at which the release reaches End of General Support. After that date, the release no longer receives routine security patches, bug fixes, or technical support. A host left on an unsupported build accumulates exposure to every vulnerability discovered after the cutoff, since fixes are not back-ported to releases that are out of support.

Operators should track the published lifecycle dates for the ESX release in use and plan upgrades so the cluster moves to a supported build before its current release reaches End of General Support. Upgrade paths from an out-of-support build are more complex than upgrade paths from a supported one, so deferring an upgrade past the cutoff increases both the cost of the eventual upgrade and the security exposure during the gap.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: A host left on an out-of-support build accumulates exposure to vulnerabilities discovered after End of General Support, since fixes are not back-ported. The eventual upgrade is also more complex than from a supported build, because skip-version paths and required intermediate steps grow over time.
Setting Location: Compare ESX build numbers to the lifecycle policy published on the Broadcom Support Portal.

Description/Title: The ESX host must synchronize its clock with organizational reference sources.
Discussion:
Accurate time on an ESX host underpins certificate validity checks, audit log ordering, cluster operations, Kerberos and other time-bounded authentication protocols, and incident response timelines that rely on event correlation across systems. A host whose clock has drifted outside a certificate validity window fails TLS handshakes; one outside the Kerberos clock-skew tolerance fails Active Directory authentication; logs from drifted hosts cannot be reliably aligned with logs from other systems during a forensic investigation.

This control covers both halves of NTP on the ESX host: an authoritative server list configured against organizational time sources, and the NTP daemon running and set to start with the host so synchronization continues across reboots without operator intervention. A configured server list with the daemon stopped produces no sync; a running daemon with no server list has nowhere to sync against. Other VCF components carry their own per-component time controls because each has a separate NTP configuration surface.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: A host with an inaccurate or unsynchronized clock fails any operation that depends on time: TLS handshakes against certificates outside the validity window, Active Directory authentication that requires Kerberos clock-skew tolerance, and cluster operations (vMotion, HA, vSAN coordination) that assume consistent timestamps across hosts. Audit logs from such a host cannot be aligned with logs from other systems, which degrades incident response and forensic investigation.
Setting Location: UI: Host > Configure > System > Time Configuration
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
# Replace the example servers with your organization's authoritative NTP sources
$ntpServers = @("ntp1.example.com","ntp2.example.com","ntp3.example.com","ntp4.example.com")
Add-VmHostNtpServer -VMHost $ESX -NtpServer $ntpServers -Confirm:$false
Get-VMHostService -VMHost $ESX | Where-Object {$_.Key -eq "ntpd"} | Set-VMHostService -Policy "on" -Confirm:$false
Get-VMHostService -VMHost $ESX | Where-Object {$_.Key -eq "ntpd"} | Restart-VMHostService -Confirm:$false

Description/Title: The ESX host's TLS profile must restrict negotiated ciphers and protocols to a modern, approved set.
Discussion:
ESX uses TLS to protect management connections to and from the host, including connections from vCenter, the vSphere Client, the Host Client, esxcli, and API consumers. A TLS profile on the ESX host is a named bundle of allowed protocol versions, cipher suites, key exchange groups, and signature algorithms; when a client connects, the host negotiates only what the active profile permits. The installed default profile preserves backwards compatibility with older TLS clients and so accepts legacy cipher suites and TLS versions with known weaknesses.

The recommended profile narrows the negotiable set to TLS 1.3 only with modern cipher suites, which removes the legacy options an attacker on the management network could otherwise downgrade to. Switching profiles requires a host reboot, and the vSphere Client marks the pending change with a '(Reboot Required)' suffix until the host is restarted. Confirm that connecting clients (VCF management components, monitoring agents, automation tooling) can negotiate TLS 1.3 before rolling the change cluster-wide; a less strict profile that allows TLS 1.2 with modern cipher suites is available if the client population cannot fully support TLS 1.3.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: A strict TLS profile drops legacy cipher suites and TLS versions that older clients may still depend on; any client unable to negotiate TLS 1.3 with the profile's cipher set loses connectivity to the host until either the client is updated or the profile is widened. The profile change also requires a host reboot to take effect, so applying it under vSphere Configuration Profiles drives a rolling cluster reboot.

Description/Title: The ESX host must require TPM-based configuration encryption.
Discussion:
An ESX host's configuration consists of files for each service that runs on the host, primarily in the /etc/ tree but also in other namespaces. A cron job backs up these files periodically, on graceful shutdown, and on demand, producing an archived configuration file in the boot bank that the host reads on the next reboot to restore its state.

When a Trusted Platform Module (TPM) is present on the host, ESX seals the archived configuration to that TPM. The sealed archive can only be read on this specific host with this specific TPM; an attacker who pulls the host's disk and reads it on different hardware (an offline attack) sees encrypted content with no usable key. Without TPM-based encryption, the archived configuration on disk includes secrets and policy settings in a form that can be read or modified out-of-band.

Configuration encryption uses the physical TPM when it is available and supported at install or upgrade time. If a TPM is added or enabled after the fact, the ESX host must be explicitly reconfigured to use it. Once TPM-based configuration encryption is enabled, it cannot be deactivated; on hardware without a TPM, the host's encryption mode falls back to NONE and the on-disk archive is left unprotected.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With TPM-based encryption deactivated (mode NONE), the archived ESX configuration on disk includes secrets and policy settings in a form readable by anyone who reads the disk outside the running host. With TPM-based encryption enabled (the recommendation), traditional root-password recovery techniques no longer work, and a host whose TPM is cleared or replaced without a backed-up recovery key cannot boot and must be rebuilt; back up the TPM recovery key out-of-band before relying on this control.

Description/Title: The ESX host configuration encryption recovery key must be backed up to a secure off-host location.
Discussion:
ESX configuration encryption protects the host's sensitive configuration state, including stored secrets and keys, by encrypting it with a key sealed to the host's Trusted Platform Module (TPM). A TPM is a hardware security chip that releases the sealing key only when the host boots into the same measured state it recorded when encryption was set up. This binds the configuration to a known-good boot, but it also means that if the TPM can no longer release the key, the host cannot decrypt its own configuration.

When configuration encryption is enabled, ESX generates a recovery key: a fallback that can decrypt the configuration if the TPM stops releasing the sealing key. That happens when the TPM is cleared, the system board or TPM is replaced, or a firmware update changes the boot measurements the TPM checks. Without the recovery key, such an event leaves the host unable to boot, and it must be rebuilt. The product generates the recovery key but does not store a copy off the host, so an operator has to retrieve it and keep it safe.

Back up each host's recovery key and recovery ID to a secured, off-host location such as an encrypted secret store, and confirm the stored copy is complete and retrievable. The recovery key is itself sensitive: anyone who holds it can decrypt that host's configuration, so it must not be left in a plaintext file or on storage the host itself can reach.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Capturing and backing up the recovery key does not change host runtime behavior. If the key is not backed up, a TPM clear, a system board or TPM replacement, or a firmware update that changes boot measurements will leave the host unable to decrypt its configuration and unable to boot, forcing a rebuild. Because the recovery key can decrypt the host configuration, storing it insecurely exposes that configuration to anyone who obtains the key.
Configuration Parameter: Recovery Key (esxcli system settings encryption recovery list)

Description/Title: The ESX host must enforce execInstalledOnly.
Discussion:
The companion control esx-9.vib-trusted-binaries sets the VMkernel boot option execInstalledOnly, which restricts the kernel to executing only binaries that arrived through a signed vSphere Installation Bundle (VIB). On its own, that setting is an advanced configuration value, which means an attacker with physical disk access could read the host's boot configuration offline, set execInstalledOnly to FALSE, and put the modified configuration back so the host boots with the protection disabled.

When this control is enabled, TPM-based configuration encryption seals the execInstalledOnly value into the TPM-attested boot policy. The host's boot process then refuses to proceed unless the sealed value matches the running value, which removes the offline-tampering path that an attacker would otherwise use to disable execInstalledOnly between reboots. This control depends on Secure Boot (esx-9.secureboot-enforcement), TPM-based configuration encryption (esx-9.tpm-configuration), and execInstalledOnly itself being enabled (esx-9.vib-trusted-binaries); on hardware without a TPM, this flag has no effect.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With this flag deactivated, execInstalledOnly remains enforced while the host is running but its value is no longer sealed to the TPM-attested boot policy. An attacker with physical disk access could disable execInstalledOnly in the host's boot configuration offline; the host would then boot with the protection turned off, and the only signal of tampering would be the absence of the expected setting at runtime.

Description/Title: The ESX host must restrict inter-VM transparent page sharing to VMs configured with sched.mem.pshare.salt.
Discussion:
Transparent Page Sharing (TPS) is the ESX memory-deduplication mechanism that maps identical-content guest memory pages to a single physical page, reducing the host's overall memory footprint. With unrestricted inter-VM TPS, pages from different VMs could be deduplicated together, which exposes a side-channel: a guest in one VM can use timing measurements against the processor's cache to detect specific page contents in a neighboring VM, and under tightly controlled laboratory conditions this technique has been used to extract AES keys from a co-resident workload.

The Mem.ShareForceSalting setting controls how the dedup hash is computed. At the recommended value, TPS combines a per-VM salt into the hash so identical pages from different VMs produce different hashes and are not deduplicated together; pages within the same VM still dedupe normally. A VM that has the sched.mem.pshare.salt advanced setting configured can opt back into cross-VM sharing with explicitly named peers, which is useful for tightly trusted workload groups but should be reserved for workloads where the operator has decided the trade-off is worthwhile.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: At the recommended value, inter-VM page deduplication is disabled, so identical-content 4 KB pages held by different VMs each consume their own physical memory. The practical efficiency loss is small because TPS only operates on 4 KB small pages and modern guests run most of their working set on 2 MB large pages that TPS cannot deduplicate at all; even on memory-overcommitted hosts the recoverable memory is bounded by the small-page portion of guest memory. Lowering the value back toward the legacy inter-VM-sharing default reintroduces the side-channel surface described in the Discussion.
Setting Location: UI: Host > Configure > System > Advanced System Settings

Description/Title: The ESX host must have all available software updates and patches installed.
Discussion:
Broadcom publishes regular patch releases for each ESX major version that address security vulnerabilities and bug fixes discovered after the original release date. A host running back-revision patches accumulates known vulnerabilities, including any that have been published in Broadcom security advisories or otherwise disclosed since the host's current build.

When applying updates, update vCenter before the ESX hosts: the management layer must be on a build that supports the new ESX build for cluster operations to function during the rolling host upgrade. Patch releases address vulnerabilities and bug fixes within the current major version; Update releases add or change functionality, and both classes carry security fixes.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: A host left back-level on patches is exposed to any vulnerability published or disclosed since its current build. The eventual catch-up upgrade is also larger and more involved, since multiple Update releases may need to be applied in sequence to reach the current build, each with its own pre-check, evacuation, and reboot cycle.
Setting Location: Compare ESX build numbers to the latest available patch on the Broadcom Support Portal.

Description/Title: The ESX host image profile acceptance level must be PartnerSupported or higher.
Discussion:
The acceptance level on the ESX host's image profile determines which VIBs (vSphere Installation Bundles) the host accepts at install time. Four levels exist, in descending order of vetting: VMwareCertified VIBs are created, tested, and signed by VMware; VMwareAccepted VIBs are partner-created but tested and signed by VMware; PartnerSupported VIBs are created, tested, and signed by a certified VMware partner; and CommunitySupported VIBs have not been tested by VMware or a partner and carry no digital signature.

The recommended level accepts the first three but refuses CommunitySupported VIBs. Refusing unsigned VIBs is the install-time complement to the run-time signed-binary enforcement in esx-9.vib-trusted-binaries (which sets execInstalledOnly): if an unsigned VIB cannot be installed in the first place, none of its binaries can later be presented to the kernel as 'installed from a VIB.' A host left at the CommunitySupported level can install code with no signature provenance and no compatibility testing, which raises the risk that a malicious or buggy VIB causes a host outage or compromise.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With the host's acceptance level at PartnerSupported (the recommendation), CommunitySupported VIBs are refused, which prevents installation of unsigned or untested community packages. Lowering the level to CommunitySupported allows those packages to install, which can lead to a host outage from an untested driver or to a host compromise from a maliciously crafted VIB.
Setting Location: UI: Host > Configure > Security Profile; ESX Shell

Description/Title: The ESX host must only execute binaries delivered through a signed VIB.
Discussion:
The VMkernel.Boot.execInstalledOnly setting tells the ESX kernel to refuse execution of any binary that did not arrive on the host through a vSphere Installation Bundle (VIB) recognized by the host's acceptance level. When the setting is TRUE, an attacker who manages to drop a binary onto the host filesystem (through a misconfigured share, a compromised management agent, or any other path that lands a file in a writable location) cannot run it; the kernel refuses to load the image because it has no VIB provenance.

This control is the run-time complement to esx-9.vib-acceptance-level-supported, which decides at install time which VIBs the host will accept, and is also the setting that esx-9.tpm-trusted-binaries seals into the TPM-attested boot policy so execInstalledOnly cannot be silently turned off between reboots. The three controls work together: only signed VIBs install, only VIB-delivered binaries execute, and the execInstalledOnly setting itself cannot be tampered with offline.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: At the recommended value, the kernel refuses to execute any binary that did not arrive through a VIB; third-party tooling delivered outside the VIB framework (custom monitoring agents, ad-hoc diagnostic scripts that compile a binary on the host) cannot run until it is packaged in a properly signed VIB. With the setting at FALSE, any executable that reaches the host's filesystem can run, including a binary an attacker drops through a misconfigured share or a compromised management agent.
Setting Location: UI: Host > Configure > System > Advanced System Settings

Description/Title: The ESX host must isolate management communications.
Discussion:
A VMkernel (vmk) interface is the ESX host's own network endpoint, used for hypervisor-originated traffic such as management, vMotion, vSAN, IP-storage, and replication. Each vmk has a set of feature flags that determine which categories of traffic it carries; the management flag advertises the interface as a destination for vCenter and other management-plane connections. When the management flag is enabled on a vmk that lives on a non-management network (a vMotion-only segment, a storage segment, a workload segment), management connections become reachable from that network, which broadens the attack surface to whatever else is on the wire.

The management vmk should live on a network segment dedicated to virtualization management, separated from workload VMs and unrelated systems, with perimeter controls that allow only authorized vSphere administrators from authorized workstations to reach the management interfaces. The non-management vmks should each carry only the feature flag they need (vMotion-only, vSAN-only, and so on) so that an intruder on a non-management segment cannot directly initiate a management session against the host. Where vCenter sits relative to ESX management is a related design choice; documenting that choice and applying consistent perimeter controls matter more than which option is picked.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With management traffic confined to the dedicated management vmk on the management segment, only hosts on that segment can initiate a management session against the ESX host. Enabling the management flag on a non-management vmk exposes the host's management plane to whatever sits on that other segment (workload VMs, storage clients, vMotion source/target hosts), and any compromise on those networks gains direct access to the management interface.
Setting Location: UI: Host > Configure > Networking > VMkernel adapters

Description/Title: The ESX host must protect the confidentiality and integrity of transmitted storage traffic.
Discussion:
IP-based storage protocols (vSAN, iSCSI, NFS) carry datastore traffic over the host's TCP/IP stack, which means any workload sharing the same VLAN can in principle observe the traffic. The first protection is isolation: each storage VMkernel interface should be on a VLAN or network segment dedicated to that storage protocol, separated from management, vMotion, and workload networks. The PowerCLI audit in this row inspects each vmk's VsanTrafficEnabled flag; for iSCSI and NFS, which bind to a vmk by IP address rather than a service flag, the equivalent audit is to verify that each storage vmk's IP and VLAN tag place it on the dedicated storage segment.

Where isolation alone is not sufficient or in-transit encryption is required, vSAN provides built-in data-in-transit encryption for node-to-node traffic and NFS v4.1 supports the krb5p security flavor that encrypts mount traffic on the wire. Enabling either adds CPU overhead at the host, so monitor storage latency and throughput when applying it. For Fibre Channel storage, the equivalent isolation primitives live on the SAN itself: LUN masking on the storage controller restricts which initiator WWPNs can address which LUNs, and SAN zoning on the fabric restricts which initiators can see which target ports.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With each storage vmk isolated to its own segment and carrying only its storage service flag, IP-storage traffic is reachable only by hosts on that segment, and FC storage is reachable only by initiator WWPNs the SAN admin has explicitly authorized. Leaving storage VMkernel adapters on shared VLANs or with extra service flags enabled exposes datastore traffic to whatever else shares the wire (workload VMs, management consoles, vMotion sessions), where it can be captured by anyone with packet-capture access to that segment.
Setting Location: UI: Host > Configure > Networking > VMkernel adapters

Description/Title: The ESX host must protect the confidentiality and integrity of transmitted vMotion traffic.
Discussion:
The vMotion network carries virtual machine memory state during compute vMotion operations. Each VMkernel interface marked for vMotion advertises itself as a destination for migration traffic; if that flag is set on a vmk that lives on a non-vMotion network (a management segment, a workload segment), an attacker on that other network can capture the memory pages of any VM being migrated. The PowerCLI audit in this row inspects each vmk's VMotionEnabled flag, and the vMotion vmks should sit on a VLAN or network segment dedicated to vMotion, separated from management, storage, and workload networks.

vMotion encryption protects the in-transit memory state on the wire when isolation alone is not enough. The per-VM vMotion encryption setting (Disabled, Opportunistic, Required) controls whether each migration uses encryption; setting individual VMs to Required forces encryption regardless of any other configuration. Encryption adds CPU overhead at both source and destination hosts and can lengthen migration duration, so apply it where the threat model warrants it rather than as a blanket default.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With each vMotion vmk isolated to its own segment and the vMotion service flag only on vMotion vmks, migration memory traffic is reachable only by hosts on that segment. Leaving the vMotion flag enabled on a non-vMotion vmk, or co-locating the vMotion network with workload or management traffic, exposes the memory state of migrating VMs (including credentials, keys, and in-memory data) to anyone with packet-capture access to that segment. Encryption makes the captured traffic unintelligible without the migration key, at the cost of CPU overhead and longer migrations.
Setting Location: UI: Host > Configure > Networking > VMkernel adapters

Description/Title: Accelerate vMotion encryption using available hardware offload on ESX hosts.
Discussion:
When vMotion encrypts a migrating VM's in-flight memory, that encryption is AES-GCM. On ESX hosts equipped with an Intel QuickAssist (QAT) accelerator, the AES-GCM work can be offloaded to the accelerator rather than running on host CPU cores, which lowers the CPU cost of encrypted migrations. The Migrate.VMotionEncryptionOffload setting controls whether ESX uses the offload when an accelerator is present; hosts without QAT hardware are unaffected. If the accelerator becomes contended during a migration, ESX falls back to CPU-based encryption automatically and the migration completes.

The reason to keep the default in place is that the offload path uses the accelerator's cryptographic implementation, which matches the FIPS posture of the rest of ESX only when the accelerator runs FIPS-validated firmware. Accelerator firmware is supplied by the hardware vendor, not Broadcom, so its validation status depends on what the vendor ships and what the operator has installed. On hosts where the QAT firmware is not FIPS-validated, disable the offload so vMotion encryption stays on ESX's validated software cryptography.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Leaving the offload enabled is the default behavior on QAT-equipped hosts and reduces host CPU consumption during encrypted vMotion. Disabling it on hosts whose QAT firmware is not FIPS-validated returns vMotion encryption to ESX's software cryptography at the cost of additional host CPU during migrations; hosts without QAT hardware are unaffected either way.

Description/Title: Limit the maximum lifetime of access tokens.
Discussion:
Access tokens are the short-lived credentials issued after authentication to carry a session's API calls. The access token expiry setting caps their maximum time-to-live. Aligning this maximum with the idle session timeout used elsewhere keeps a captured access token from outliving the session it belongs to.

A shorter maximum limits how long a stolen access token can be replayed before it must be refreshed through an authenticated path.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Setting the access token maximum lifetime to match the session timeout keeps tokens from outliving their sessions; existing tokens continue until they expire. A longer maximum leaves a captured token replayable for that window.
Setting Location: UI: Manage > Fleet Management > Identity and Access > IAM Settings

Description/Title: Do not automatically include all users in role assignments.
Discussion:
The ALL_USERS group setting controls whether every authenticated user is automatically included for role assignments. When automatic inclusion is off, a user receives access only through an explicit role assignment; when on, all authenticated users are swept into role assignments collectively, which makes it easy to grant access more broadly than intended.

Keeping automatic inclusion disabled enforces least privilege: access is granted deliberately, per user or group, rather than to everyone at once. This is the default state, so the control verifies it has not been turned on.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With automatic inclusion disabled, users gain access only through explicit role assignments. Enabling it grants role assignments to all authenticated users at once, which can over-grant access well beyond what was intended.
Setting Location: UI: Manage > Fleet Management > Identity and Access > IAM Settings

Description/Title: Limit the maximum lifetime of API tokens.
Discussion:
API tokens authenticate programmatic access to VCF Operations. The API token expiry setting caps how long an issued token remains valid (its maximum time-to-live). A long maximum lifetime means a token captured from a script, a log, a CI system, or a backup stays usable to an attacker for that whole window.

Reducing the maximum token lifetime shrinks that exposure: a stolen token expires sooner and must be reissued through an authenticated path. Set it to the shortest maximum that still meets the operational needs of the integrations that rely on API tokens.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: A shorter maximum API token lifetime means integrations renew their tokens more often; set it short enough to limit exposure but long enough to avoid breaking automated workflows. A long maximum leaves captured tokens usable for an extended period.
Baseline Suggested Value: Reduced to the shortest maximum time-to-live that meets operational needs
Setting Location: UI: Manage > Fleet Management > Identity and Access > IAM Settings

Description/Title: Configure fleet-wide DNS settings.
Discussion:
VCF Operations Fleet Management pushes DNS resolver settings to the managed VCF instances in the fleet. Configure authoritative DNS servers so fleet components resolve names through a known, controlled source.

Name resolution underpins other security-relevant functions: components reach certificate validation endpoints, NTP servers, log-collection targets, and identity providers by name. Pointing the fleet at controlled DNS servers reduces the chance that a component resolves one of those names to an attacker-controlled host. This is foundational configuration rather than a strong standalone control.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With fleet DNS pointed at authoritative servers, components resolve names consistently through a controlled source. Leaving it unset or pointed at an untrusted resolver risks misdirected name resolution for time, certificate, logging, and identity endpoints.
Setting Location: UI: Manage > Fleet Management > Fleet Settings > DNS

Description/Title: Configure an inactivity expiry for Just-In-Time users.
Discussion:
VCF Operations supports Just-In-Time (JIT) users -- accounts provisioned on demand from a federated identity source rather than created and managed locally. The JIT user inactivity period controls how long such an account remains valid after its last use before it is automatically removed. The default leaves JIT users valid indefinitely, so an account provisioned once stays present long after the person stops using it.

Standing accounts that no longer correspond to an active user widen the set of credentials an attacker can target and that an administrator must keep track of. Setting an inactivity expiry retires JIT users automatically once they go unused for the configured period, keeping the active-account set aligned with current need.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With an inactivity expiry set, a JIT user that goes unused for the configured period is removed and the person must be re-provisioned on next access. Leaving it at the default keeps every JIT user ever provisioned valid indefinitely, accumulating stale accounts.
Baseline Suggested Value: An inactivity expiry configured (JIT users removed after a defined period of inactivity)
Setting Location: UI: Manage > Fleet Management > Identity and Access > IAM Settings

Description/Title: Configure a fleet-wide password policy.
Discussion:
VCF Operations Fleet Management lets you define a password policy once and push it to the managed VCF instances in the fleet, so one standard applies across components instead of being set on each individually. Configure the fleet password policy to meet your organization's password standard.

The guidance recommends, as a minimum floor: a length well above the typical product default; at least one uppercase, lowercase, numeric, and special character; lockout after a small number of consecutive failed attempts; no forced periodic rotation (rotate on evidence of compromise rather than on a schedule); and a modest password history to prevent immediate reuse. Length is the dominant factor in resisting guessing and brute-force attempts; the character-class floor adds basic variety without forcing the predictable substitutions that heavy composition rules produce.

The fleet policy applies to instances managed through Fleet Management. Components and local appliance accounts outside the fleet push (for example, ESX, which is not managed at the fleet level by default) still rely on their per-component password controls.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With a fleet password policy assigned, new and changed passwords across managed instances must meet the standard; existing passwords keep working until changed. Without one, each component falls back to its own default policy, which may be weaker and is harder to keep consistent across the fleet.
Baseline Suggested Value: Fleet password policy assigned to meet the organization's standard. SCG recommended minimum: length 15; at least 1 uppercase, 1 lowercase, 1 numeric, and 1 special character; lockout after 5 failed attempts; no forced periodic rotation; password history 5
Setting Location: UI: Manage > Fleet Management > Fleet Settings > Password Policy

Description/Title: Configure fleet-wide time synchronization.
Discussion:
VCF Operations Fleet Management pushes NTP (time synchronization) settings to the managed VCF instances in the fleet, so components share a common, accurate time source instead of being configured one at a time. Configure authoritative internal NTP servers for the fleet.

Accurate, synchronized time is foundational to security operations: log entries from different components can be correlated on a common timeline during an investigation, certificate validity windows are evaluated correctly, and time-sensitive authentication such as Kerberos and token expiry functions as intended. Skew or an untrusted time source undermines all three.

vCenter and ESX keep their own time controls so the guidance still applies to non-VCF deployments; the other fleet-managed components receive NTP from this fleet setting.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With fleet NTP configured to authoritative servers, managed components share an accurate timeline. Leaving it unset lets components drift or fall back to unsynchronized or untrusted time, which breaks cross-component log correlation and can cause certificate and authentication failures.
Setting Location: UI: Manage > Fleet Management > Fleet Settings > NTP

Description/Title: The guest OS must disable automatic addition of optional VMware Tools features.
Discussion:
VMware Tools includes a number of optional in-guest components (script execution agents, monitoring helpers, service-discovery features, container introspection) that can be installed or omitted depending on the guest's needs. The autoupgrade allow-add-feature setting in tools.conf controls whether the periodic VMware Tools auto-upgrade process is allowed to add new optional features to an existing installation.

When the setting is true (the default), an auto-upgrade can re-enable an optional component that the operator had previously removed from the guest's hardening baseline, restoring a surface that was intentionally pared down. Setting it to false means auto-upgrades still patch the currently installed feature set but cannot add anything new; any new feature an operator wants is an explicit install, not a quietly added one.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With auto-feature-addition disabled, VMware Tools auto-upgrades continue to patch and update the features that are already installed in the guest, but new optional features cannot be silently added through that channel. An operator who needs a feature that is not currently installed must add it explicitly through a manual Tools install or a guest-OS package update path, rather than relying on the auto-upgrade process to bring it in.
PowerCLI Command Assessment: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config get autoupgrade allow-add-feature
PowerCLI Command Remediation: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config set autoupgrade allow-add-feature false

Description/Title: The guest OS must disable MSI transforms in VMware Tools auto-upgrades.
Discussion:
MSI transforms (.mst files) are Windows Installer modifiers that change the behavior of an MSI installation at install time: adding or removing features, changing registry keys, redirecting file destinations, or overriding default options. When autoupgrade allow-msi-transforms permits transforms, the VMware Tools auto-upgrade process on a Windows guest will apply any MSI transform present in the upgrade payload.

With the setting at the recommended value, the auto-upgrade installs the base VMware Tools MSI without applying any transform, which removes the path by which a tampered or unauthorized transform could redirect the Tools installation. An operator who has a legitimate need for a transform must apply it explicitly through a manual install, not through the auto-upgrade flow.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With MSI transforms disabled in the auto-upgrade flow, VMware Tools upgrades on Windows guests install the unmodified MSI shipped with the upgrade, and any customization an operator wants must be applied explicitly through a manual install. A guest that re-enables transforms in the auto-upgrade flow accepts whatever transform is staged for that upgrade, which is the path an attacker would target if they gained the ability to modify tools.conf or stage a transform on the host.
PowerCLI Command Assessment: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config get autoupgrade allow-msi-transforms
PowerCLI Command Remediation: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config set autoupgrade allow-msi-transforms false

Description/Title: The guest OS must deactivate Appinfo information gathering unless required.
Discussion:
Appinfo is the VMware Tools module that reports the list of running applications and their versions from a guest OS up to vCenter, where consumers like VCF Operations and other inventory tools can use the data to drive dependency mapping, license tracking, and application-aware reporting. When Appinfo is enabled, the module periodically enumerates running processes inside the guest and emits the inventory to the host via the VMware Tools channel.

In environments that do not consume Appinfo data, the module collects and emits inventory that nothing reads, and keeps a module running in the guest that contributes nothing. Disabling Appinfo stops the in-guest enumeration and narrows the VMware Tools attack surface by one module; the protection is modest in absolute terms (Appinfo enumerates process names and versions, not file contents) but follows the principle of not running code that nothing needs. If a tool that depends on Appinfo (such as VCF Operations application monitoring) is in use, leave the module enabled in the VMs whose data that tool needs.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With Appinfo disabled, the in-guest enumeration stops and any vCenter consumer or downstream tool that depends on per-VM application inventory loses that data for the affected VMs; this includes VCF Operations application monitoring and any third-party tooling that reads the same inventory feed. Leaving Appinfo enabled in a VM where no downstream tool consumes the data costs nothing functionally but keeps a module running and emitting data that is then discarded.
PowerCLI Command Assessment: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config get appinfo disabled
PowerCLI Command Remediation: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config set appinfo disabled true

Description/Title: The guest OS must deactivate ContainerInfo unless required.
Discussion:
ContainerInfo is the VMware Tools plugin that periodically enumerates the containers running inside a Linux guest and reports the inventory up to vCenter through the VMware Tools channel. The containerinfo poll-interval setting controls how often the plugin runs and emits the inventory. The recommended value turns the polling off entirely, which stops the in-guest enumeration and the inventory feed.

In environments that do not consume container inventory at the vCenter level, the plugin polls and emits data that nothing reads, and runs an extra in-guest enumerator on every Linux VM. Disabling the poll narrows the VMware Tools attack surface by one module; the protection is modest in absolute terms (the plugin enumerates container names and images, not container contents) but follows the principle of not running code that nothing needs. If a tool that consumes container inventory through vCenter is in use, leave the polling enabled in the VMs whose data is needed.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With ContainerInfo polling disabled, the in-guest enumeration stops and any vCenter consumer that depends on per-VM container inventory loses that data for the affected Linux VMs. Leaving the polling enabled in a VM where no downstream tool consumes the data costs nothing functionally but keeps an extra module running and emitting data that is then discarded.
PowerCLI Command Assessment: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config get containerinfo poll-interval
PowerCLI Command Remediation: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config set containerinfo poll-interval 0

Description/Title: The guest OS must deactivate Guest Operations unless required.
Discussion:
Guest Operations is the VMware Tools API surface that lets a caller in vCenter run commands, transfer files, list processes, query environment variables, and otherwise interact with the guest OS without going through the guest's own authentication. It is the mechanism behind vCenter's 'run script in guest', VLR failover reconfiguration, OS customization specs, application-consistent backup and recovery tools that need to quiesce filesystems or coordinate in-guest agents, and many third-party automation paths that touch VMs.

An attacker who reaches vCenter can use Guest Operations to execute arbitrary commands inside any VM, bypassing the guest's local authentication entirely. Disabling Guest Operations on a VM closes that path for that VM, at the cost of breaking any workflow that relies on the same API for that VM. Do not disable Guest Operations on template VMs: customization specs run during guest customization use this API, and a template with Guest Operations off will fail to customize at deploy time.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With Guest Operations disabled, vCenter and any tool that drives the API can no longer touch the guest's interior; this includes VCF Protection and Recovery's failover reconfiguration, guest customization specs, vCenter's run-script-in-guest workflows, application-consistent backup tools that quiesce the guest or coordinate VSS writers, and third-party automation that uses Guest Operations to interact with the VM. A VM in a fleet that depends on any of these workflows should keep the API enabled, even though doing so leaves the in-guest-execution surface reachable by anyone with vCenter access.
PowerCLI Command Assessment: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config get guestoperations disabled
PowerCLI Command Remediation: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config set guestoperations disabled true

Description/Title: The guest OS must deactivate Guest Store Upgrade operations unless required.
Discussion:
GuestStore is a vSphere feature for distributing files from the host to guest VMs through the VMware Tools channel: typically VMware Tools upgrade packages, but the mechanism is general enough to deliver other content the operator chooses to publish. The gueststoreupgrade policy setting controls whether VMware Tools will accept and apply upgrades that arrive through this channel.

In environments that do not use GuestStore to deliver content (the common case), the plugin is an unused intake path that can be closed. Disabling the plugin makes VMware Tools ignore the channel entirely, which removes one route a compromised vCenter, a compromised GuestStore repository, or a host with a tampered configuration could use to push content into the guest. If GuestStore is in use for legitimate distribution, leave the plugin enabled at whatever policy the distribution workflow needs.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With GuestStore upgrades disabled, VMware Tools ignores any content offered through the GuestStore channel; legitimate use cases such as host-driven Tools upgrade rollouts that rely on GuestStore distribution lose that delivery path and must use an alternative (in-guest package manager, vSphere Lifecycle Manager, direct MSI/installer push). Leaving the plugin at the default leaves the channel open for content delivery; an attacker who can stage content in the GuestStore repository (typically requiring control of the host or a path to it) can push that content to guests where the plugin is active.
PowerCLI Command Assessment: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config get gueststoreupgrade policy
PowerCLI Command Remediation: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config set gueststoreupgrade policy off

Description/Title: The guest OS must deactivate Service Discovery unless required.
Discussion:
Service Discovery is the VMware Tools plugin that periodically enumerates running services (daemons, listening processes, port bindings) inside a guest OS and reports the inventory up to vCenter, where VCF Operations consumes the data for service-level topology mapping, dependency analysis, and infrastructure-aware monitoring. When the plugin is enabled, it performs this enumeration on a recurring basis and emits the inventory through the VMware Tools channel.

In environments that do not consume Service Discovery data, the plugin collects and emits inventory that nothing reads, and keeps a module running in the guest that contributes nothing. Disabling Service Discovery stops the in-guest enumeration and narrows the VMware Tools attack surface by one module; the protection is modest in absolute terms (the plugin enumerates service and port metadata, not file contents) but follows the principle of not running code that nothing needs. If a tool that depends on Service Discovery (such as VCF Operations service-aware monitoring or topology mapping) is in use, leave the plugin enabled in the VMs whose data that tool needs.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With Service Discovery disabled, the in-guest enumeration stops and any vCenter consumer or downstream tool that depends on per-VM service inventory loses that data for the affected VMs; this includes VCF Operations service-aware monitoring and topology mapping. Leaving the plugin enabled in a VM where no downstream tool consumes the data costs nothing functionally but keeps a module running and emitting data that is then discarded.
PowerCLI Command Assessment: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config get servicediscovery disabled
PowerCLI Command Remediation: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config set servicediscovery disabled true

Description/Title: The guest OS must deactivate GlobalConf unless required.
Discussion:
GlobalConf is the VMware Tools feature that lets a central source push tools.conf updates to a guest, so an operator can reconfigure VMware Tools settings across many VMs without touching each guest individually. When GlobalConf is enabled, VMware Tools accepts and applies the pushed tools.conf changes on the guest.

Most environments do not need centralized tools.conf push: VMware Tools settings are typically established at provisioning time and rarely change across the fleet. With GlobalConf enabled, an attacker who can stage tools.conf content in the path GlobalConf consumes (typically requiring control of the host or the configuration distribution mechanism) can rewrite any tools.conf setting on the affected guests, including re-enabling previously-disabled plugins or relaxing the logging configuration. Keeping GlobalConf off limits tools.conf changes to whoever has direct access to each guest.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With GlobalConf disabled (the recommendation and the installed default), any tools.conf change must be applied directly inside each guest, which is operationally heavier in large fleets where multiple VMs need the same reconfiguration. Enabling GlobalConf simplifies that fleet-wide reconfiguration at the cost of opening a path by which a compromised host or distribution mechanism could rewrite tools.conf on every guest that accepts GlobalConf input.
PowerCLI Command Assessment: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config get globalconf enabled
PowerCLI Command Remediation: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config set globalconf enabled false

Description/Title: The guest OS must send VMware Tools logs to the system log service.
Discussion:
VMware Tools comprises several in-guest services (vmsvc, vmtoolsd, toolboxcmd, vgauthsvc) that each produce their own log output covering guest-side events: VMware Tools-driven file transfers, in-guest authentication via vgauth, configuration changes through toolboxcmd, and the Tools service lifecycle. By default each of these services writes to a file inside the guest, which means the log is lost or unreadable if the guest is deleted, becomes unbootable, or is compromised by an attacker who clears the on-disk record.

Setting each handler to syslog (Linux) or the Windows Event Service (Windows) routes the log entries through the guest's standard logging facility, where they can be picked up by any agent the operator already runs to ship logs off the guest. Centralizing the VMware Tools log stream lets an operator correlate VMware Tools events with the rest of the system's audit trail and survives the loss or compromise of the source guest. Set the handler on all four subsystems (vmsvc, toolboxcmd, vgauthsvc, vmtoolsd) so the entire VMware Tools activity stream is captured, not just one component.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With the handlers set to syslog or Windows Event Service, VMware Tools log entries flow into the guest's standard logging facility, where they can be shipped to a central collector by whatever log-forwarding agent the operator already runs in the guest. Any in-guest scripts or monitoring tools that previously parsed the VMware Tools log files directly (typically under /var/log/vmware-* on Linux or in the VMware install directory on Windows) will need to be updated to read from the syslog stream or event log instead.
PowerCLI Command Assessment:
C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config get logging vmsvc.handler
C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config get logging toolboxcmd.handler
C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config get logging vgauthsvc.handler
C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config get logging vmtoolsd.handler
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config set logging vmsvc.handler syslog
C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config set logging toolboxcmd.handler syslog
C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config set logging vgauthsvc.handler syslog
C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config set logging vmtoolsd.handler syslog

Description/Title: The guest OS must enable VMware Tools logging.
Discussion:
VMware Tools logging is the master switch that determines whether the in-guest services (vmsvc, vmtoolsd, toolboxcmd, vgauthsvc) produce log output at all. With logging enabled, those services record their activity through whichever handler is configured for each subsystem; with logging off, the activity is not recorded anywhere.

Logging serves two purposes: it gives operators a troubleshooting trail when a VMware Tools workflow fails, and it provides an audit record of guest-side events (in-guest authentication, file transfers, configuration changes) that can be correlated with the rest of the system's audit data. The destination of those log entries is set by the companion control guest-9.tools-log-forwarding; this row just keeps logging itself turned on. Individual log levels and verbosities can be tuned per subsystem through additional tools.conf entries when more or less detail is needed.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With VMware Tools logging enabled (the recommendation and the installed default), the in-guest services record their activity for both operator troubleshooting and audit correlation. Disabling logging silences all four VMware Tools subsystems and removes the only on-host record of guest-side VMware Tools activity, which would otherwise help an operator diagnose a misbehaving Tools service or reconstruct what happened on the guest after an incident.
PowerCLI Command Assessment: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config get logging log
PowerCLI Command Remediation: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config set logging log true

Description/Title: The guest OS must prevent re-customization of an already-deployed VM.
Discussion:
VM customization is the deploy-time workflow where vCenter pushes a customization spec into a freshly deployed VM and lets VMware Tools execute it inside the guest: the spec can set the hostname and IP, join the guest to a domain, run pre-customization and post-customization scripts, and otherwise reconfigure the guest at root or Administrator privilege level. The deployPkg enable-customization setting controls whether VMware Tools will accept and run a customization spec on the guest after the initial deployment.

Leaving customization enabled on an in-service VM means anyone who can stage a customization spec through vCenter (a legitimate operator, but also an attacker who has gained vCenter access or compromised the deployment automation) can run arbitrary scripts inside that VM at the most privileged user level. Disabling the setting after the initial customization closes that path; if an operator later needs to recustomize the VM, the setting can be re-enabled temporarily. Do not disable customization on template VMs themselves: the first customization run on a deployed clone will fail if the template has the setting off.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With re-customization disabled on a deployed VM, any subsequent attempt to push a customization spec into that VM will fail. Workflows that rely on running customization at later points (VCF Protection and Recovery failover IP reconfiguration, clones produced from this VM, automation that recustomizes for environment changes) need an operator to re-enable the setting temporarily, perform the workflow, and disable it again. Leaving the setting at its default keeps re-customization always available, which is operationally easier but means anyone who can stage a customization spec through vCenter can run arbitrary scripts inside the VM at the host OS's most privileged user level.
PowerCLI Command Assessment: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config get deployPkg enable-customization
PowerCLI Command Remediation: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config set deployPkg enable-customization false

Description/Title: The guest OS must disable automatic removal of optional VMware Tools features.
Discussion:
