VMware Tools includes a number of optional in-guest components (script execution agents, monitoring helpers, service-discovery features, container introspection) that can be installed or omitted depending on the guest's needs. The autoupgrade allow-remove-feature setting in tools.conf controls whether the periodic VMware Tools auto-upgrade process is allowed to drop existing optional features from an installation.

When the setting is true (the default), an auto-upgrade can remove an optional component that the operator deliberately installed to support a specific workload, breaking whatever depends on that feature without warning. Setting it to false means auto-upgrades still patch the currently installed feature set but cannot remove anything; any change to the installed feature set is an explicit operator action, not a quiet consequence of upgrade.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With auto-feature-removal disabled, VMware Tools auto-upgrades continue to patch and update the features that are already installed in the guest, but installed optional features cannot be silently removed through that channel. An operator who needs to remove a feature must do so explicitly through a manual Tools install or a guest-OS package management path, rather than relying on the auto-upgrade process to drop it.
PowerCLI Command Assessment: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config get autoupgrade allow-remove-feature
PowerCLI Command Remediation: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config set autoupgrade allow-remove-feature false

Description/Title: The guest OS must run a current version of VMware Tools.
Discussion:
VMware Tools is the package of paravirtualized drivers, hypervisor integration agents, and in-guest services that lets ESX coordinate with the guest OS: graceful shutdown, performance counters, time sync, customization at deploy time, and the network and storage drivers (vmxnet3, pvscsi) that replace the emulated legacy device drivers. Tools releases include both functional updates and security fixes for the in-guest binaries.

A guest running an out-of-date Tools version is exposed to any vulnerability fixed since its installed version, and may not run optimally because driver and performance fixes from newer releases are not present. On Linux distributions, open-vm-tools is typically installed and updated through the distribution's package manager; on Windows, Tools is delivered separately and must be tracked alongside guest OS patching, with the vmxnet3 and pvscsi drivers also available through Windows Update for environments that prefer that delivery path.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: A guest running back-revision VMware Tools accumulates exposure to any vulnerability fixed in subsequent Tools releases, and may miss driver and integration improvements that affect performance or compatibility with newer host features. The update workflow differs by guest type: Linux distributions update open-vm-tools through the package manager, while Windows guests need an explicit Tools update path (in-guest installer, vSphere Lifecycle Manager, or Windows Update for the driver components in WSUS).
PowerCLI Command Remediation: Site-Specific. Multiple ways to update VMware Tools. Drivers for vmxnet3 and pvscsi are also available via Windows Update, please ensure you are importing them into tools such as WSUS.

Description/Title: The guest OS must enable VMware Tools auto-upgrade, or use an equivalent external update path.
Discussion:
The autoupgrade allow-upgrade setting controls whether VMware Tools accepts the periodic auto-upgrade workflow that vSphere initiates against the guest. With auto-upgrade enabled, the host can push Tools updates into the guest on a schedule, which keeps the in-guest binaries close to current without operator intervention on each VM.

The recommendation is to leave auto-upgrade enabled unless Tools is being patched through another path (open-vm-tools through a Linux distribution's package manager, WSUS for Windows, vSphere Lifecycle Manager image-based update, or a configuration management tool that pushes installer packages directly). The companion controls guest-9.tools-add-feature and guest-9.tools-remove-feature scope what auto-upgrade is allowed to change: the recommended configuration lets auto-upgrade patch the installed feature set but not add or remove features, so a guest that has been deliberately hardened keeps its feature set across upgrades.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With auto-upgrade enabled and the companion controls (guest-9.tools-add-feature, guest-9.tools-remove-feature) set to refuse feature changes, the host can keep Tools patched across the fleet without modifying which features are installed in each guest. Disabling auto-upgrade shifts the update responsibility entirely to whatever external channel is in use; a guest with no external update path and auto-upgrade off will drift downlevel.
PowerCLI Command Assessment: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config get autoupgrade allow-upgrade
PowerCLI Command Remediation: C:\Program Files\VMware\VMware Tools\VMwareToolboxCmd.exe config set autoupgrade allow-upgrade true

Description/Title: The VCF Installer appliance must be removed or powered off after deployment completes, unless it has been converted into the SDDC Manager.
Discussion:
The VCF Installer is a temporary appliance used to drive the initial bringup of a VCF deployment. During bringup it holds the deployment automation, generates initial credentials for the management components, and retains a record of the configuration applied to the new environment. Once bringup is complete, the Installer typically has no further role: ongoing fleet management is done from VCF Operations and the operator-facing UIs.

Leaving the Installer running after deployment keeps an appliance on the management network that still holds bringup-time credentials and a complete picture of the deployment, which an attacker who reaches it could use to learn the environment topology or extract privileged credentials. Power off or remove the appliance once deployment is finished. In deployments where the VCF Installer was placed onto an existing ESX host, the appliance is reused and becomes the SDDC Manager; in that case it must be left in place.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With the Installer powered off or removed after deployment, the bringup-time appliance is no longer reachable on the management network and the credentials it held during deployment can no longer be queried from it. Leaving it running indefinitely keeps an extra attack surface alive: an attacker who reaches the Installer can extract the deployment record and any credentials still cached on the appliance, and use that information to move laterally into the management plane it just deployed.
Baseline Suggested Value: Either removed or confirmed that it has been converted to the SDDC Manager.

Description/Title: Capture user identity in VCF Operations for Networks audit logs.
Discussion:
VCF Operations for Networks gates the capture of user-identifying information in audit logs behind an "Allow collection of Personally Identifiable Information" toggle. Without it enabled, audit records describe what happened but not who initiated the action, which makes accountability and incident response harder. Enable the setting so that audit records carry the operator's identity. Confirm with your privacy team that user identity in audit logs is permitted under the deployment's data-handling rules.

Not applicable if VCF Operations for Networks is not deployed.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Audit records will include user identity (usernames and similar attributes) from the moment the setting is enabled. Existing audit records are not retroactively enriched. Leaving the setting disabled means audit records continue to describe what happened on the system without identifying which operator initiated the action, which complicates accountability investigations and incident response.
Setting Location: UI: VCF Operations for Networks > Settings > Logs > Audit Logs

Description/Title: Configure the VCF Operations for Networks login banner.
Discussion:
A login banner displayed on the authentication page tells anyone reaching the login screen the conditions of system use, the consent-to-monitoring stance, and the consequences of unauthorized access. It is not a direct security control: a banner does not prevent an attacker from attempting to authenticate. Its value is procedural; the banner sets the conditions an authorized user agrees to by continuing, and the disclosure of monitoring supports administrative and legal follow-up against unauthorized access.

The banner text should be supplied by the organization's legal advisors.

Not applicable if VCF Operations for Networks is not deployed.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With a banner configured, anyone reaching the VCF Operations for Networks login page sees the organization's notice and consent text before authenticating. Leaving the banner unconfigured does not change the system's authentication behavior; users authenticate the same way, but without the notice that supports administrative or legal action against unauthorized use.
Baseline Suggested Value: Configured (Display Banners enabled with organization-approved consent text)
Setting Location: UI: VCF Operations for Networks > Settings > System Configuration > Login Banners

Description/Title: VCF Operations for Networks must enforce an idle session timeout.
Discussion:
Session timeouts limit how long an authenticated session in VCF Operations for Networks can remain idle before the user is forced to re-authenticate. An unattended session that never expires is a credential exposure: anyone with physical access to the operator's workstation, or anyone who finds an unlocked terminal, can use the session as the original user with no further authentication challenge.

A reasonable inactivity timeout balances operator convenience against this exposure: long enough that operators don't have to log in repeatedly during a working session, short enough that an unattended terminal does not stay authenticated overnight or across the operator's absence.

Not applicable if VCF Operations for Networks is not deployed.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With an inactivity timeout enforced, an unattended VCF Operations for Networks session ends after the configured idle interval and the user must re-authenticate to continue. Without a configured timeout, sessions remain authenticated as long as the browser tab or API client is alive, which leaves the credential usable by anyone who reaches the operator's workstation while the session is still open.
Setting Location: UI: VCF Operations for Networks > Settings > System Configuration

Description/Title: NSX Manager must enforce an account lockout after consecutive failed authentication attempts.
Discussion:
NSX Manager exposes both an API and a CLI as authentication surfaces, and configures account lockout policy separately for each. When the configured number of consecutive failed authentication attempts is reached on either surface, NSX Manager blocks further attempts against that account for the configured lockout period; after the period elapses the account becomes available again without operator intervention. This rate-limits brute-force password-guessing attacks, where an attacker submits many login attempts to discover a working credential.

The recommended values follow current authentication-policy thinking: a small but non-zero failure threshold that tolerates occasional mistyped passwords from legitimate users, paired with a lockout duration short enough to avoid a denial-of-service vector against legitimate accounts. Both the API and CLI auth-policy must be configured to apply consistent lockout across the two surfaces.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With lockout policy configured on both surfaces, NSX Manager refuses further authentication attempts against an account after the threshold is reached and the account becomes available again after the lockout period without operator intervention. Without the policy in place, an attacker can submit unlimited password guesses against any NSX Manager account; with the policy too aggressive, legitimate users get locked out by routine typos and the operator has to intervene more often.
Configuration Parameter:
From an NSX Manager shell, run the following commands:

> set auth-policy api lockout-reset-period 900
> set auth-policy api lockout-period 900
> set auth-policy api max-auth-failures 5
> set auth-policy cli lockout-period 900
> set auth-policy cli max-auth-failures 5
Baseline Suggested Value: API and CLI: max-auth-failures 5; lockout-period 900 seconds; API lockout-reset-period 900 seconds

Description/Title: Configure NSX Manager API rate and concurrency limits.
Discussion: NSX Manager exposes a REST API that automation, operators, and third-party tooling all consume. Without explicit rate and concurrency limits, a misbehaving client or a malicious caller can saturate the API service and degrade availability for everyone else. Setting per-client and global limits gives NSX Manager a defined budget for serving API traffic.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Automation that exceeds the per-client rate or concurrency limit will be throttled. Tune the limits upward for environments with heavy legitimate API use rather than removing them.
Configuration Parameter:
From an NSX Manager shell, run the following NSX CLI commands:

> set service http client-api-rate-limit 100
> set service http client-api-concurrency-limit 40
> set service http global-api-concurrency-limit 199
Baseline Suggested Value: client-api-rate-limit: 100 requests/sec, client-api-concurrency-limit: 40 connections, global-api-concurrency-limit: 199 connections

Description/Title: Configure scheduled backups of NSX Manager.
Discussion: NSX Manager backups contain the NSX configuration, certificates, and credential material needed to recover the management plane after a failure or compromise. A backup destination and a recurring schedule must both be configured: a destination alone produces no backups, and an unscheduled manual export goes stale quickly. Where supported, additionally enable change-detection so a backup is triggered when NSX configuration changes.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Without backups configured, a failure, failed upgrade, or compromise that corrupts the NSX management plane leaves no recovery point: the configuration (segments, gateways, firewall policy, certificates) must be rebuilt by hand. With scheduled backups to a reachable SFTP destination, NSX Manager can be restored to a recent state instead. Backup files contain sensitive material protected by the backup passphrase, and the SFTP target must be reachable from each NSX Manager with capacity for the retained set.
Configuration Parameter:
From the NSX Manager web interface, go to System > Lifecycle Management > Backup and Restore.

Under "SFTP Server", click "Edit" and enter the server, directory path, username, password, SSH fingerprint, and passphrase.

Under "Schedule", click "Edit", enable "Recurring Backup", and set the interval. Optionally enable "Detect NSX configuration change" to trigger backups when configuration changes.
Baseline Suggested Value: Site-Specific (SFTP destination + recurring schedule; change-detection enabled where supported)
Setting Location: UI: NSX Manager > System > Lifecycle Management > Backup and Restore

Description/Title: Enable BGP authentication on Tier-0 Gateway peers with a unique password per autonomous system.
Discussion:
An unauthenticated BGP session can be hijacked by a peer that injects a fictitious route, allowing an attacker to attract traffic to an unintended destination. Configure an MD5 password on every BGP neighbor on the Tier-0 Gateway, and use a different password for each peer autonomous system so that compromise of one peer's key does not extend to other BGP sessions.

Not applicable to Tier-0 Gateways that do not use BGP.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: The matching password must be configured on the peer side at the same time. Mismatched or missing passwords cause the BGP session to fail to establish.
Baseline Suggested Value: Authentication: MD5, Password: Configured (unique per AS, up to 20 chars)

Description/Title: Configure BGP maximum prefix limits on Tier-0 Gateway neighbors.
Discussion:
Without a maximum-prefix limit on a BGP neighbor, a misconfigured or malicious peer can flood the Tier-0 Gateway with a large number of routes, degrading routing performance and causing legitimate traffic to be dropped. Set Maximum Routes on each BGP neighbor to a value slightly above the expected number of prefixes from that peer.

Not applicable to Tier-0 Gateways that do not use BGP.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: If the limit is set below the legitimate prefix count from a peer, the BGP session will be torn down when the limit is reached. Tune the threshold for normal peer growth and review the maximum after routing topology changes.
Baseline Suggested Value: Per-neighbor Maximum Routes configured to a value slightly above the expected prefix count for that peer
Setting Location: UI: Networking, Connectivity, Tier-0 Gateways > BGP > BGP Neighbors > Router Filter > Maximum Routes

Description/Title: Disable the NSX Manager Customer Experience Improvement Program.
Discussion: The Customer Experience Improvement Program (CEIP) collects product usage and diagnostic data from NSX Manager and sends it to the vendor for product improvement. The data describes how the product is configured and operated; it is not customer workload data. Organizations that restrict outbound data from the management environment, or that operate the management plane in an isolated network, disable CEIP so NSX Manager does not originate that traffic. This is a data-handling policy choice that reduces outbound communication from the management plane rather than a control that changes the security state of NSX.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling CEIP removes a feedback channel to the vendor for product improvement data; it does not affect NSX function or the supportability of unrelated cases. Leaving it enabled continues that outbound reporting.
Setting Location: UI: NSX Manager > System > Settings > General Settings > Customer Program

Description/Title: NSX Tier-0 Gateway must not enable DHCP unless it is required.
Discussion:
An NSX Tier-0 Gateway can run a DHCP server or DHCP relay to provide addressing for workloads on connected segments. Where DHCP is provided by another system, or where the segments behind the gateway use static addressing, the DHCP function on that Tier-0 Gateway is an unused service.

Keeping unused DHCP off is a least-functionality measure: it removes a service that would otherwise need maintenance and monitoring, and closes a path by which a misconfigured or tampered DHCP service could hand out incorrect addresses, gateways, or DNS servers to redirect workload traffic (an attack that requires NSX management-plane access to mount). DHCP is disabled by default on a Tier-0 Gateway, so for most environments this control confirms the default state has not drifted; enable DHCP only on the gateways where NSX is deliberately the DHCP provider.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: DHCP is off by default on Tier-0 Gateways, so confirming this state has no operational effect on environments that use external DHCP or static addressing. Enabling DHCP where NSX is not the intended provider can produce address conflicts or misdirected traffic; keep it off except on gateways deliberately configured as the DHCP source.

Description/Title: Disable unused NSX Manager local accounts.
Discussion: NSX Manager ships with the local accounts "audit", "guestuser1", and "guestuser2" active in addition to the designated administrator. Limiting active local accounts to a single designated account of last resort reduces the surface available for credential attacks against the local database when remote authentication is unavailable. Deactivate any local account that is not required.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: None for accounts that are not in use. Deactivating an account in active use breaks any process or operator depending on it.
Baseline Suggested Value: Only the designated administrator (or account of last resort) active
Setting Location: UI: NSX Manager > System > Settings > User Management > Local Users

Description/Title: Configure Tier-0 Gateway iBGP peering to use the loopback address as the source.
Discussion:
iBGP sessions are easier to manage and harder to spoof when peers source their session traffic from a loopback address rather than a physical interface address. Ingress filters for management traffic become simpler because all session source addresses come from the loopback range, and audit records record the loopback address consistently rather than whichever physical interface happened to carry the session.

Not applicable to Tier-0 Gateways that do not use iBGP.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Adding a loopback interface and re-pointing iBGP sessions briefly disrupts the affected sessions while they are re-established. More than one loopback may be required depending on the routing architecture.
Baseline Suggested Value: Loopback interface defined on Tier-0; iBGP neighbor Source Address set to the loopback address
Setting Location: UI: Networking, Connectivity, Tier-0 Gateways > Interfaces (Loopback) and BGP > BGP Neighbors > Source Addresses

Description/Title: NSX Tier-0 Gateway must remove inactive interfaces.
Discussion:
An NSX Tier-0 Gateway connects to external networks through logical interfaces (uplinks, service interfaces, and VLAN sub-interfaces on those uplinks). An interface that is configured but no longer in use is a forwarding path that nobody is actively monitoring: it can keep a segment or VLAN reachable through the gateway after that connectivity was supposed to be retired, and a later misconfiguration on a stale interface can reintroduce a path without anyone noticing.

Remove the configuration for interfaces that are no longer needed, and remove VLAN sub-interfaces that sit on a removed uplink or are themselves unused. Keeping the gateway's interface set limited to what is actively in use is a least-functionality measure that also keeps the routing configuration easier to review.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: May affect connectivity if interfaces that appear inactive are actually in standby or are planned for future use. Thorough network topology review is required before removing interfaces to avoid unintended connectivity loss.
Baseline Suggested Value: No inactive or unused interfaces remain configured

Description/Title: Enable FIPS-validated cryptography for NSX load balancers.
Discussion:
NSX load balancer instances terminate TLS for the services they front. Enabling the global FIPS mode for load balancers constrains the load balancer crypto stack to FIPS 140-validated algorithms and modes, aligning load balancer TLS with the rest of the VCF FIPS posture.

The global setting applies to load balancer instances created after the change. Existing load balancer instances must be detached from and reattached to their Tier-1 gateway to pick up the new setting.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Detaching a load balancer from its Tier-1 gateway interrupts traffic for the load balancer instance. Plan the reattachment during a maintenance window. Older clients that cannot negotiate FIPS-approved cipher suites will fail to connect.
Setting Location: API: NSX policy API /infra/global-config (no UI toggle)

Description/Title: NSX Manager must set service logging levels to capture audit-relevant events.
Discussion:
NSX Manager runs several internal services (authentication, HTTP/API, manager, async replicator, telemetry), each with its own logging level. At the default level, a service records only failures, which omits the routine authentication events, API calls, and configuration changes that an audit trail and a forensic investigation depend on. Raising the logging level on each service captures those events.

Set the logging level on each of the relevant services so audit coverage is consistent across the management plane. The captured events feed the central log forwarding configured in nsx-9.log-forwarding, where they are retained and correlated with logs from other components.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Raising the logging level increases log volume on the NSX Manager appliances and on the central log server, which needs capacity for the additional events. The trade-off is intended: without the additional detail, security-relevant events (authentication, API activity, configuration changes) are not recorded and cannot be reviewed after an incident.
Configuration Parameter:
From an NSX Manager shell, run the following NSX CLI commands:

> set service async_replicator logging-level info
> set service auth logging-level info
> set service http logging-level info
> set service manager logging-level info
> set service telemetry logging-level info

Description/Title: Configure the NSX Manager login banner.
Discussion:
A login banner displayed on the authentication page tells anyone reaching the login screen the conditions of system use, the consent-to-monitoring stance, and the consequences of unauthorized access. It is not a direct security control: a banner does not prevent an attacker from attempting to authenticate. Its value is procedural; the banner sets the conditions an authorized user agrees to by continuing, and the disclosure of monitoring supports administrative and legal follow-up against unauthorized access.

The banner text should be supplied by the organization's legal advisors; an example wording is included in the Baseline Suggested Value field as a starting point.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With a banner configured, anyone reaching the NSX Manager login page sees the organization's notice and consent text before authenticating. Leaving the banner unconfigured does not change the system's authentication behavior; users authenticate the same way, but without the notice that supports administrative or legal action against unauthorized use.
Baseline Suggested Value:
Consult your organization's legal advisors for text that is applicable to your environment.

An example is: "Authorized users only. Actual or attempted unauthorized use of this system is prohibited and may result in criminal, civil, security, or administrative proceedings and/or penalties. Use of this information system indicates consent to monitoring and recording, without notice or permission. Users have no expectation of privacy in any use of this system. Any information stored on, or transiting this system, or obtained by monitoring and/or recording, may be disclosed to law enforcement and/or used in accordance with Federal law, State statute, and organization policy. If you are not an authorized user of this system, exit the system at this time."
Setting Location: UI: NSX Manager > System > General System Settings > User Interface > Login Consent Settings

Description/Title: NSX Tier-0 Gateway must disable multicast and PIM where they are not required.
Discussion:
Protocol Independent Multicast (PIM) is the routing protocol an NSX Tier-0 Gateway uses to forward multicast traffic between networks. Multicast and PIM are not configured on a Tier-0 Gateway by default; they are enabled deliberately when an application requires multicast delivery. Where multicast is not used, PIM should remain off; where it is used, PIM should be enabled only on the interfaces that actually carry multicast, not on every interface.

Leaving PIM enabled on interfaces that do not need it widens where multicast traffic can flow, which can let multicast reach networks or listeners it was never intended for. Confirm multicast requirements before enabling PIM, and scope it to the minimum set of interfaces.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling multicast where it is not in use, or scoping PIM to only the interfaces that carry multicast, has no effect on environments that do not use multicast. Where multicast is in use, removing PIM from an interface a multicast application depends on will break that application's delivery, so confirm which interfaces carry multicast before narrowing PIM. Unicast routing protocols such as OSPF and BGP are unaffected, since they do not rely on multicast routing.

Description/Title: NSX Tier-0 Gateway must enable OSPF authentication.
Discussion:
An NSX Tier-0 Gateway that peers via OSPF accepts routing updates from its OSPF neighbors. Without authentication, a rogue or spoofed neighbor can inject false routes (a traffic-attraction attack), pulling traffic toward an unintended or attacker-controlled destination where it can be observed or dropped. OSPF authentication attaches a keyed MD5 digest to each routing message so the gateway accepts updates only from neighbors that share the configured key; it protects the integrity and authenticity of the routing exchange, not its confidentiality.

Configure OSPF authentication with a key ID and password on every OSPF adjacency on the Tier-0 Gateway. Not applicable to Tier-0 Gateways that do not use OSPF.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With OSPF authentication configured, the Tier-0 Gateway rejects routing updates that do not carry the correct key, which closes the route-injection path. The matching key must be configured on the OSPF neighbors at the same time; a mismatched or missing key causes the OSPF adjacency to fail to form, so coordinate the change on both sides. Not applicable where OSPF is not in use.
Baseline Suggested Value: Authentication: MD5, Key ID: Configured, Password: Configured

Description/Title: Enforce password complexity on NSX Manager local accounts.
Discussion:
NSX Manager enforces a password policy on its local accounts, controlling what passwords the system accepts when an operator sets or changes one. The recommended policy raises the minimum length and requires at least one uppercase, lowercase, numeric, and special character, and limits how many times a single character can repeat. Length is the dominant factor in resisting guessing and brute-force attempts, where an attacker systematically tries many candidate passwords; the character-class floor adds basic variety without forcing the long lists of composition rules that push users toward predictable substitutions.

This policy should be in place on each NSX Manager node so it is consistent across the cluster. In the NSX CLI, the upper-chars, lower-chars, digits, and special-chars values set to -1 each require at least one character of that class.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Raising the minimum length and adding a repeat limit means new or changed NSX Manager passwords must be longer and avoid long single-character runs; existing passwords keep working until they are next changed. A shorter minimum or fewer required classes lets operators set trivially guessable passwords that brute-force attempts crack quickly.
Configuration Parameter:
From an NSX Manager shell, run the following NSX CLI commands:

> set password-complexity minimum-password-length 15
> set password-complexity upper-chars -1
> set password-complexity lower-chars -1
> set password-complexity digits -1
> set password-complexity special-chars -1
> set password-complexity max-repeats 8
Installation Default Value: Minimum length 12; at least 1 uppercase, 1 lowercase, 1 numeric, and 1 special character; no limit on repeated characters
Baseline Suggested Value: Minimum length 15; at least 1 uppercase, 1 lowercase, 1 numeric, and 1 special character; maximum repeated characters 8

Description/Title: NSX Tier-0 Gateway must enable unicast Reverse Path Forwarding to drop spoofed-source packets.
Discussion:
Unicast Reverse Path Forwarding (uRPF) checks the source address of an incoming packet against the gateway's routing table: in strict mode, the packet is accepted only if it arrived on the interface the gateway would use to route a reply back to that source. Packets whose source address could not legitimately have come from the interface they arrived on are dropped. This blocks IP source-address spoofing, where an attacker forges the source address of packets (for example, to launch reflection or denial-of-service attacks from a compromised host, or to impersonate a trusted address).

Enable uRPF in strict mode on the Tier-0 Gateway interfaces where the expected traffic path is symmetric. Where routing is intentionally asymmetric (traffic arrives on a different interface than replies leave), strict mode will drop legitimate traffic, so confirm the routing design before enabling it.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With uRPF in strict mode, the gateway drops packets whose source address does not match the return path for the interface they arrived on, blocking spoofed-source traffic. The main caveat is asymmetric routing: if legitimate traffic for a source can arrive on an interface other than the one the gateway would route replies to, strict mode will drop it. Confirm the routing is symmetric on the affected interfaces, or use a looser mode, before enabling strict uRPF in production.

Description/Title: Disable Tier-0 Gateway routing protocols that are not in use.
Discussion: Routing protocols (BGP, OSPF) left enabled on a Tier-0 Gateway without a corresponding peer offer no operational benefit and broaden the attack surface available to a hostile or misconfigured router on the same segment. Review the routing-protocol configuration on each Tier-0 Gateway and disable any protocol that is not actively peering.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling a routing protocol that is in use brings down its sessions and causes routes learned through that protocol to be withdrawn. Confirm that the protocol has no live peers before disabling.
Installation Default Value: Site-Specific (BGP and OSPF are configurable per Tier-0; VCF deployments often have BGP enabled to peer with the physical fabric)
Baseline Suggested Value: Only routing protocols actively in use are enabled
Setting Location: UI: Networking, Connectivity, Tier-0 Gateways > BGP and OSPF panels

Description/Title: When NSX Manager runs as a multi-node cluster, DRS anti-affinity rules must separate the nodes across hosts.
Discussion:
When NSX Manager is deployed as a multi-node cluster for high availability or redundancy, DRS anti-affinity rules keep the cluster member appliances on different ESX hosts, so a single host failure cannot take down more than one cluster member at once. Without anti-affinity, DRS could place multiple cluster members on the same host, where one host failure removes the redundancy the cluster was meant to provide.

Use DRS anti-affinity "should" rules rather than "must" rules, so DRS and Lifecycle Manager can still relocate the appliances during patching or HA events. Not applicable if NSX Manager runs as a single appliance rather than a cluster.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With anti-affinity rules in place, a single ESX host failure can take at most one NSX Manager cluster member offline, preserving management-plane availability. Without them, DRS may co-locate cluster members on one host, so a single host failure can remove multiple members and defeat the cluster's redundancy. The control does not apply to single-appliance deployments.

Description/Title: NSX Manager must enforce an idle session timeout on its API and CLI.
Discussion:
Session timeouts limit how long an authenticated session can remain idle before the user must re-authenticate. NSX Manager exposes both an HTTP API and a CLI as administrative access surfaces, and an idle timeout should be configured on each. An unattended session that never expires is a credential exposure: anyone who reaches the operator's workstation or an open terminal can act as the original user with no further authentication.

Configure the idle timeout on both the API and the CLI. A timeout value of 0 disables the timeout entirely and must not be used.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With an idle timeout enforced on both surfaces, an unattended NSX Manager session ends after the configured interval and the user must re-authenticate; administrators may be logged out during a long pause in interactive work and need to log back in. Without a timeout (or with it set to 0), sessions stay authenticated indefinitely, leaving the credential usable by anyone who reaches an open session.
Configuration Parameter:
From an NSX Manager shell, run the following NSX CLI commands:

> set service http session-timeout 900
> set cli-timeout 900

Description/Title: NSX Manager must not use SNMP v1 or v2c.
Discussion:
Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) versions 1 and 2c authenticate using community strings that are sent across the network in cleartext and provide no encryption of the data they carry. An attacker who can observe SNMP traffic can capture the community string and use it to read device and network information, and a writable community string can be used to alter device configuration. SNMP version 3 replaces the community-string model with per-user authentication and optional encryption.

SNMP is not configured on NSX Manager by default. Where SNMP monitoring is required, use version 3 with authentication and privacy; do not configure version 1 or 2c polling or trap destinations. If any v1/v2c configuration exists on the node profile, remove it.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Removing SNMP v1/v2c affects any monitoring system that polls NSX over those versions; move those integrations to SNMP v3, the NSX API, or syslog/VCF Operations forwarding before removing the v1/v2c configuration. SNMP v3 monitoring is unaffected, and environments that do not use SNMP at all have nothing to change.
Configuration Parameter: From the NSX Manager web interface, go to System > Configuration > Fabric > Profiles > Node Profiles. Click "All NSX Nodes" and delete any v1 or v2c polling or trap configurations.
Baseline Suggested Value: SNMP v1 and v2c disabled; SNMP v3 only if SNMP is required
Setting Location: UI: NSX Manager > System > Configuration > Fabric > Profiles > Node Profiles

Description/Title: The NSX Manager SSH service must be deactivated except for temporary diagnostic use.
Discussion:
NSX Manager runs an SSH daemon that gives administrators an interactive shell on the appliance. Anyone who reaches the SSH listener with valid credentials gets shell access to the NSX management appliance, including its configuration and stored secrets. SSH is intended as a break-glass tool for diagnostics and recovery when the UI and API are not enough; it is not the routine management interface.

The SSH service should be stopped, set not to start on boot, and root login disabled, so the appliance does not present an interactive shell listener during normal operation. An operator who needs SSH for a specific task starts it for the duration of that task and stops it again afterward.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Keeping SSH stopped with start-on-boot disabled has no day-to-day operational cost; NSX is managed through the UI and API. Leaving SSH running, set to start on boot, or with root login enabled leaves an interactive shell listener on the management appliance at all times, which is the surface the recommendation removes.
Configuration Parameter:
From an NSX Manager shell, run the following NSX CLI commands:

> stop service ssh
> clear service ssh start-on-boot
> clear ssh root-login
Installation Default Value: Stopped, start-on-boot disabled, root login disabled
Baseline Suggested Value: SSH stopped, start-on-boot disabled, root login disabled

Description/Title: The NSX Tier-0 Gateway must advertise a sufficient IPv6 Router Advertisement hop limit.
Discussion:
IPv6 Router Advertisement messages convey a Neighbor Discovery hop limit that hosts on the segment adopt for their outbound packets. If a custom Neighbor Discovery profile advertises a hop limit that is too small, packets expire in transit before reaching destinations more than a few hops away, breaking connectivity for those hosts. An attacker who can influence the advertised hop limit could use a low value to disrupt communications on the segment.

The default Neighbor Discovery profile on a Tier-0 Gateway advertises a hop limit that meets the baseline, so the common case requires no change. Any custom ND profile in use must continue to advertise at least the baseline minimum. Not applicable to Tier-0 Gateways without IPv6 forwarding enabled.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: The default hop limit meets the baseline, so confirming it has no operational effect. If a custom Neighbor Discovery profile sets the advertised hop limit too low, hosts on the segment lose connectivity to destinations beyond the reduced number of hops, since their packets expire in transit before arriving.
Setting Location: UI: Networking, Connectivity, Tier-0 Gateways > Additional Settings > ND Profile

Description/Title: Disable Tier-1 Gateway DHCP service when not in use.
Discussion:
A Tier-1 Gateway can provide DHCP services (a DHCP server or relay) to the segments attached to it. A new Tier-1 Gateway does not allocate DHCP by default; the service is configured deliberately where dynamic addressing is required. DHCP left configured on a Tier-1 Gateway that has no active clients is an unused service that adds attack surface and configuration to maintain.

Where no segment served by the Tier-1 Gateway requires dynamic IP allocation, set DHCP Configuration to "No Dynamic IP Address Allocation". Enable DHCP only on the gateways where NSX is the intended DHCP provider.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling DHCP on a Tier-1 Gateway that is serving DHCP-dependent workloads will leave new attachments unable to obtain IP addresses. Confirm that no connected segment depends on Tier-1-provided DHCP before disabling.
Baseline Suggested Value: DHCP Configuration = No Dynamic IP Address Allocation (when DHCP is not required)
Setting Location: UI: Networking, Connectivity, Tier-1 Gateways > DHCP Configuration

Description/Title: Remove inactive interfaces and stale linked segments from Tier-1 Gateways.
Discussion: An inactive interface or linked segment is rarely monitored and can expose a path into the network if an attacker reaches the underlying fabric. On a Tier-1 Gateway, review both Service Interfaces and Linked Segments and remove any that are no longer in use.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Removing an interface that is in standby or planned for future use will break connectivity for the attached workloads. Confirm there are no active workloads on a linked segment before disconnecting it.
Baseline Suggested Value: Inactive linked segments disconnected; inactive Service Interfaces deleted
Setting Location: UI: Networking > Connectivity > Tier-1 Gateways > Service Interfaces and Linked Segments

Description/Title: The NSX Tier-1 Gateway must advertise a sufficient IPv6 Router Advertisement hop limit.
Discussion:
IPv6 Router Advertisement messages convey a Neighbor Discovery hop limit that hosts on the segment adopt for their outbound packets. If a custom Neighbor Discovery profile advertises a hop limit that is too small, packets expire in transit before reaching destinations more than a few hops away, breaking connectivity for those hosts. An attacker who can influence the advertised hop limit could use a low value to disrupt communications on the segment.

The default Neighbor Discovery profile on a Tier-1 Gateway advertises a hop limit that meets the baseline, so the common case requires no change. Any custom ND profile in use must continue to advertise at least the baseline minimum. Not applicable to Tier-1 Gateways without IPv6 forwarding enabled.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: The default hop limit meets the baseline, so confirming it has no operational effect. If a custom Neighbor Discovery profile sets the advertised hop limit too low, hosts on the segment lose connectivity to destinations beyond the reduced number of hops, since their packets expire in transit before arriving.
Setting Location: UI: Networking > Connectivity > Tier-1 Gateways > Additional Settings > ND Profile

Description/Title: Disable Tier-1 Gateway multicast when not in use.
Discussion:
A Tier-1 Gateway can route multicast traffic for the workloads attached to it. Multicast is not enabled on a Tier-1 Gateway by default; it is turned on deliberately when an application requires multicast delivery. Multicast left enabled on a Tier-1 Gateway with no active multicast applications is an unused capability that broadens the gateway's attack surface.

Where no workloads attached to the Tier-1 Gateway require multicast routing, disable multicast at the Tier-1 level. Enable it only where a multicast application actually depends on it.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling multicast where applications depend on it breaks those flows. Inventory multicast-using applications before disabling.
Setting Location: UI: Networking > Connectivity > Tier-1 Gateways > Multicast

Description/Title: Set NSX Manager timezone to UTC for consistent audit timestamps.
Discussion: Audit records must use a consistent time reference across components so that events can be correlated during forensic analysis. NSX Manager appliances default to UTC. Verify that all NSX Manager nodes report UTC rather than a local time zone, and apply a node profile when nodes need to be brought back into line.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With all NSX Manager nodes on UTC, their audit timestamps line up directly with other VCF components for cross-system correlation. Setting a local time zone instead shifts the timestamps, which complicates aligning NSX events with other logs during an investigation (especially across daylight-saving transitions) without changing what is recorded.
Configuration Parameter:
From an NSX Manager shell, run the following NSX CLI command:

> set timezone UTC

To apply the setting via node profile, edit the "All NSX Nodes" node profile and select UTC from the timezone drop-down.
Setting Location: UI: NSX Manager > System > Configuration > Fabric > Profiles > Node Profiles (or NSX Manager Shell)

Description/Title: NSX Manager must use TLS with approved protocol versions and cipher suites.
Discussion:
NSX Manager uses TLS to protect its management connections: the web UI, the policy and manager APIs, and inter-node cluster communication. The protections depend on which TLS protocol versions and cipher suites the manager will negotiate. Older protocol versions (TLS 1.1) and legacy CBC-mode cipher suites have known weaknesses and should not be negotiable.

The recommended configuration disables TLS 1.1 and the legacy CBC cipher suites, leaving modern TLS 1.2/1.3 with AEAD (GCM) cipher suites. NSX Manager ships with a modern profile; this control confirms that weak protocol versions or ciphers have not been re-enabled. Applying a change restarts the API service on each node, with a brief delay before the new configuration takes effect.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: A stricter TLS configuration drops legacy protocol versions and cipher suites that older clients may still rely on; any client that cannot negotiate TLS 1.2+ with a modern cipher loses connectivity to NSX Manager until it is updated. Applying the change restarts the API service on each cluster node, so expect a short management-plane interruption during the rollout.

Description/Title: VCF Operations must lock accounts after consecutive failed logon attempts.
Discussion: By limiting the number of failed login attempts, the risk of unauthorized system access via user password guessing, otherwise known as brute forcing, is reduced. Limits are imposed by locking the account.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With account lockout configured, VCF Operations refuses further authentication against an account after the failure threshold is reached, rate-limiting brute-force password guessing. Without it, an attacker can submit unlimited password guesses; with the threshold set too low, legitimate users get locked out by routine typos and need an administrator to intervene.
Baseline Suggested Value: 5 consecutive failed attempts within a 15-minute window
Setting Location: UI: Operate > Global Settings > User Access > Password Policy > Account Lockout

Description/Title: Retain VCF Operations remediation action history for an appropriate interval.
Discussion:
VCF Operations keeps a historical record of the remediation actions taken through it: who ran what action, against what, and when. The action history retention setting controls how many days that record is kept before it is purged. It is part of the audit trail for changes made to the environment through VCF Operations.

The default retention is short enough that the record of an action may be gone before it is needed for an investigation into a change or incident. A longer retention keeps that history available for forensic review; set it to cover the period your investigations and audit obligations require, bounded by available storage.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: A longer action-history retention keeps the record of remediation actions available for after-the-fact review. Too short an interval purges the record before an investigation into a change can use it.
Setting Location: UI: Operate > Global Settings > Data Retention > Action History

Description/Title: Disable the VCF Operations Customer Experience Improvement Program.
Discussion: The Customer Experience Improvement Program (CEIP) collects product usage and diagnostic data from VCF Operations and sends it to the vendor for product improvement. The data describes how the product is configured and operated; it is not customer workload data. Organizations that restrict outbound data from the management environment, or that operate the management plane in an isolated network, disable CEIP so VCF Operations does not originate that traffic. This is a data-handling policy choice that reduces outbound communication from the management plane rather than a control that changes the security state of the product.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling CEIP removes a feedback channel to the vendor for product improvement data; it does not affect VCF Operations function or the supportability of unrelated cases. Leaving it enabled continues that outbound reporting.
Setting Location: UI: Operate > Global Settings > System Settings > Customer Experience Improvement Program

Description/Title: VCF Operations must activate certificate validation.
Discussion:
VCF Operations connects to many endpoints across the environment (vCenter, ESX, NSX, data sources, integrations), and certificate validation is what lets it confirm that the system it is talking to is the genuine one rather than an impostor. Validating a certificate means checking its certification path: that it chains to a trusted certificate authority, is within its validity period, and has not been revoked (via certificate revocation lists or OCSP).

With certificate validation off, VCF Operations accepts whatever certificate an endpoint presents, including a forged or self-signed certificate from an attacker positioned between VCF Operations and the endpoint (a man-in-the-middle). That attacker could then intercept or alter the data on those connections. Turning validation on closes that path, at the cost of requiring the endpoints VCF Operations connects to present trusted, valid certificates.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With certificate validation enabled, VCF Operations refuses connections to endpoints presenting untrusted, expired, or revoked certificates, so the endpoints it integrates with must have valid certificates from a trusted CA. With validation disabled, those connections succeed against any certificate, including a forged one, leaving the integration traffic open to interception by a man-in-the-middle.
Setting Location: UI: Operate > Global Settings > System Settings

Description/Title: VCF Operations must limit the number of concurrent sessions.
Discussion:
VCF Operations can either allow a user to hold multiple simultaneous UI sessions or limit each user to a single active session. Limiting each account to one session means a credential cannot be used in two places at once: a second simultaneous login with the same account is not allowed, so an attacker using a stolen credential cannot quietly run a session alongside the legitimate user. It also bounds the UI resources a single account can consume.

Deactivate the "Concurrent UI login sessions" option so each user is held to a single active UI session.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With concurrent sessions disabled, each user account is limited to one active UI session; a user (or automation) that legitimately needs to be logged in from two places at once will be blocked from the second session. Leaving concurrent sessions enabled allows a single account to hold many simultaneous sessions, which lets a stolen credential be used alongside the legitimate user without contention and removes a signal of credential misuse.
Baseline Suggested Value: Concurrent UI login sessions deactivated (single session per user)
Setting Location: UI: Operate > Global Settings > User Access

Description/Title: VCF Operations must enable credential ownership enforcement.
Discussion:
VCF Operations stores credentials for the adapters it uses to integrate with other products (vCenter, storage, third-party data sources). These stored credentials grant access to the integrated systems, so they are sensitive. Credential ownership enforcement restricts each stored credential to the user who created it, so other VCF Operations users cannot select, reuse, or act through someone else's stored credential.

Without ownership enforcement, any user who can configure adapters could use another user's stored credentials to reach the systems those credentials authenticate to, which is a privilege-escalation and lateral-movement path. Enabling enforcement keeps each credential bound to its owner.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With ownership enforcement enabled, a stored adapter credential can only be used by the user who created it; workflows that relied on sharing a credential across operators will need each operator to hold their own, or to use a shared service account by design. Without enforcement, any user with adapter-configuration access can act through credentials another user stored, reaching the integrated systems those credentials authenticate to.
Setting Location: UI: Operate > Global Settings > System Settings

Description/Title: VCF Operations must enable firewall hardening.
Discussion: A VCF Operations cluster uses some network ports only for inter-node communication, but by default those ports accept incoming connections from any source. Firewall hardening restricts those inter-node ports so they accept connections only from other members of the Operations cluster, removing the rest of the network as a potential source of traffic to them.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With firewall hardening enabled, the cluster's inter-node ports reject connections from outside the cluster, narrowing the attack surface to cluster members only. Leaving it off keeps those ports reachable from any source on the network, where they can be probed or attacked by anything that can route to a cluster node.
Setting Location: UI: Admin portal (/admin/) > Administrator Settings > Security Settings > Activate Firewall Hardening

Description/Title: Disable VCF Operations in-page feedback.
Discussion: The in-page feedback setting controls whether VCF Operations presents a way to submit feedback to the vendor from within the interface. When enabled, an administrator can send comments and the submission goes to the vendor. This is outbound communication originating from the management environment, which some organizations restrict by policy. Disabling it removes the in-product feedback path. This is a data-handling and interface policy choice rather than a control that changes the security state of VCF Operations.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling in-page feedback removes the feedback submission option from the VCF Operations interface; it does not affect operation or administration. Leaving it enabled keeps the in-product path for sending feedback to the vendor.
Setting Location: UI: Operate > Global Settings > System Settings > In Page Feedback

Description/Title: Configure VCF Operations log collection for all deployed components.
Discussion:
VCF Operations centralizes log collection for the VCF environment: deployed components send their logs to VCF Operations, which retains and indexes them for search and correlation. VCF Operations also retains its own logs in the same platform, so they are captured without a separate forwarding step. This control covers enabling log collection for every deployed component so no source is left uncollected. vCenter and ESX are the exception -- they forward their logs directly to a central destination under their own log-forwarding controls -- so this umbrella covers the remaining components (NSX, VCF Operations for Networks, SDDC Manager, VCF Automation, HCX, and others present in the environment).

A central log destination gives two security benefits: the collected copy survives the loss or compromise of any single source component, since it is independent of the source; and it lets an operator correlate events across components during incident response without pulling data from each source individually. A component left out of collection keeps its only log record on the source, where an attacker who compromises that component can alter or clear it before it is seen elsewhere.

Enabling per-component collection has a prerequisite: each VCF instance must first be registered and enabled for collection in VCF Operations (Integrations > Accounts -- expand the VMware Cloud Foundation section and enable each instance and its workload domains). Until an instance is enabled there, the component log-collection toggles for it are inert and no logs are gathered, so register the instances and enable collection together.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With log collection configured for all deployed components, their log records survive source compromise and operators can correlate activity across the environment during an investigation. Leaving a component out of collection means its logs stay only on the source, where they can be altered or cleared before anyone reviews them, and no consolidated record exists for that component.
Baseline Suggested Value: Log collection enabled for all deployed components
Setting Location: UI: Administration > Configurations > Log Management > Log Collection

Description/Title: Configure the VCF Operations log retention period.
Discussion:
VCF Operations retains collected logs for a configurable period before aging them out to reclaim partition space. The retention period should be set to meet the organization's audit and forensic requirements: long enough that the log record covers the window an investigation is likely to need, but bounded so the log partition does not fill and force ingestion to pause or logs to age out faster than expected.

A retention period shorter than the required window means the log record an investigation depends on may already have been aged out by the time it is needed. One set too long for the available partition risks storage exhaustion, which can pause ingestion or drop the oldest data without warning. Size the partition and retention together against the expected ingest rate.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Setting retention to meet the audit requirement keeps the log record available for the period investigations need. Too short a period ages out logs before they are needed; too long for the partition risks filling it, which pauses ingestion or drops the oldest data.
Installation Default Value: Default partition allocation (not aligned to a specific retention requirement)
Baseline Suggested Value: Retention period configured to meet the organization's audit retention requirement
Setting Location: UI: Administration > Configurations > Log Processing > Log Partition

Description/Title: Configure the VCF Operations login banner.
Discussion:
A login banner displayed on the authentication page tells anyone reaching the login screen the conditions of system use, the consent-to-monitoring stance, and the consequences of unauthorized access. It is not a direct security control: a banner does not prevent an attacker from attempting to authenticate. Its value is procedural; the banner sets the conditions an authorized user agrees to by continuing, and the disclosure of monitoring supports administrative and legal follow-up against unauthorized access.

The banner text should be supplied by the organization's legal advisors; an example wording is included in the Baseline Suggested Value field as a starting point.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With a banner configured, anyone reaching the VCF Operations login page sees the organization's notice and consent text before authenticating. Leaving the banner unconfigured does not change the system's authentication behavior; users authenticate the same way, but without the notice that supports administrative or legal action against unauthorized use.
Baseline Suggested Value:
Consult your organization's legal advisors for text that is applicable to your environment.

An example is: "Authorized users only. Actual or attempted unauthorized use of this system is prohibited and may result in criminal, civil, security, or administrative proceedings and/or penalties. Use of this information system indicates consent to monitoring and recording, without notice or permission. Users have no expectation of privacy in any use of this system. Any information stored on, or transiting this system, or obtained by monitoring and/or recording, may be disclosed to law enforcement and/or used in accordance with Federal law, State statute, and organization policy. If you are not an authorized user of this system, exit the system at this time."
Setting Location: UI: Operate > Global Settings > User Access > Login Message

Description/Title: Require authenticated log ingestion in VCF Operations.
Discussion:
VCF Operations accepts forwarded logs over syslog and its ingestion API. The enforced authenticated log ingestion setting requires log sources to authenticate before their data is accepted, so a host on the network cannot inject fabricated or altered log entries into the central store simply by sending to the ingestion endpoint. The setting is off by default, which accepts log data without verifying the sender.

Unauthenticated ingestion lets any reachable system submit entries that then sit alongside genuine records, which an attacker can use to bury real activity in noise, plant misleading entries, or flood the pipeline. Requiring authentication ties accepted log data to known, credentialed sources.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Requiring authenticated ingestion means log forwarders must present valid credentials before their data is accepted; a source not configured to authenticate stops being ingested until it is updated. Leaving it off accepts log data from any reachable sender, including fabricated entries.
Setting Location: UI: Operate > Global Settings > System Settings > Enforced Authenticated Log Ingestion

Description/Title: Enable SSL for API log ingestion in VCF Operations.
Discussion:
VCF Operations can also receive logs through its ingestion API. The SSL API Ingestion setting encrypts that path so log data submitted by API clients is protected in transit. The setting is enabled by default; this control confirms it has not been turned off.

Disabling SSL on the API ingestion path would let submitted log data and the requests carrying it cross the network in cleartext, exposing them to capture or modification. Because the secure state is the default, the control is an audit that the default has been preserved.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Keeping SSL API Ingestion enabled protects log data submitted over the API in transit. Disabling it allows cleartext API ingestion, exposing log contents and requests to capture or modification on the network path.
Setting Location: UI: Operate > Global Settings > System Settings > SSL API Ingestion

Description/Title: Enable SSL for syslog log ingestion in VCF Operations.
Discussion:
VCF Operations can receive logs over syslog. The SSL Syslog setting encrypts that ingestion path so log data sent by forwarders is protected in transit from interception or modification by anyone on the network path. The setting is enabled by default; this control confirms it has not been turned off.

Disabling SSL on the syslog path would let forwarded log data cross the network in cleartext, exposing it to passive capture and to tampering before it reaches the collector. Because the secure state is the default, the control is an audit that the default has been preserved.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Keeping SSL Syslog enabled protects forwarded log data in transit and matches what TLS-capable forwarders expect. Disabling it sends syslog ingestion in cleartext, exposing log contents to capture or modification on the network path.
Setting Location: UI: Operate > Global Settings > System Settings > SSL Syslog

Description/Title: Restrict VCF Operations access to imported identity-provider users.
Discussion:
When VCF Operations is integrated with an external identity provider, the non-imported user-access setting governs whether identity-provider users who have not been explicitly imported into VCF Operations can still authenticate and gain access. Left enabled, any user the identity provider can authenticate may reach VCF Operations even if an administrator never provisioned them with a role. Restricting access to imported users enforces explicit provisioning: a user gains access only after an administrator has added them and assigned a role, keeping the set of people who can reach the management plane aligned with the set an administrator has deliberately authorized.

This applies only where an external identity provider is integrated; it has no effect on locally defined accounts.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Restricting to imported users means a federated identity-provider user must be explicitly added and assigned a role before they can log in; until then they are denied. Leaving non-imported access enabled lets any identity-provider-authenticated user reach VCF Operations without an administrator having provisioned them.
Setting Location: UI: Operate > Global Settings > User Access

Description/Title: Enforce password complexity on VCF Operations local accounts.
Discussion:
VCF Operations uses a local user store for administrative accounts that authenticate directly against the appliance, alongside any external identity provider the operator has configured. The password complexity policy on those local accounts determines what passwords the system accepts when an operator sets or rotates one. The recommended policy requires a 15-character minimum and at least one uppercase, lowercase, numeric, and special character.

Length is the dominant factor in resisting guessing and brute-force attempts, where an attacker systematically tries many candidate passwords; the character-class floor adds basic variety without forcing the long lists of composition rules that push users toward predictable substitutions. This policy is set in the appliance's global password policy and applies to local accounts. Do not require rotation in the absence of evidence of compromise.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Weakening the policy below the standard lets operators set short or trivially guessable passwords that a brute-force attack would succeed against quickly. Keeping it at the standard does not otherwise affect operation; operators choose passwords that meet the minimum length and include each required class.
Installation Default Value: Minimum length 15; at least 1 uppercase, 1 lowercase, 1 numeric, and 1 special character
Baseline Suggested Value: Minimum length 15; at least 1 uppercase, 1 lowercase, 1 numeric, and 1 special character
Setting Location: UI: Operate > Global Settings > User Access > Password Policy

Description/Title: When VCF Operations runs as a multi-node cluster, DRS anti-affinity rules must separate the nodes across hosts.
Discussion:
When VCF Operations is deployed as a multi-node cluster for high availability or redundancy, DRS anti-affinity rules keep the cluster member appliances on different ESX hosts, so a single host failure cannot take down more than one cluster member at once. Without anti-affinity, DRS could place multiple cluster members on the same host, where one host failure removes the redundancy the cluster was meant to provide.

Use DRS anti-affinity "should" rules rather than "must" rules, so DRS and Lifecycle Manager can still relocate the appliances during patching or HA events. Not applicable if VCF Operations runs as a single node rather than a cluster.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With anti-affinity rules in place, a single ESX host failure can take at most one VCF Operations cluster member offline, preserving the analytics and management service. Without them, DRS may co-locate cluster members on one host, so a single host failure can remove multiple members and defeat the cluster's redundancy. The control does not apply to single-node deployments.

Description/Title: VCF Operations must enforce an idle session timeout.
Discussion:
Session timeouts limit how long an authenticated session in VCF Operations can remain idle before the user is forced to re-authenticate. An unattended session that never expires is a credential exposure: anyone with physical access to the operator's workstation, or anyone who finds an unlocked terminal, can use the session as the original user with no further authentication challenge.

A reasonable inactivity timeout balances operator convenience against this exposure: long enough that operators don't have to log in repeatedly during a working session, short enough that an unattended terminal does not stay authenticated overnight or across the operator's absence.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With an inactivity timeout enforced, an unattended VCF Operations session ends after the configured idle interval and the user must re-authenticate to continue. Without a configured timeout, sessions remain authenticated as long as the browser tab or API client is alive, which leaves the credential usable by anyone who reaches the operator's workstation while the session is still open.
Setting Location: UI: Operate > Global Settings > System Settings > Session Inactivity Timeout

Description/Title: VCF Operations must disable unsigned management pack installation.
Discussion:
VCF Operations is extended with management packs (PAK files) that add adapters, dashboards, and integrations. Signature verification checks that a management pack was signed by a recognized, approved publisher before it is installed, so that only vetted code and content is added to the platform. The "Allow unsigned PAK installation" option bypasses that check.

With unsigned PAK installation allowed, an operator (or an attacker who reaches the admin portal) can install a management pack that has not been signed or vetted, introducing untrusted code into VCF Operations with the platform's privileges. Keep unsigned PAK installation disabled so every management pack must carry a valid signature.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With unsigned PAK installation disabled, only management packs signed by an approved publisher can be installed; an unsigned or third-party pack that is not signed will be refused until it is properly signed. Allowing unsigned installation opens a path to load untrusted code into VCF Operations, which runs with broad access to the environment it monitors.

Description/Title: The VCF Protection and Recovery appliance must forward its logs to a central log server.
Discussion:
The Protection and Recovery appliance writes system and audit logs locally. Configuring syslog forwarding in the appliance management interface sends those logs to one or more central log servers, so the appliance's audit trail is retained off the appliance.

Logs kept only on the appliance are lost if it is destroyed, rebuilt, or compromised by an attacker who clears them, and the appliance has limited local log storage. Forwarding to a central collector keeps a durable copy that can be retained per policy and correlated with logs from other components. The fleet-wide VCF settings do not configure this appliance's syslog forwarding, so set it on the appliance directly. Use a secure transport (TLS) to the log server where the collector supports it.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With the appliance forwarding its logs to a central server, its audit trail survives an appliance loss, rebuild, or compromise that clears local logs, and can be retained and correlated centrally. Leaving forwarding unconfigured confines the logs to the appliance, where they are subject to local storage limits and lost with the appliance.
Setting Location: UI: Protection and Recovery Appliance Management > Syslog Forwarding

Description/Title: The VCF Protection and Recovery appliance SSH service must be deactivated except for temporary diagnostic use.
Discussion:
The Protection and Recovery appliance runs an SSH daemon that gives administrators an interactive shell on the appliance. Anyone who reaches the SSH listener with valid credentials gets shell access to the appliance, including its configuration and stored secrets. SSH is intended as a break-glass tool for diagnostics and recovery when the appliance management interface is not enough; it is not the routine management interface.

Disable SSH login so the appliance does not present an interactive shell listener during normal operation. An operator who needs SSH for a specific task enables it for the duration of that task and disables it again afterward.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Keeping SSH login disabled has no day-to-day operational cost; the appliance is managed through its management interface. Leaving SSH login enabled keeps an interactive shell listener on the appliance at all times, which is the surface the recommendation removes.
Setting Location: UI: Protection and Recovery Appliance Management > Access > SSH

Description/Title: The VCF Protection and Recovery appliance must synchronize its clock with organizational reference sources.
Discussion:
Accurate time on the Protection and Recovery appliance underpins certificate validity checks, the ordering of its audit and event logs, time-bounded authentication, and incident response that correlates events across systems. If the appliance clock drifts outside a certificate validity window, TLS handshakes fail, and its logs can no longer be aligned with logs from other components during an investigation.

Set the appliance's time-synchronization mode to NTP rather than disabling synchronization or following the underlying host, so it tracks a known reference rather than no source or a host whose own clock may be wrong. Point it at your organization's authoritative time sources and set the time zone to the organization's chosen value. Use one, or three or more, time sources rather than exactly two, so the appliance is never left arbitrating between two clocks that disagree.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: An appliance with an inaccurate or unsynchronized clock fails operations that depend on time, such as TLS handshakes against certificates outside the validity window, and its audit logs cannot be aligned with other systems, which weakens incident investigation. Synchronizing to authoritative NTP sources has no workload impact.
Baseline Suggested Value: Site-Specific (NTP mode; authoritative internal time servers; organizational time zone)
Setting Location: UI: Protection and Recovery Appliance Management > Time > Time Synchronization

Description/Title: SDDC Manager must configure the API admin account.
Discussion:
SDDC Manager has a local "admin" account used to reach the VCF APIs when the management vCenter is unavailable and SSO-based access cannot be used. It is a break-glass account for recovery scenarios. The VCF installer sets a strong password on this account during deployment, so fresh installations have it configured. On environments upgraded from an earlier release, or deployed via the API without setting it, the account's password can be left unset, which leaves the account unusable for the recovery cases it exists for.

Confirm the API admin account has a strong password set, particularly after an upgrade, and store the credential where the recovery team can reach it out-of-band, since the situations that call for it are exactly those where normal management access is down.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With the API admin password set (which the VCF installer does by default), the account is available for API-based recovery when vCenter or SSO is down; if it has been left unset on an upgraded or API-deployed environment, that recovery path is unavailable until a password is set. The credential is highly privileged, so protect it and store it securely out-of-band.
Configuration Parameter:
Set a strong password on the local admin account via the SDDC Manager API:

curl 'https://<sddc-manager>/v1/users/local/admin' \
  -i -k -X PATCH \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  --data '{"password": "<new-strong-password>"}'

Confirm the exact request-body schema in Developer Center > API Explorer > Users.
Installation Default Value: Configured (strong password set by the VCF installer)
Baseline Suggested Value: Strong password set on the API admin account
Setting Location: API: SDDC Manager API (no UI toggle; obtain a bearer token from SDDC Manager > Developer Center > API Explorer > Tokens > POST first)

Description/Title: Disable HTTP Basic Authentication on the SDDC Manager API.
Discussion: HTTP Basic Authentication transmits credentials as a base64-encoded string in the Authorization header, which is trivially decoded once captured. Even when the session is wrapped in TLS, any logging or proxy that records the header exposes the credentials. SDDC Manager supports bearer-token authentication for API access; basic authentication should be disabled in favor of that.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Existing automation that uses basic authentication against the SDDC Manager API will start failing. Identify and re-tool such consumers to use bearer tokens before disabling.
Configuration Parameter:
Disable via the SDDC Manager API:

curl 'https://<sddc-manager>/v1/sddc-manager' \
  -i -k -X PATCH \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
