  --data '{"basicAuthSpec": {"status": "DISABLE"}}'

Verify by GET on the same URL; "basicAuthDetails.status" should report "DISABLED".
Setting Location: API: SDDC Manager API (no UI toggle; obtain a bearer token from SDDC Manager > Developer Center > API Explorer > Tokens > POST first)

Description/Title: Set the vCenter account auto-unlock interval.
Discussion: After an account is locked by repeated failed logins, the auto-unlock interval (Unlock time) controls how long it stays locked before vCenter SSO unlocks it automatically. Never unlocking forces an administrator to clear every locked account by hand; while that stops an automated guessing attack from retrying, it also hands an attacker an easy denial-of-service: deliberately failing logins against many accounts locks them all until manual intervention. An auto-unlock interval lets locked accounts recover on their own after a delay, which limits both brute-force retries and the lockout-as-denial-of-service risk. The default interval balances these.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With an interval set, a locked account becomes usable again after it elapses without administrator action, so a burst of failed logins causes only a temporary lockout. Setting it to never-unlock turns repeated failed logins into a denial-of-service against the affected accounts until an administrator intervenes.

Description/Title: Lock vCenter accounts after repeated failed login attempts.
Discussion: vCenter SSO locks a local account after a set number of consecutive failed logins, limiting how many guesses an attacker can make before the account becomes unavailable, which slows brute-force attempts where an attacker systematically tries many candidate passwords. A small but non-zero threshold catches guessing without locking users out over a few honest typos. The default meets this and satisfies many regulatory minimums.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With the threshold set, an account locks after the configured number of consecutive failures, briefly interrupting a user who mistypes repeatedly. Setting it too low locks users out on normal typos; disabling it removes a key brake on password guessing.
Configuration Parameter: Maximum number of failed login attempts

Description/Title: Set the vCenter failed-login counting interval.
Discussion: The failed-login counting interval (Time interval between failures) is the window over which vCenter SSO counts consecutive failures toward the lockout threshold. A longer window means failures are remembered longer, so an attacker cannot reset the count by spacing guesses out, which strengthens the lockout against slow brute-force attempts where an attacker systematically tries many candidate passwords. The recommendation lengthens the default window.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Lengthening the interval makes the lockout threshold harder to evade by pacing guesses; it does not affect normal use. Too short an interval lets an attacker avoid lockout by pausing between attempts.

Description/Title: Reduce or eliminate third-party vCenter plugins.
Discussion:
Third-party plugins extend vCenter and the vSphere Client, and they run with access to vCenter and, for UI plugins, within the vSphere Client's privileged context. A plugin is therefore a trust relationship: a malicious or vulnerable plugin, or a compromise of the third-party system a plugin connects to, becomes a path into vCenter and a way for an attacker to move laterally between systems. Tightly coupling other products to vCenter through plugins also tends to impede timely patching and upgrades, because the plugin and the coupled product must stay compatible.

Inventory the installed plugins and keep only those that add enough value to justify the trust and coupling they introduce; prefer a product's own management console over a deep vCenter plugin where that is a reasonable alternative. Remove plugins that are unused, unmaintained, or no longer needed.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Removing an unused or unnecessary plugin has no effect on the workflows that did not rely on it, and narrows the set of third-party code that runs with vCenter access. Removing a plugin that an active workflow depends on breaks that integration, so confirm a plugin is genuinely unneeded before removing it; keeping unnecessary plugins enlarges the vCenter attack surface and the patch-coordination burden.
Setting Location: UI: vCenter > Administration > Solutions > Client Plugins

Description/Title: VMware vCenter must separate authentication and authorization for administrators.
Discussion:
Many organizations use a single central directory (such as Active Directory) for both authenticating administrators and authorizing them. When the same source does both, an attacker who compromises that directory can add themselves to the group that grants vCenter administration and simply log in; the directory's own administrators also implicitly hold vCenter administration. Separating the two means an administrator authenticates against the directory but their vCenter authorization comes from a separate source that the directory's compromise does not control.

Use local VCF SSO groups to grant vCenter administrative authorization, rather than mapping vCenter admin roles directly onto Active Directory groups. An administrator still authenticates with their directory identity, but membership in the local SSO group that confers vCenter privileges is managed in vCenter SSO, so an AD compromise alone does not grant vCenter administration.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With administrative authorization granted through local VCF SSO groups, provisioning or changing a vCenter administrator's access is done in vCenter SSO rather than purely in the directory, which is a small additional step (automatable with PowerCLI). The benefit is that compromising the directory no longer directly yields vCenter administration; an attacker would also have to alter the local SSO group membership.
Baseline Suggested Value: Administrator authorization granted via local VCF SSO groups; directory (e.g., AD) used for authentication only

Description/Title: VMware vCenter must restrict membership of the SystemConfiguration.BashShellAdministrators SSO group.
Discussion:
vCenter VCF SSO integrates with PAM in the underlying Photon operating system so members of the "SystemConfiguration.BashShellAdministrators" VCF SSO group can log on to the operating system without needing a separate account. However, even though unique VCF SSO users log on, they are transparently using a group account named "sso-user" as far as Photon auditing is concerned. While the audit trail can still be traced back to the individual VCF SSO user, it is a more involved process.

To force accountability and nonrepudiation, the VCF SSO group "SystemConfiguration.BashShellAdministrators" must be severely restricted.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Restricting the BashShellAdministrators group to the minimum set of administrators who genuinely need vCenter OS shell access limits how many identities can reach the appliance shell, and keeps the (shared sso-user) shell audit trail short enough to trace. Over-populating the group, for example by leaving the SSO Administrators group as a member, grants OS shell access to everyone in it, expanding the highly privileged shell-access surface and muddying accountability.
Baseline Suggested Value: Membership restricted to the minimum set of administrators who require OS shell access
Setting Location: UI: Administration >> Single Sign On >> Users and Groups >> Groups

Description/Title: Disable the vCenter Customer Experience Improvement Program.
Discussion: The Customer Experience Improvement Program (CEIP) collects product usage and diagnostic data from vCenter and sends it to the vendor for product improvement. The data describes how the product is configured and operated; it is not customer workload data. Organizations that restrict outbound data from the management environment, or that operate the management plane in an isolated network, disable CEIP so vCenter does not originate that traffic. This is a data-handling policy choice that reduces outbound communication from the management plane rather than a control that changes the security state of vCenter.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling CEIP removes a feedback channel to the vendor for product improvement data; it does not affect vCenter function or the supportability of unrelated cases. Leaving it enabled continues that outbound reporting.
Setting Location: UI: Administration > Deployment > Customer Experience Improvement Program

Description/Title: Disable vSphere Client in-product feedback.
Discussion: The in-product feedback setting controls whether the vSphere Client offers a way to submit feedback to the vendor from within the interface. When enabled, an administrator can send comments and the submission goes to the vendor. Like UI telemetry, this is outbound communication originating from the management environment, which some organizations restrict by policy. Disabling it removes the in-client feedback path. This is a data-handling and interface policy choice rather than a control that changes the security state of vCenter.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling in-product feedback removes the feedback submission option from the vSphere Client; it does not affect vCenter operation or administration. Leaving it enabled keeps the in-client path for sending feedback to the vendor.

Description/Title: Disable vSphere Client UI telemetry.
Discussion: The UI telemetry setting controls whether the vSphere Client collects data about how the interface is used and sends it to the vendor. The data describes administrator interaction with the client, such as which screens and actions are exercised; it is not workload or configuration data. Organizations that restrict outbound data from the management environment, or that run the management plane in an isolated network, may turn this off so the vSphere Client does not originate that traffic. This is a data-handling policy choice rather than a control that changes the security state of vCenter.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling UI telemetry stops the vSphere Client from sending interface usage data; it does not affect vCenter operation, workloads, or administrator workflows. Leaving it enabled continues that outbound usage reporting.

Description/Title: VMware vCenter must require authentication for published content libraries.
Discussion:
A vCenter content library stores and distributes templates, ISO images, OVF/OVA packages, and other deployment content; a published library makes that content available for other vCenter instances (or other consumers) to subscribe to and download. Without authentication on a published library, anyone who can reach the library's publish URL can subscribe to it and pull down all of its content.

Requiring authentication on a published content library limits subscribers to those who present valid credentials, so the templates and images (which can embed configuration, scripts, or sensitive data, and serve as a workload-deployment vector) are not exposed to unauthenticated download. Enable authentication on any content library that is published.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With authentication required, subscribers to the published library must supply the configured credentials, so existing subscriptions must be updated with those credentials and new subscribers must be given them. Leaving a published library unauthenticated lets anyone who can reach its publish URL download its full contents, including any sensitive material embedded in the templates or images.
Configuration Parameter: Enable user authentication for access to this content library

Description/Title: VMware vCenter must enable the OVF security policy on content libraries.
Discussion:
A vCenter content library can store and distribute OVF and OVA templates used to deploy workloads. The OVF security policy, when applied to a content library, enforces strict validation of OVF/OVA items: on import, deployment, update, and synchronization, the item must be signed by a certificate from a trusted CA that the operator has added. Without the policy, the library accepts and deploys OVF/OVA templates without verifying their signature.

Applying the OVF security policy means a tampered or untrusted template cannot be silently imported or deployed from the library; an attacker who substitutes or modifies a template would also have to present a signature from a trusted CA. Apply the policy on content libraries used to hold OVF/OVA deployment content, and add the signing certificate(s) for the templates in use.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With the OVF security policy applied, only OVF/OVA templates signed by a trusted CA can be imported or deployed from the library; unsigned or untrusted-signed templates are rejected, so legitimate templates must be signed and their signing certificate added to the library first. Without the policy, the library deploys OVF/OVA content without signature validation, allowing a tampered or malicious template to be deployed as a workload.

Description/Title: VMware vCenter must have unnecessary or unused user accounts disabled or removed.
Discussion:
Disabling expired, inactive, unused, or otherwise anomalous accounts supports the concepts of least privilege and least functionality which reduce the attack surface of the system.

VMware vCenter has "K/M" and "krbtgt" accounts that, if IWA is not in use, can be disabled.

This control does NOT apply to internal product appliance accounts (Linux user accounts often found in /etc/passwd). Those accounts are part of the product distribution and used for process isolation within the product. Those accounts are already disabled by having their shell set to /bin/nologin, /bin/false, or having "!!" in the passwd field. Removal or alteration of appliance internal accounts is unsupported and will cause operational issues, often later with upgrades and patching.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling the unused K/M and krbtgt accounts (when Integrated Windows Authentication is not in use) removes credentials that nothing in the environment relies on, with no effect on normal operation. Do not disable or remove the appliance's internal Linux accounts; they are already non-interactive (nologin/false shells) and altering them is unsupported and breaks upgrades and patching.
Setting Location: UI: Administration >> Single Sign On >> Users and Groups >> Users.

Description/Title: VMware vCenter must enable the Distributed Resource Scheduler for resilience and to enable automated patching.
Discussion:
The Distributed Resource Scheduler (DRS) automatically balances virtual machine workloads across the ESX hosts in a cluster, and migrates VMs off hosts as needed for maintenance, patching, and High Availability restarts. Availability is a core security property: DRS keeps workloads running and lets the cluster absorb host failures and planned maintenance without manual workload juggling or downtime.

Enable DRS on clusters so the platform can rebalance automatically and perform non-disruptive maintenance and patching. Use "fully automated" mode for hands-off balancing, or a more conservative mode where operators want to review recommendations first.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With DRS enabled, the cluster rebalances workloads automatically and can evacuate hosts for patching, maintenance, and HA recovery without manual intervention, which keeps workloads available during those operations. Without DRS, hosts must be balanced and evacuated by hand, which slows maintenance and patching and increases the chance that a host failure or maintenance window causes resource contention or downtime.

Description/Title: Configure the vCenter SSH login banner (/etc/issue).
Discussion:
The /etc/issue text is displayed when a client connects to vCenter over SSH. It serves two purposes here. First, the installed default banner includes the vCenter product and version string, which discloses build information to anyone who reaches the SSH port; replacing it removes that version disclosure. Second, a consent/notice banner sets out the conditions of use and the consent-to-monitoring stance for anyone connecting.

A login banner is not a direct security control: it does not stop an attacker from connecting or authenticating. Its value is removing the default version disclosure and providing the procedural notice that supports administrative and legal follow-up against unauthorized access. The banner text should be supplied by the organization's legal advisors; an example is in the Baseline Suggested Value field.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With the default banner replaced, the vCenter version string is no longer disclosed at the SSH prompt and anyone connecting sees the organization's notice and consent text. Replacing it does not change SSH authentication behavior; it removes an information-disclosure detail and adds the procedural notice.
Installation Default Value: "VMware vCenter Server 8.0.2.00000Type: vCenter Server with an embedded Platform Services Controller"
Baseline Suggested Value:
Consult your organization's legal advisors for text that is applicable to your environment.

An example is: "Authorized users only. Actual or attempted unauthorized use of this system is prohibited and may result in criminal, civil, security, or administrative proceedings and/or penalties. Use of this information system indicates consent to monitoring and recording, without notice or permission. Users have no expectation of privacy in any use of this system. Any information stored on, or transiting this system, or obtained by monitoring and/or recording, may be disclosed to law enforcement and/or used in accordance with Federal law, State statute, and organization policy. If you are not an authorized user of this system, exit the system at this time."
PowerCLI Command Remediation: Get-AdvancedSetting -Entity $VC -Name etc.issue | Set-AdvancedSetting -Value "****************************************************************************`n* Authorized users only. Actual or attempted unauthorized use of this      *`n* system is prohibited and may result in criminal, civil, security, or     *`n* administrative proceedings and/or penalties. Use of this information     *`n* system indicates consent to monitoring and recording, without notice     *`n* or permission. Users have no expectation of privacy. Any information     *`n* stored on or transiting this system, or obtained by monitoring and/or    *`n* recording, may be disclosed to law enforcement and/or used in accordance *`n* with Federal law, State statute, and organization policy. If you are not *`n* an authorized user of this system, exit the system at this time.         *`n****************************************************************************`n"

Description/Title: VMware vCenter must retain task and event data for an appropriate interval.
Discussion:
vCenter retains task and event records (the record of what was done in the environment and when) in its database, aging them out after a configurable number of days to bound database growth. This retention is local to the vCenter appliance; it provides a recent-history window in the vSphere Client, not the durable audit archive.

For long-term audit retention, forward vCenter events to a central log collector (see the log-forwarding guidance), where they are kept according to the organization's retention policy independent of the local database. The local retention interval should be long enough to support routine troubleshooting and recent-incident review in the UI without growing the database excessively; the product default is a reasonable balance for most environments.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: At the default retention, the vCenter database keeps a recent window of task and event history for in-UI troubleshooting and review without growing unbounded. Shortening it loses recent history sooner (relevant only for the local copy, since durable retention should be handled by log forwarding); lengthening it grows the database and can affect vCenter performance and backup size.
Setting Location: UI: vCenter > Configure > Settings > General > Database

Description/Title: Enable remote logging of vCenter events.
Discussion:
The vpxd.event.syslog.enabled setting controls whether vCenter's task and event records are emitted to the host syslog stream. When enabled, the vCenter audit trail (the record of what was done in vCenter and by whom) is included in the syslog output that is forwarded to a central log collector, rather than living only in the local vCenter database where it ages out per the configured retention (see vcenter-9.events-database-retention).

Routing events to syslog is what makes the vCenter audit trail durable and centrally analyzable: it survives the local database's retention window and a loss or compromise of the vCenter appliance, and it can be correlated with logs from other components. This setting works together with the log-forwarding configuration (vcenter-9.log-forwarding) that ships the syslog stream to the collector.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With event-to-syslog enabled (the default), vCenter task and event records flow into the forwarded log stream and reach the central collector, where they are retained and correlated. Disabling it confines the vCenter audit trail to the local database, where it is subject to retention aging and is lost if the appliance is destroyed or compromised.

Description/Title: VMware vCenter must forward its logs to a central log server.
Discussion:
vCenter writes system and audit logs locally on the appliance. Configuring syslog forwarding in the vCenter appliance management interface (VAMI) sends those logs to one or more central log servers, so the vCenter audit trail is retained off the appliance.

Logs kept only on vCenter are lost if the appliance is destroyed, rebuilt, or compromised by an attacker who clears them, and the appliance has limited local log storage. Forwarding to a central collector keeps a durable copy that can be retained per policy and correlated with logs from other components. Use a secure transport (TLS) to the log server where the collector supports it.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With vCenter forwarding its logs to a central server, the vCenter audit trail survives an appliance loss, rebuild, or compromise that clears local logs, and can be retained and correlated centrally. Leaving forwarding unconfigured confines the logs to the appliance, where they are subject to local storage limits and lost with the appliance.

Description/Title: Configure the vCenter login banner for the vSphere Client.
Discussion:
A login banner displayed on the vSphere Client authentication page tells anyone reaching the login screen the conditions of system use, the consent-to-monitoring stance, and the consequences of unauthorized access. It is not a direct security control: a banner does not stop an attacker from attempting to authenticate. Its value is procedural. The banner sets the conditions an authorized user agrees to by continuing, and the disclosure of monitoring supports administrative and legal follow-up when use is unauthorized.

Three settings in the Single Sign-On login message configuration work together: a toggle that turns the banner on, a short login message that titles it, and the detailed notice and consent text shown to the user. All three must be set for the banner to appear with the intended content. The banner text should be supplied by the organization's legal advisors; an example is in the Baseline Suggested Value field.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With the login banner configured, anyone reaching the vSphere Client login page sees the organization's notice and consent text before authenticating. Leaving it unconfigured does not change authentication behavior; users authenticate the same way, but without the notice that supports administrative or legal action against unauthorized use.
Configuration Parameter: "Show Login Message", "Login Message", and "Details of Login Message"
Baseline Suggested Value:
Consult your organization's legal advisors for text that is applicable to your environment.

An example is: "Authorized users only. Actual or attempted unauthorized use of this system is prohibited and may result in criminal, civil, security, or administrative proceedings and/or penalties. Use of this information system indicates consent to monitoring and recording, without notice or permission. Users have no expectation of privacy in any use of this system. Any information stored on, or transiting this system, or obtained by monitoring and/or recording, may be disclosed to law enforcement and/or used in accordance with Federal law, State statute, and organization policy. If you are not an authorized user of this system, exit the system at this time."
PowerCLI Command Assessment:
N/A (No public API available)

Can be configured using "/opt/vmware/bin/sso-config.sh -set_logon_banner -title <banner title> <banner file>" from an appliance shell. Please deactivate the shell again when you are done.
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
N/A (No public API available)

Can be configured using "/opt/vmware/bin/sso-config.sh -set_logon_banner -title <banner title> <banner file>" from an appliance shell. Please deactivate the shell again when you are done.

Description/Title: Configure the vCenter logging level to capture security-relevant events.
Discussion:
The config.log.level advanced setting controls how much detail vCenter records in its own operational logs, deciding whether routine informational events are written or only warnings and errors. These records are the audit trail a VCF administrator relies on to reconstruct what happened on the vCenter appliance and who did it.

A level that is too sparse omits the routine events needed to establish what occurred and in what order, which weakens incident investigation and event correlation. A level that is too verbose floods the log with low-value debug detail, raising storage use and making the events that matter harder to find. The default level records operationally and security-relevant events without that noise. This setting governs vCenter's own logging detail only; forwarding those logs to a central collector and retaining task and event records are handled by vcenter-9.log-forwarding and vcenter-9.events-remote-logging.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Lowering the level below the default suppresses informational events, leaving gaps in the record used to investigate operational and security incidents. Raising it to a debug or trace level sharply increases log volume and storage consumption and can bury relevant events, so it is appropriate only for short-term troubleshooting.

Description/Title: Protect Native Key Provider backups with a strong password.
Discussion:
The vCenter Native Key Provider lets vCenter act as the key manager for encryption features such as encrypted virtual machines and vSAN encryption, without deploying a separate external key management server (KMS). The keys it issues are what protect that encrypted data.

Enabling the Native Key Provider requires exporting a backup, a PKCS#12 file (a standard password-protected container for keys and certificates). That backup holds the key material needed to restore the provider, so it is as sensitive as the data those keys protect. If the backup is exported without a password, anyone who obtains the file can recover the keys and decrypt the virtual machines and other data they secure. Set a strong password when creating the backup, store it securely, and delete any earlier backups that were saved without one.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Setting a password means that password is then required to restore the Native Key Provider from the backup; if it is lost, the backup cannot be used for recovery, so retain it as carefully as other recovery credentials. A backup exported without a password can be used by anyone who obtains the file.
Setting Location: UI: vCenter > Configure > Security > Key Providers

Description/Title: Disable MAC learning on distributed switch port groups unless a workload requires it.
Discussion:
MAC learning lets a distributed switch forward traffic to a virtual NIC that presents more than one MAC address, which is needed in special cases such as nested virtualization (running ESX inside an ESX virtual machine). A MAC address is the hardware identifier a switch uses to decide which port a frame belongs to.

MAC learning also enables unknown unicast flooding. Without it, a frame whose destination MAC the switch has not learned is dropped; with it, that frame is copied to every port on the switch that also has MAC learning and flooding enabled. Sending frames to ports that should not receive them gives a workload on the same port group the chance to observe traffic not addressed to it, so the feature widens the path for traffic sniffing. Leave MAC learning disabled except on a port group serving a workload that specifically requires it, such as a nested-virtualization host.

vSAN File Services is another such workload: its enablement process turns on MAC learning for the dedicated distributed switch port group it uses, because its File Service nodes present more than one MAC address. Leave MAC learning enabled on that dedicated port group and keep it confined there. On an NSX-backed network, the equivalent MAC learning is enabled on the segment from the NSX side.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Workloads that legitimately require multiple MAC addresses on a vNIC, such as nested hypervisors or some load balancers, lose connectivity when MAC learning is disabled on their port group, so enable it only on those specific port groups. Leaving it enabled broadly exposes other workloads on the port group to flooded traffic they should not receive.
Setting Location: UI: Distributed Port Group > Configure > Settings > Policies
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
$VDPGview = Get-VDPortgroup -Name $VDPG | Get-View
$spec = New-Object VMware.Vim.DVPortgroupConfigSpec
$spec.DefaultPortConfig = New-Object VMware.Vim.VMwareDVSPortSetting
$spec.DefaultPortConfig.MacManagementPolicy = New-Object VMware.Vim.DVSMacManagementPolicy
$spec.DefaultPortConfig.MacManagementPolicy.MacLearningPolicy = New-Object VMware.Vim.DVSMacLearningPolicy
$spec.DefaultPortConfig.MacManagementPolicy.MacLearningPolicy.Enabled = $false
$spec.ConfigVersion = $VDPGview.Config.ConfigVersion
$VDPGview.ReconfigureDVPortgroup_Task($spec)

Description/Title: Enable Network I/O Control on distributed switches.
Discussion:
Network I/O Control (NIOC) is a quality-of-service feature on the vSphere distributed switch (vDS). It divides the bandwidth of the physical network adapters into resource pools for the traffic types that share them, such as management, vMotion, vSAN, and virtual machine traffic, and lets an administrator assign each pool shares, reservations, and limits. Shares set relative priority when the uplinks are congested, reservations guarantee a minimum, and limits cap a maximum.

A denial-of-service (DoS) condition is one where legitimate users cannot reach a resource because it is exhausted. On a shared uplink, a workload that floods the network, whether through a fault or an attack, can starve the management and vMotion traffic the platform depends on. With NIOC enabled, the switch arbitrates bandwidth by the configured priorities during contention, so essential traffic keeps a share of the link. NIOC is a scheduling feature; it consumes no dedicated capacity and only takes effect when the uplinks are saturated.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Enabling NIOC with its default shares has little day-to-day effect, since arbitration applies only when the uplinks are saturated. The risk is in the pool configuration: setting low limits or large reservations on a pool can starve other traffic, so size shares and reservations to the environment rather than constraining a pool aggressively.
Setting Location: UI: Distributed Switch > Configure > Settings > Properties

Description/Title: Reject forged transmits on distributed switches and port groups.
Discussion:
A virtual machine's virtual NIC has an assigned MAC address (the hardware identifier a switch uses to forward frames). The guest operating system can send frames with a different source MAC address, a "forged transmit", which lets the VM impersonate another device on the network. The Forged Transmits option on a distributed switch port group controls this: "Accept" allows it, while "Reject" makes the host compare each outbound frame's source MAC to the VM's assigned MAC and drop any frame where they do not match.

Rejecting forged transmits limits a compromised or malicious VM's ability to impersonate another host on the same Layer 2 segment, a technique used to intercept or redirect traffic. The distributed switch sets a default policy that its port groups inherit, and a port group can override it, so verify the setting at both levels.

One designed exception is the dedicated distributed switch port group that vSAN File Services uses: enabling File Services turns on Forged Transmits (Accept) for that port group as part of its enablement process, because the File Service nodes present more than one MAC address. Apply the reject policy to the other port groups and leave the vSAN File Services port group as the enablement configured it; rejecting forged transmits there breaks File Service traffic.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Some workloads, such as clustered applications and network devices or functions, rely on forged transmits as a normal part of their operation. If those workloads are present, create a separate distributed port group that allows forged transmits and connect only the authorized VMs to it. The default Reject setting has no effect on workloads that use their assigned MAC.
Setting Location: UI: Distributed Port Group > Configure > Settings > Policies
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
Get-VDSwitch -Name $VDS | Get-VDSecurityPolicy | Set-VDSecurityPolicy -ForgedTransmits $false
Get-VDPortgroup -Name $VDPG | Get-VDSecurityPolicy | Set-VDSecurityPolicy -ForgedTransmits $false

Description/Title: Reject guest MAC address changes on distributed switches and port groups.
Discussion:
Each virtual machine NIC has an initial MAC address assigned in its configuration and an effective MAC address the guest operating system actually uses. The guest can change its effective MAC at runtime. The MAC Address Changes policy on a distributed switch port group controls what the host does when the two differ: "Accept" lets the VM receive traffic addressed to the changed MAC, while "Reject" makes the host stop delivering inbound frames to the VM once its effective MAC no longer matches the initial MAC.

Rejecting MAC changes limits a VM's ability to receive traffic intended for another host by reassigning its MAC, a step in impersonation and man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks on the same Layer 2 segment, where an attacker positions themselves to intercept traffic between two parties. Rejecting MAC changes complements rejecting forged transmits: forged transmits governs impersonation on a VM's outbound frames, and MAC changes governs it on inbound frames. The distributed switch sets a default policy that its port groups inherit, and a port group can override it, so verify the setting at both levels.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Some workloads, such as clustered applications, network devices or functions, applications licensed by MAC address, and vCenter Reduced Downtime Upgrade, rely on MAC changes as a normal part of their operation. If those workloads are present, create a separate distributed port group that allows MAC changes and connect only the authorized VMs to it. The default Reject setting has no effect on workloads that keep their assigned MAC.
Setting Location: UI: Distributed Port Group > Configure > Settings > Policies
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
Get-VDSwitch -Name $VDS | Get-VDSecurityPolicy | Set-VDSecurityPolicy -MacChanges $false
Get-VDPortgroup -Name $VDPG | Get-VDSecurityPolicy | Set-VDSecurityPolicy -MacChanges $false

Description/Title: Reject promiscuous mode on distributed switches and port groups.
Discussion:
A network adapter normally accepts only the frames addressed to its own MAC address. Promiscuous mode makes the adapter accept every frame on its port group, regardless of destination. The Promiscuous Mode policy on a distributed switch port group controls whether the guest is allowed to do this: "Reject" keeps the host filtering frames to each VM's own address, while "Accept" lets a VM see all traffic on the port group.

With promiscuous mode allowed, a single compromised or malicious VM can passively capture the traffic of every other VM on the same port group, including credentials and other data in transit, a technique known as network sniffing. The distributed switch sets a default policy that its port groups inherit, and a port group can override it, so verify the setting at both levels.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Certain workloads and types of work, such as DHCP servers, network devices, and security monitoring, rely on promiscuous mode as a normal part of their operation. If those workloads are present, create a separate distributed port group that allows promiscuous mode and connect only the authorized VMs to it. The default Reject setting has no effect on workloads that only need their own traffic.
Setting Location: UI: Distributed Port Group > Configure > Settings > Policies
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
Get-VDSwitch -Name $VDS | Get-VDSecurityPolicy | Set-VDSecurityPolicy -AllowPromiscuous $false
Get-VDPortgroup -Name $VDPG | Get-VDSecurityPolicy | Set-VDSecurityPolicy -AllowPromiscuous $false

Description/Title: Reset distributed switch port configuration when a virtual machine disconnects.
Discussion:
A distributed switch applies security and other network policies at the port group level, but an individual port can carry per-port overrides that differ from the port group's settings. The reset-at-disconnect setting controls what happens to those overrides when a virtual machine disconnects from a port: when enabled, the port's configuration is cleared back to the port group's policy; when disabled, any per-port overrides persist on that now-vacant port.

If a vacant port keeps a per-port override that is weaker than the port group's policy, the next virtual machine that connects to it inherits that weaker setting rather than the intended baseline. Resetting the port on disconnect means each newly connected VM starts from the port group's policy, so a leftover or unauthorized override cannot quietly lower the security of a later workload.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Leaving this enabled has no effect on normal connectivity; ports simply return to the port group's policy when a VM disconnects. Disabling it lets per-port overrides linger on vacant ports, so a later virtual machine can inherit a configuration that was never intended for it.
Setting Location: UI: Distributed Port Group > Configure > Settings > Properties
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
$VDPGview = Get-VDPortgroup -Name $VDPG | Get-View
$spec = New-Object VMware.Vim.DVPortgroupConfigSpec
$spec.DefaultPortConfig = New-Object VMware.Vim.VMwareDVSPortSetting
$spec.Policy = New-Object VMware.Vim.VMwareDVSPortgroupPolicy
$spec.Policy.PortConfigResetAtDisconnect = $true
$spec.ConfigVersion = $VDPGview.Config.ConfigVersion
$VDPGview.ReconfigureDVPortgroup_Task($spec)

Description/Title: Disable CDP/LLDP discovery protocols on distributed switches unless required.
Discussion:
Cisco Discovery Protocol (CDP) and Link Layer Discovery Protocol (LLDP) let network devices announce and learn about their directly connected neighbors. A distributed switch can participate as a listener (receiving neighbor information), an advertiser (announcing its own), or both. The information exchanged includes device names, management IP addresses, and software versions, and it is sent unencrypted on the physical network.

When the distributed switch advertises, it exposes details about the virtualization environment that an adversary can use to map the network or impersonate a device. Listen mode does not advertise, so it avoids that disclosure entirely and is a defensible middle ground where the inbound neighbor data helps manage physical connectivity; the residual is that the switch still parses unauthenticated discovery frames from the network. Setting the protocol to none removes the channel completely. Neither mode is needed for normal workload traffic, so enable discovery only on the switches and during the periods where it is genuinely useful, such as troubleshooting uplink connectivity or validating physical switch configuration.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling discovery protocols removes the CDP/LLDP data that network topology, monitoring, and troubleshooting tools rely on to map links between the distributed switch and the physical network; verify which tools depend on it before turning it off. Re-enable it temporarily on a specific switch when that visibility is needed.
Setting Location: UI: Distributed Switch > Configure > Settings > Properties
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
$VDview = Get-VDSwitch -Name $VDS | Get-View
$spec = New-Object VMware.Vim.VMwareDVSConfigSpec
$spec.LinkDiscoveryProtocolConfig = New-Object VMware.Vim.LinkDiscoveryProtocolConfig
$spec.LinkDiscoveryProtocolConfig.Protocol = 'cdp'
$spec.LinkDiscoveryProtocolConfig.Operation = 'none'
$spec.ConfigVersion = $VDview.Config.ConfigVersion
$VDview.ReconfigureDvs_Task($spec)

Description/Title: Send distributed switch NetFlow data only to authorized collectors.
Discussion:
NetFlow (implemented as IPFIX on the distributed switch) exports metadata about the traffic crossing the switch, the source and destination addresses, ports, protocols, and volumes of each flow, to an external collector for monitoring and analysis. It describes traffic patterns rather than packet contents, and it is sent unencrypted.

That metadata reveals how the virtual network is laid out and which systems talk to each other, which is useful reconnaissance for an adversary. If a collector address is wrong, unauthorized, or has been added by an attacker, this picture of the environment is delivered straight to them. NetFlow is not configured out of the box; the task is to confirm that any configured collectors are known and approved, and to remove any that are not.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Removing an unrecognized collector stops NetFlow export to it, which would interrupt any legitimate monitoring that depended on that target, so confirm a collector is genuinely unauthorized before removing it. Leaving NetFlow unconfigured, the default, has no effect on workload traffic.
Setting Location: UI: Distributed Switch > Configure > Settings > NetFlow
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
$VDPGview = Get-VDPortgroup -Name $VDPG | Get-View
$spec = New-Object VMware.Vim.DVPortgroupConfigSpec
$spec.DefaultPortConfig = New-Object VMware.Vim.VMwareDVSPortSetting
$spec.DefaultPortConfig.IpfixEnabled = New-Object VMware.Vim.BoolPolicy
$spec.DefaultPortConfig.IpfixEnabled.Inherited = $false
$spec.DefaultPortConfig.IpfixEnabled.Value = $false
$spec.ConfigVersion = $VDPGview.Config.ConfigVersion
$VDPGview.ReconfigureDVPortgroup_Task($spec)

Description/Title: Disallow per-port policy overrides on distributed port groups, except port blocking.
Discussion:
A distributed port group defines the network policy, security settings, VLAN, traffic shaping, and teaming, for the ports it contains. By default a port cannot override most of these; the per-port override flags are off, except the one that allows a port to be administratively blocked. The block override is benign and is left enabled.

Allowing the other per-port overrides lets an individual port's configuration diverge from the port group's baseline, including to a weaker security policy, and a single port's override is far easier to overlook than the central port group setting. Keeping overrides disabled means every port in the group inherits the same reviewed policy. When a particular workload genuinely needs different settings, create a separate port group with those settings rather than overriding at the port.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Keeping per-port overrides disabled has no effect on normal connectivity; ports follow their port group's policy. Enabling them allows individual ports to deviate from that policy, including weakening it, and those per-port changes are easy to miss during review. Workloads that need different settings should get a dedicated port group instead.
Installation Default Value: Block Ports Override: TRUE, All Other Overrides: FALSE
Baseline Suggested Value: Block Ports Override: TRUE, All Other Overrides: FALSE
Setting Location: UI: Distributed Port Group > Configure > Settings > Properties
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
$VDPGview = Get-VDPortgroup -Name $VDPG | Get-View
$spec = New-Object VMware.Vim.DVPortgroupConfigSpec
$spec.DefaultPortConfig = New-Object VMware.Vim.VMwareDVSPortSetting
$spec.Policy = New-Object VMware.Vim.VMwareDVSPortgroupPolicy
$spec.Policy.UplinkTeamingOverrideAllowed = $false
$spec.Policy.BlockOverrideAllowed = $true
$spec.Policy.LivePortMovingAllowed = $false
$spec.Policy.VlanOverrideAllowed = $false
$spec.Policy.SecurityPolicyOverrideAllowed = $false
$spec.Policy.VendorConfigOverrideAllowed = $false
$spec.Policy.ShapingOverrideAllowed = $false
$spec.Policy.IpfixOverrideAllowed = $false
$spec.Policy.TrafficFilterOverrideAllowed = $false
$spec.ConfigVersion = $VDPGview.Config.ConfigVersion
$VDPGview.ReconfigureDVPortgroup_Task($spec)

Description/Title: Remove unauthorized port mirroring sessions on distributed switches.
Discussion:
Port mirroring on a distributed switch copies the traffic from one or more source ports to a destination port or a remote address, the same idea as a physical switch SPAN port. Unlike NetFlow, which exports flow metadata, a mirror session copies the actual packets, including their contents, so it is a complete view of the mirrored traffic.

That makes an unauthorized mirror session a direct way to eavesdrop on another virtual machine: an attacker who can create one can stream a target's traffic to a destination they control, without touching the target VM. No mirror sessions exist by default, so the task is to review the configured sessions and remove any that are not known and approved.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Removing a mirror session stops the traffic copy it produced, which would interrupt a legitimate monitoring or intrusion-detection feed if that session was authorized, so confirm a session is unauthorized before removing it. No mirror sessions exist by default, so the baseline state has no effect on workload traffic.
Setting Location: UI: Distributed Switch > Configure > Settings > Port Mirroring

Description/Title: Restrict VLAN trunking (Virtual Guest Tagging) on distributed port groups to authorized VMs.
Discussion:
A VLAN (virtual LAN) tag marks which logical network a frame belongs to, keeping traffic on different VLANs separated even when they share physical links. On a distributed switch, tagging is normally handled by the switch (Virtual Switch Tagging): each port group has one VLAN, and the switch adds and strips the tag so the guest never sees it. Virtual Guest Tagging (VGT) instead sets a port group to trunk a range of VLANs and passes the tagged frames straight through, leaving the guest operating system to process the VLAN tags with an 802.1Q driver.

A port group in trunking mode therefore exposes the attached VM to every VLAN in the trunk range, which is what makes it sensitive: a VM that is not specifically built and authorized for this can see traffic from other VLANs, or inject frames into them, undermining the VLAN segmentation that separates tenants or zones. Use VGT only on port groups serving VMs that are equipped to handle VLAN tags and are authorized for that cross-VLAN access; otherwise keep port groups on a single VLAN.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: A VM that relies on VGT to handle multiple VLANs itself, such as a virtual router, firewall, or nested hypervisor, loses that multi-VLAN access if its port group is changed off trunking, so confirm a trunking port group is unauthorized before changing it. Configuring trunking on a port group whose VMs are not prepared for it exposes those VMs to traffic from other VLANs.
Setting Location: UI: Distributed Port Group > Configure > Settings > Policies

Description/Title: Enforce password complexity for vCenter SSO accounts.
Discussion:
Password complexity rules govern what a password must satisfy before vCenter SSO accepts it. Length is the dominant factor in resisting guessing and brute-force attempts, where an attacker systematically tries many candidate passwords; a character-class floor adds basic variety without forcing the short, hard-to-remember strings that rigid composition rules tend to produce. The default policy already requires one character of each class but ships with a shorter minimum length than this baseline recommends and a short maximum length that prevents longer passphrases, so raising the length floor and lifting the maximum are the changes this control calls for.

These rules apply to accounts created in vCenter SSO, including administrator@vsphere.local. They do not apply to Active Directory users when vCenter is joined to a domain; those password policies are enforced by Active Directory.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Raising the minimum length means new or changed SSO passwords must be longer, and any tooling that sets SSO passwords must supply a conforming value; existing passwords keep working until they are next changed. Weakening the policy makes passwords easier to guess, while tightening it with rigid composition or a short maximum can push users toward predictable patterns.
Configuration Parameter: Minimum Length, Maximum Length, Character Requirements
Installation Default Value: Minimum Length: 8, Maximum Length: 20, At least 1 uppercase, At least 1 lowercase, At least 1 numeric, At least 1 special, At least 2 alphabetic, Identical adjacent characters: 3
Baseline Suggested Value: Minimum Length: 15, Maximum Length: 64, At least 1 uppercase, At least 1 lowercase, At least 1 numeric, At least 1 special, At least 2 alphabetic, Identical adjacent characters: 3

Description/Title: Restrict password reuse on vCenter SSO accounts.
Discussion:
Password history makes vCenter SSO remember a number of a user's previous passwords and reject a new password that matches one of them. This stops a user from cycling straight back to a password they just moved away from, which matters most when the change was prompted by a suspected compromise or a reset: reusing the old value would undo the point of the change.

The default already remembers several recent passwords, so this control verifies that history has not been reduced or disabled. It complements the SCG password standard, which does not force periodic rotation; history is not about churning passwords but about preventing immediate reuse of a retired one.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Keeping the default has no effect on normal use. Reducing the count or disabling history lets a user set a new password back to one they recently retired, including the one a change was meant to replace.

Description/Title: Disable forced password expiration on vCenter SSO accounts.
Discussion:
Current authentication guidance (NIST SP 800-63B) finds that forcing users to change passwords on a fixed schedule does not improve security when passwords already have adequate length and entropy; it tends to push users toward minor, predictable variations of the same password. The recommendation is to not expire passwords on a timer and instead rotate only on evidence of compromise.

The baseline value is effectively no expiration, set to the highest value vCenter SSO reliably accepts rather than a literal zero. Many automated security tools and regulatory frameworks have not caught up to this guidance and may push for periodic expiration; where a policy mandates it, that requirement overrides this recommendation.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Lowering this value forces users to change vCenter SSO passwords on a recurring schedule, which drives them toward predictable variations and adds churn without a real security gain. Keeping it at the baseline leaves passwords in place until there is a reason to change them.

Description/Title: Enable Enhanced vMotion Compatibility on vCenter clusters.
Discussion:
Enhanced vMotion Compatibility (EVC) presents a common CPU feature baseline across the hosts in a cluster, so vMotion can move a running workload between hosts even when their processors are from different generations or are running different CPU microcode.

This matters most when a CPU-level vulnerability is disclosed and its mitigation arrives as a microcode or firmware update: applying that update changes the CPU features a host exposes, which without a common baseline can break vMotion compatibility between patched and not-yet-patched hosts in the middle of a rollout. With EVC enabled, an administrator can live-migrate workloads off each host to apply the mitigation and patch it, then move them back, without a compatibility error stranding running VMs. The tradeoff is small: EVC masks the newest CPU instructions to hold the common baseline, which can withhold a few features from workloads that would otherwise use them, and it consumes no additional capacity.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Enabling EVC on a cluster sets a common CPU baseline; virtual machines already using CPU features above that baseline may need to be powered off and on for the change to take effect. Leaving EVC off means a microcode change or a mixed-CPU host can block vMotion, which can strand running workloads on a host that needs patching.
Setting Location: UI: Cluster > Configure > Configuration > VMware EVC
PowerCLI Command Remediation: Get-Cluster -Name $Cluster | Set-Cluster -EVCMode <EVC mode for the cluster CPUs>

Description/Title: Enable vSphere High Availability on clusters.
Discussion:
vSphere High Availability (HA) monitors the hosts in a cluster and, when a host fails, automatically restarts the virtual machines that were running on it on the remaining hosts. Availability is a core security property: a workload that cannot be reached after a hardware fault is as unavailable to its legitimate users as one taken down by an attack, and unplanned outages also pressure operators into rushed, less careful recovery actions.

Enabling vSphere HA is a low-cost cluster setting, and the restart capability itself consumes no dedicated resources. Guaranteeing that spare capacity always exists to restart workloads from a failed host is a separate decision, made through HA admission control, which reserves failover capacity and is a cost-benefit trade-off the customer sizes for their environment. Enable HA for the restart protection, and size admission control to the cluster.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With HA enabled, workloads from a failed host restart automatically on surviving hosts after a short interruption while they boot. Without it, a host hardware failure leaves its virtual machines down until an operator manually restarts them elsewhere. If HA is enabled without enough spare capacity or admission control, some restarts can fail when a host is lost.
Setting Location: UI: Cluster > Configure > Services > vSphere Availability

Description/Title: Enable vMotion for live workload migration.
Discussion:
vMotion live-migrates a running virtual machine from one host to another with no downtime. It is the foundation for non-disruptive operations: an administrator can evacuate a host to patch or remediate it, and DRS and maintenance mode rely on vMotion to rebalance and drain hosts. Without it, taking a host out of service means powering off or interrupting the workloads on it.

Availability is a core security property, and patchability depends on it: if workloads cannot be moved off a host, operators face a choice between running unpatched and accepting downtime, and that pressure tends to delay security fixes. vMotion is a low-cost capability and adds no dedicated resource overhead beyond the network used to carry migrations. Securing that migration network is a separate control (see esx-9.vmk-vmotion).
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: With vMotion available, hosts can be drained for patching and DRS can rebalance load without interrupting workloads. Without it, every host maintenance action requires powering off or otherwise disrupting the virtual machines on that host.
Setting Location: UI: Host > Configure > Networking > VMkernel adapters

Description/Title: Enforce an idle session timeout for the vSphere Client.
Discussion:
The vSphere Client session timeout controls how long an idle, authenticated session to the vSphere Client stays valid before the server ends it and requires the user to sign in again. An idle session is a logged-in window into vCenter with the user's privileges; if it stays open, someone with access to the unattended browser or workstation, or who hijacks the session, can act as that user without authenticating. A shorter idle timeout narrows that window.

The product default leaves sessions valid far longer than necessary for interactive administration. The recommended value ends idle sessions promptly while still allowing normal working pauses. This applies to the vSphere Client UI; it does not affect API clients or active, in-use sessions.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: A shorter timeout signs idle administrators out sooner, so someone who steps away and returns after the interval must log in again; set it long enough to avoid interrupting normal working pauses. Leaving the long default means an unattended, authenticated vSphere Client session stays usable for an extended period.

Description/Title: Send vCenter notification email over a secure SMTP connection.
Discussion:
vCenter can send email notifications for alarms and alerts through an SMTP server. If that connection is not protected, the notification content travels in cleartext on the network, where it can be read or altered, and the SMTP credentials can be exposed. Configure vCenter to use a secure submission connection to the mail server.

The SMTP username and password are stored in vCenter advanced settings, where any administrator with access to those settings can read them. Use a dedicated service account scoped only to sending mail, or restrict the mail relay to accept vCenter by IP address instead of a password, so that a credential read from the settings cannot be reused for anything sensitive. This control applies only where vCenter SMTP notifications are configured.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: These settings apply only if vCenter SMTP notifications are in use; with email unconfigured there is no effect. Pointing vCenter at a port or server that does not support the secure connection, or supplying credentials the mail server rejects, stops alarm and alert emails from being delivered.
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
Get-AdvancedSetting -Entity $VC -Name "mail.smtp.port" | Set-AdvancedSetting -Value "587"
Get-AdvancedSetting -Entity $VC -Name "mail.smtp.username" | Set-AdvancedSetting -Value $SmtpUser
Get-AdvancedSetting -Entity $VC -Name "mail.smtp.password" | Set-AdvancedSetting -Value $SmtpPassword

Description/Title: Disable SNMP v1 and v2c receivers on vCenter.
Discussion:
Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) lets a monitoring system collect device data and receive event notifications, called traps. vCenter can forward SNMP traps to one or more receivers configured in the vSphere Client. SNMP versions 1 and 2c authenticate with a community string, a shared secret sent across the network in cleartext along with the data it carries. Anyone who can observe that traffic can read the community string and the trap contents, and can replay or forge messages to a receiver.

vCenter ships with no SNMP v1 or v2c receivers enabled, so the default state already meets this recommendation; the control audits that none have been added. Where SNMP monitoring is required, use SNMP version 3, which adds cryptographic authentication and payload encryption, configured separately (see vcenter-9.snmp3).
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling SNMP v1 and v2c receivers affects any monitoring system that relies on vCenter traps over those versions; move those integrations to SNMP v3 before removing the receivers. Leaving a v1 or v2c receiver enabled exposes the community string and trap contents to anyone who can observe the network path.
Setting Location: UI: vCenter Server >> Configure >> Settings >> General >> SNMP receivers

Description/Title: Enforce SNMPv3 authentication and privacy where SNMP is enabled on vCenter.
Discussion:
vCenter includes an SNMP agent that can publish appliance and host data to a monitoring system. The agent is disabled by default and only needs configuration if SNMP monitoring is turned on. When it is enabled, use SNMP version 3, which adds two protections the earlier versions lack: authentication, which lets the agent and the monitoring system verify each other's identity using a keyed hash rather than a shared cleartext string, and privacy, which encrypts the message payload so it cannot be read in transit.

Configure SNMPv3 with a hash-based authentication algorithm (SHA) and an AES privacy algorithm, and set every SNMP user to the privacy security level so that both authentication and encryption are always applied. A user left at a lower security level authenticates but still sends its data in cleartext, which defeats the reason for choosing version 3.

This control applies only where SNMP is in use; if no monitoring system polls vCenter over SNMP, leave the agent disabled. Disabling the SNMP v1 and v2c receivers is covered separately (see vcenter-9.snmp).
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Where SNMP monitoring is in use, enforcing SNMPv3 with authentication and privacy means the monitoring system must hold matching credentials and algorithms; a mismatch stops it from polling vCenter until both sides agree. Enabling SNMP without these settings, or leaving a user below the privacy level, sends monitoring data over the network in cleartext to anyone on the path.

Description/Title: Deactivate SSH access to the vCenter appliance except for temporary diagnostic use.
Discussion:
The vCenter appliance runs an SSH daemon that gives an administrator an interactive command-line session into the appliance's operating system. Anyone who reaches the SSH listener with a valid credential gets a shell on the appliance, outside the vSphere Client's role-based access control and its event log, with direct access to the files and services that run vCenter. SSH is a break-glass tool for diagnostics and recovery when the vSphere Client, the appliance management interface, and the API are not enough; it is not the routine management interface.

vCenter ships with SSH deactivated, so the default state already meets this recommendation; the control audits that it has not been left on. Enable it only for the duration of a specific support or troubleshooting task, then deactivate it again. Who may use the shell once SSH is on is governed separately (see vcenter-9.bashshelladministrators).
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Keeping SSH deactivated has no day-to-day operational cost; routine management uses the vSphere Client, the appliance management interface, and the vCenter API rather than SSH. Leaving SSH enabled keeps an interactive shell on the appliance listening continuously, which is the login surface this recommendation removes; plan for brief, deliberate re-enablement when support work genuinely needs it.
Setting Location: UI: vCenter Server Management Interface (VAMI) > Access

Description/Title: vCenter must run a version that has not reached End of General Support.
Discussion:
Broadcom publishes a lifecycle policy for each vCenter release that sets the date it reaches End of General Support. After that date the release no longer receives routine security patches, bug fixes, or technical support, so a vCenter left on an unsupported version accumulates exposure to every vulnerability found after the cutoff, since fixes are not back-ported to out-of-support releases.

vCenter is the management plane for the environment: it holds administrative credentials, the certificates that anchor trust between components, and the control path to every host and workload. Keeping it on a supported version matters more than for most components, because a compromise there reaches the whole estate. Track the published lifecycle dates and plan upgrades before the support cutoff; deferring past End of General Support also makes the eventual upgrade harder, as the number of required intermediate steps grows over time.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: A vCenter left on an out-of-support version accumulates exposure to vulnerabilities discovered after End of General Support, since fixes are not back-ported. The eventual upgrade is also more complex than from a supported version, because skip-version paths and required intermediate steps grow over time.
Setting Location: Compare the vCenter version and build number to the lifecycle policy published on the Broadcom Support Portal.

Description/Title: vCenter must synchronize its clock with organizational reference sources.
Discussion:
Accurate time on vCenter underpins certificate validity checks, the ordering of audit and event logs, cluster operations, Kerberos and other time-bounded authentication, and incident response that correlates events across systems. vCenter ties these together for the whole environment: if its clock drifts outside a certificate validity window, TLS handshakes fail; outside the Kerberos clock-skew tolerance, Active Directory logins fail; and its logs can no longer be aligned with logs from hosts and other components.

Configure vCenter to synchronize from your organization's authoritative time sources, and confirm the time-synchronization mode is set to NTP rather than following the underlying host, so the appliance tracks a known reference rather than a host whose own clock may be wrong. Use one, or three or more, time sources rather than exactly two, so the appliance is never left arbitrating between two clocks that disagree.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: A vCenter with an inaccurate or unsynchronized clock fails any operation that depends on time: TLS handshakes against certificates outside the validity window, Active Directory logins that require Kerberos clock-skew tolerance, and cluster operations that assume consistent timestamps. Its audit logs also cannot be aligned with other systems, which weakens incident investigation.
Setting Location: UI: vCenter Server Management Interface (VAMI) > Time
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
# Replace the example servers with your organization's authoritative NTP sources
(Get-CisService -Name "com.vmware.appliance.timesync").set("NTP")
(Get-CisService -Name "com.vmware.appliance.ntp").set("ntp1.example.com,ntp2.example.com,ntp3.example.com")

Description/Title: vCenter's TLS profile must restrict negotiated ciphers and protocols to a modern, approved set.
Discussion:
vCenter uses TLS to protect its management connections: the vSphere Client and Host Client, the vCenter API, the links between vCenter and ESX hosts and other VCF components, and external integrations such as identity sources and monitoring systems. A TLS profile is a named bundle of allowed protocol versions, cipher suites, key exchange groups, and signature algorithms; when a client connects, vCenter negotiates only what the active profile permits. The installed default profile keeps backwards compatibility with older clients by allowing protocol versions and cipher suites that are no longer considered strong.

The recommended profile narrows that set to TLS 1.3 with a modern cipher suite, removing the older protocol versions and weak ciphers an attacker could try to force a connection down to. Before applying it broadly, confirm that the clients, components, and integrations that talk to vCenter can all negotiate TLS 1.3, because any party that cannot will lose its connection until it is updated or the profile is widened. Changing the global profile requires restarting vCenter services for the new profile to take effect.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: A strict TLS profile drops the older protocol versions and cipher suites that some clients, components, or integrations may still depend on; any party unable to negotiate TLS 1.3 with the profile's cipher set loses connectivity to vCenter until it is updated or the profile is widened. Applying the profile also restarts vCenter services, causing a brief management-plane interruption.
Setting Location: API: vSphere Client > Developer Center > API Explorer (appliance, tls/profiles/global)

Description/Title: Configure the vCenter appliance firewall to allow inbound traffic only from authorized networks.
Discussion:
vCenter's appliance management interface lets you define inbound firewall rules that limit which source networks can reach the appliance's management services. By default the appliance accepts management connections from any source address, so any host that can route to vCenter can attempt to reach the vSphere Client, the API, and the appliance management interface. Restricting inbound access to the networks your administrators and infrastructure actually use removes the rest of the network as a starting point for credential attacks and exploitation attempts.

These rules apply to inbound connections. Outbound connections that vCenter initiates, and the established or related return traffic for them, are not blocked, so vCenter can still reach the services it depends on. Use perimeter firewalls to constrain vCenter's outbound paths where that is required.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: The main risk is locking out legitimate management access: add an allow rule for your own administrative network before applying a default-deny rule, or you will lose connectivity to the appliance. Rules that are too broad leave the management interface reachable from networks that have no need to reach it.
Installation Default Value: Connections allowed from any IP address
Baseline Suggested Value: Connections allowed only from authorized infrastructure and administration workstations
Setting Location: UI: vCenter Server Management Interface (VAMI) > Firewall

Description/Title: Disable forced password expiration on the vCenter appliance root account.
Discussion:
Current authentication guidance (NIST SP 800-63B) finds that forcing a fixed-schedule password change does not improve security when the password already has adequate length and entropy; it mainly pushes users toward small, predictable variations of the same password. The recommendation is to not expire passwords on a timer and instead rotate the account only when there is evidence of compromise.

On the vCenter appliance, the local root account governs shell and appliance-management access. When root password expiration is left enabled and the password is not reset in time, root can become locked out, and regaining access requires the appliance recovery procedure. This lockout often takes operators by surprise because the expiration runs silently in the background. Disabling forced expiration removes that operational failure mode while keeping the account's password strength as the control that matters; pair it with a strong root password and rotation on suspicion of compromise.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Leaving root expiration enabled risks locking out the root account if the password is not reset before it expires, after which regaining access requires the appliance recovery procedure. Disabling expiration removes that risk; the tradeoff is that the root password then changes only when an administrator rotates it, so it must be strong and rotated if compromise is suspected.
Setting Location: UI: vCenter Server Management Interface (VAMI) > Administration

Description/Title: vCenter must have all available software updates and patches installed.
Discussion:
Broadcom publishes regular patch releases for each vCenter major version that address security vulnerabilities and bugs found after the original release. A vCenter running back-revision patches carries the known vulnerabilities fixed in releases it has not yet applied, including any disclosed in Broadcom security advisories since its current build.

When applying updates, update vCenter before the ESX hosts it manages. vCenter must be at a version equal to or newer than the hosts it manages, so updating it first keeps the management layer compatible with the hosts through the rest of the cycle. Apply appliance updates through the appliance management interface or vCenter Lifecycle Manager.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: A vCenter left back-level on patches is exposed to any vulnerability disclosed since its current build. The eventual catch-up upgrade is also larger and more involved, since multiple update releases may need to be applied in sequence, each with its own pre-check and reboot.
Setting Location: UI: vCenter Server Management Interface (VAMI) > Update; compare the installed build to the latest available patch on the Broadcom Support Portal.

Description/Title: Disable the vSphere Distributed Switch health check when not troubleshooting.
Discussion:
The vSphere Distributed Switch health check verifies VLAN, MTU, and network adapter teaming configuration between the distributed switch and the physical switches by sending test packets out the uplinks. Those packets carry host, distributed switch, and port identifiers, which exposes internal network topology to anyone able to observe traffic on the physical network or the VLAN.

vCenter ships with the health check disabled, so the default state already meets this recommendation; the control audits that it has not been left enabled. Turn it on only while diagnosing a VLAN, MTU, or teaming problem, and disable it again when finished. The detail it advertises overlaps with what link-layer discovery protocols expose, so where those are also restricted (see vcenter-9.network-restrict-discovery-protocol), keeping the health check off keeps that topology information off the wire. This control applies only where distributed switches are in use.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Leaving the health check disabled has no effect on normal networking; it only needs to be on while diagnosing a VLAN, MTU, or teaming mismatch. Enabling it and forgetting to turn it off continuously advertises host, switch, and port identifiers onto the physical network, giving an observer a map of the internal topology.
Setting Location: UI: vSphere Client > Networking > [Distributed Switch] > Configure > Settings > Health Check

Description/Title: Keep the vpxuser password at or above its default length.
Discussion:
vpxuser is the account vCenter creates and manages on each ESX host to authenticate and run management operations on that host. vCenter generates the password automatically and rotates it, so administrators never type or see it. Because the credential is machine-generated, it can be long at no usability cost, and a long random password resists brute-force guessing, where an attacker tries many passwords in succession to recover the credential.

This setting controls the length vCenter uses when it generates that password. The default is already long, so the recommendation is to confirm it has not been reduced. Lowering it below the default weakens every host's vpxuser credential at once and has no operational benefit, since the password is never entered by hand.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Reducing the length below the default shortens the auto-generated vpxuser credential on every managed host, making it easier to brute-force, with no offsetting benefit since the password is never entered by hand. Increasing it has no operational effect, because vCenter generates and rotates the password automatically.

Description/Title: Rotate the vpxuser password at least as often as the default.
Discussion:
vpxuser is the account vCenter creates and manages on each ESX host (see vcenter-9.vpxuser-length). vCenter not only generates this password but also rotates it automatically on a schedule, so a credential that is ever captured or leaked stops being valid after the next rotation. This setting controls how often vCenter changes it.

The default interval is already short enough to limit the useful lifetime of a captured credential, so the recommendation is to confirm it has not been changed. Setting the interval to zero disables rotation entirely, leaving each host's vpxuser password static indefinitely; lengthening it widens the window in which a captured credential remains usable. Because vCenter handles the rotation with no administrator involvement, there is no operational reason to weaken it.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling rotation (interval zero) or lengthening it leaves each host's vpxuser password valid for longer, so a credential captured once stays usable until the next change. Keeping or shortening the interval has no operational effect, because vCenter performs the rotation automatically.

Description/Title: Integrate VMware Cloud Foundation with an identity provider that enforces multifactor authentication.
Discussion:
Multifactor authentication (MFA) requires more than a password to sign in, typically a second factor from a device the user holds, so a stolen or phished password alone does not grant access. VCF administration is privileged access to the entire environment, which makes it a high-value target, so administrative sign-ins should require MFA. VCF provides single sign-on across its components (vCenter, NSX, VCF Operations, Automation, and others) and can federate to an external identity provider; configure that provider to enforce MFA for the accounts used to administer VCF.

Use an identity source dedicated to infrastructure administration rather than the general-purpose corporate directory, so that a compromise of everyday user identity does not automatically reach the management plane. The provider should apply authentication controls, such as a strong password policy and phishing-resistant MFA methods, at least as strict as the organization's standard for privileged access.

ESX hosts should likewise authenticate administrators through the central directory rather than local host accounts, so host access follows the same identity and MFA controls and is removed centrally when an administrator leaves.
