Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Integrating an external identity provider and enforcing MFA adds an authentication dependency: administrators sign in through the provider, so a provider outage or misconfiguration can block administrative access. Keep a documented break-glass local account for recovery. Leaving administration on local accounts without MFA means a single stolen or reused password is enough to reach the management plane.
Setting Location: UI: VCF SSO identity provider / federation configuration (see product documentation for integrating an external identity provider)

Description/Title: Limit access to VCF component management interfaces with perimeter firewalls.
Discussion:
The management interfaces of the VCF components (vCenter, ESX, NSX Manager, VCF Operations, SDDC Manager, and the others) should sit on a management network that is reachable only from the networks administrators use, not from general user or workload networks and not from the internet. Placing perimeter firewalls between the management network and everything else limits which sources can even reach these interfaces, so an attacker who gains a foothold on a user or workload network cannot directly probe or attack the management plane.

This network-layer isolation is part of a defense-in-depth approach, where independent layers of control each reduce risk so that no single failure exposes the system. It complements the per-appliance firewall rules (for example esx-9.firewall-restrict-access and vcenter-9.vami-firewall-restrict-access), which restrict access at each appliance: the perimeter firewall keeps the management network unreachable from unauthorized networks, while the appliance rules constrain access among the hosts that can reach it.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Segmenting the management network behind perimeter firewalls has no effect on workloads, but administrators must connect from an authorized network (or through a bastion/jump host) to manage the environment, so plan admin access paths before tightening the perimeter. Leaving the management interfaces reachable from general or untrusted networks exposes the entire management plane to anyone who can route to it.
Setting Location: Customer perimeter firewall / network segmentation (external to VCF)

Description/Title: Review and right-size accounts, permissions, and role assignments across all VCF components.
Discussion:
The VCF management plane spans multiple components with independent role systems, including vCenter, NSX Manager, VCF Operations, VCF Operations for Networks, VCF Automation, and SDDC Manager. Over time, role assignments drift: deployment-time roles persist after they are no longer needed, service accounts retain broad privileges granted during initial troubleshooting, and former employees' group memberships outlast their tenure. Each over-broad role becomes a foothold an attacker who compromises that identity can use to move laterally or escalate within the management plane.

Least privilege is the principle that every identity is granted only the access required to perform its function. Periodically review accounts and role assignments in each component's access-control interface, confirm that every user, group, and service account still needs the access it holds, and reduce or remove what is no longer justified. Disable or remove accounts that are no longer needed at all -- departed staff, decommissioned integrations, one-off troubleshooting logins -- rather than leaving them dormant where they widen the attack surface. Pay particular attention to service accounts used by automation or integrations: they accumulate privileges through configuration churn, are rarely rotated, and often hold administrative scope. Do not remove the internal product appliance accounts, which ship as non-login system accounts (a shell of /bin/nologin or /bin/false, or a locked password) and are required for patching and upgrades; removing them breaks lifecycle operations. No single product setting expresses this control; it is a recurring access-governance activity that lands on a site-specific baseline.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Reducing permissions for service accounts in active use can interrupt automation, monitoring agents, or integration workflows; communicate planned changes and stage them so that affected callers can be updated. Allowing over-broad access to persist keeps the consequences of any single compromised identity proportionally larger.

Description/Title: Apply the controls in this guidance, and document the rationale for any deviations.
Discussion:
The controls in this guide are the security baseline for a VCF deployment. The guide is not a list of every possible hardening step; it is a working set of controls balanced for security value and operational cost. An operator who chooses to deviate from a baseline value, whether to keep an in-house automation working, to satisfy a vendor integration requirement, or to accept a higher risk in exchange for performance, is taking a legitimate engineering decision but is also taking on the obligation to record what was changed, why, and what compensating measure (if any) was applied.

Maintain a written record of every deviation, with the parameter name, the chosen value, the business reason, and the compensating control where one exists. This documentation is the audit trail an external assessor will ask for and the reference an internal team will need when an incident or drift investigation later asks why a setting is at its current value. Without it, intentional deviations become indistinguishable from misconfiguration.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Skipping the deviation documentation does not change the running configuration but eliminates the ability to later distinguish an intentional choice from a misconfiguration or unintended drift. Applying every baseline without weighing operational fit can also degrade outcomes, since some controls trade availability or supportability for security.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must have console copy operations deactivated.
Discussion:
Console copy is a guest-tools feature that lets a user select text inside the guest console and paste it into their local clipboard, giving anyone with console access a path to move data out of the VM without going through the network. The attack scenario is straightforward: an interactive user with remote console access who would otherwise be denied network-level data exfiltration paths can copy credentials, configuration, or other sensitive guest-side data out of the VM directly through the console session. The reverse direction (host clipboard pasted into the guest), drag-and-drop, and copy/paste through the VMware Tools GUI agent are governed by their own sibling controls.

Console copy ships disabled by default on every modern VM hardware version, so this row primarily exists to audit that the default has not been changed. Explicitly setting the advanced parameter to TRUE produces the same behavior as leaving it absent; both satisfy the baseline. Setting it to FALSE is the only configuration that enables the data path, and is almost never desirable on a production workload.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing this setting to its secure default does not affect workload behavior. Explicitly setting the parameter to FALSE re-enables copy from the guest console to the operator's local clipboard, which is the data-extraction path the control is intended to close.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must have console paste operations deactivated.
Discussion:
Console paste is a guest-tools feature that lets a user paste content from their local clipboard into the guest VM's console session, giving anyone with console access a path to inject text into the guest without going through the network. The attack scenario is the inverse of console copy: an interactive user with remote console access can paste commands into a guest shell, drop credentials into a login prompt, or place malicious scripts into a guest editor without leaving network-side audit traces. Console copy (the reverse direction, guest content into the host clipboard), drag-and-drop, and copy/paste through the VMware Tools GUI agent are governed by their own sibling controls.

Console paste ships disabled by default on every modern VM hardware version, so this row primarily exists to audit that the default has not been changed. Explicitly setting the advanced parameter to TRUE produces the same behavior as leaving it absent; both satisfy the baseline. Setting it to FALSE is the only configuration that enables the data path, and is almost never desirable on a production workload.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing this setting to its secure default does not affect workload behavior. Explicitly setting the parameter to FALSE re-enables paste from the operator's local clipboard into the guest console, which is the injection path the control is intended to close.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must have virtual disk shrinking operations deactivated.
Discussion:
Virtual disk shrinking is a guest-tools feature that allows the guest operating system to request that VMware Tools reclaim unused space in the underlying VMDK file. The reclaim runs at the hypervisor level, and any unprivileged user or process inside the guest can request it (it is not restricted to guest administrators). While the shrink is running, the disk becomes unavailable for normal I/O, so a malicious or buggy in-guest process that calls shrink repeatedly can leave the disk effectively unavailable for legitimate workload activity, a denial-of-service condition against the VM's own storage path.

In most modern data centers, virtual disks are thin-provisioned at the datastore level and managed centrally by storage administrators, so the guest-initiated shrink operation provides little day-to-day value. The supported modern path for the same goal is guest-issued TRIM (SATA) and UNMAP (SCSI/NVMe) commands honored by the underlying storage; vSAN supports guest TRIM/UNMAP and reclaims freed blocks incrementally without the disk-unavailability behavior the shrink operation creates. Disk shrink ships disabled by default on every modern VM hardware version, so this row primarily exists to audit that the default has not been changed. Explicitly setting the advanced parameter to TRUE produces the same behavior as leaving it absent; both satisfy the baseline. Setting it to FALSE is the only configuration that enables the guest-initiated path and is almost never desirable on a production workload.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing this setting to its secure default does not affect workload behavior. Explicitly setting the parameter to FALSE re-enables the guest-initiated shrink operation, which is the denial-of-service path the control is intended to close.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must have virtual disk wiping operations deactivated.
Discussion:
Virtual disk wiping is a guest-tools feature that allows the guest operating system to request that VMware Tools write zeros across the unused space in the underlying VMDK file, typically as a prelude to the shrink operation (which reclaims the now-zeroed pages). The wipe runs at the hypervisor level, and any unprivileged user or process inside the guest can request it (it is not restricted to guest administrators). The operation is I/O-heavy, and while it runs the disk is effectively unavailable for legitimate workload I/O. A malicious or buggy in-guest process that calls wipe repeatedly can keep the disk saturated, a denial-of-service condition against the VM's own storage path.

In most modern data centers, virtual disks are thin-provisioned at the datastore level and managed centrally by storage administrators, so the guest-initiated wipe operation provides little day-to-day value. The supported modern path for the same goal is guest-issued TRIM (SATA) and UNMAP (SCSI/NVMe) commands honored by the underlying storage; vSAN supports guest TRIM/UNMAP and reclaims freed blocks incrementally without the disk-unavailability behavior the wipe operation creates. Disk wipe ships disabled by default on every modern VM hardware version, so this row primarily exists to audit that the default has not been changed. Explicitly setting the advanced parameter to TRUE produces the same behavior as leaving it absent; both satisfy the baseline. Setting it to FALSE is the only configuration that enables the guest-initiated path and is almost never desirable on a production workload.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing this setting to its secure default does not affect workload behavior. Explicitly setting the parameter to FALSE re-enables the guest-initiated wipe operation, which is the denial-of-service path the control is intended to close.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must have 3D graphics features deactivated when not required.
Discussion:
3D graphics acceleration in a VM exposes the guest to the hypervisor's 3D rendering and SVGA driver stack, a complex code path that has historically been a guest-to-host escape vector (multiple SVGA/3D vulnerabilities have been reported and patched in past releases). Most server workloads, web/database/middleware VMs, and standard administrative desktops do not use 3D acceleration, so leaving the feature available on these VMs adds attack surface without adding capability.

3D acceleration ships disabled by default on every modern VM hardware version, so this row primarily exists to audit that the default has not been changed. VDI workloads, GPU pass-through use cases, and 3D applications (CAD, simulation, certain analytics) legitimately require 3D acceleration; for those VMs the setting is correctly enabled and this control becomes a per-VM exemption rather than a fleetwide failure. Audit the fleet for VMs with 3D enabled, confirm each one needs it, and turn it off on every VM where the workload does not require it.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing this setting to its secure default does not affect workload behavior for server, database, or standard administrative VMs. Enabling 3D acceleration is required for VDI, GPU pass-through, and 3D-application workloads (CAD, simulation, certain analytics); for those VMs, the setting must be enabled and this row becomes a workload-specific exemption rather than a fleetwide directive.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must limit access through the dvFilter API.
Discussion:
The dvFilter API is a hypervisor-level networking interface that lets a VM's virtual NIC be intercepted by a filter module loaded into the ESX kernel. The per-VM attachment is expressed as an advanced parameter of the form ethernet0.filter0.name = "<filter-name>". Any VM with such an attachment has its network traffic passed through the named filter module, which can inspect, modify, or drop packets without those operations being visible to the guest or to typical network monitoring tools watching the wire.

Legitimate users of dvFilter exist. NSX and vDefend Distributed Firewall use dvFilter to deliver in-kernel microsegmentation and packet inspection, and certain third-party network security and observability products do as well. The risk is that an attacker who has reached the ESX management plane and can edit VM advanced parameters can add a malicious filter attachment to a VM, redirecting its traffic into a filter module of their choice. For most VMs no such attachment is expected, and the row is an audit-of-default; where attachments do exist, confirm each one corresponds to a deployed and authorized filter module and remove any that do not.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing this setting on VMs that do not have a deployed dvFilter consumer does not affect workload behavior. Removing a legitimate dvFilter attachment (for example, one placed by NSX or vDefend) breaks the corresponding network filtering or microsegmentation for the affected VM; verify a filter attachment is unexpected before removing it.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must prevent booting from unauthorized sources.
Discussion:
By default, a VM's EFI firmware can boot from any source available to it (bios.bootDeviceClasses = allow:all): network PXE, attached CD/DVD, attached USB, virtual hard disk, virtual floppy, even into the EFI shell. If the primary boot volume is unavailable, missing, or corrupted, the firmware falls through to the next allowed source. An attacker who can introduce a boot source (a network PXE responder on the management network, an ISO mounted on the VM, a USB device passed through) can boot the VM into an environment under their control, bypass the installed operating system, and access the VM's virtual disks directly.

Restrict the boot device classes once the guest OS is installed. The bios.bootDeviceClasses advanced parameter takes the form allow:<class-list> or deny:<class-list>, where <class-list> is one or more of hd (virtual hard disk), cd (virtual CD/DVD), net (network PXE), usb (USB), fd (virtual floppy), efishell (EFI shell), reserved (unknown devices), and the special all / any. Setting allow implicitly denies anything not listed, and deny implicitly allows anything not listed, so allow:hd permits only virtual disk boot, while deny:net blocks network boot and leaves the rest available. The recommended baseline allow:hd confines each VM to its installed virtual disk, the only legitimate boot source for a running production workload.

New VMs need allow:hd,cd during initial guest OS install to boot the installer ISO. Recovery scenarios (boot from a rescue ISO, PXE-driven re-imaging) similarly need to widen the allowed classes temporarily. Dynamic environments such as labs or PXE-managed compute pools may need to retain net permanently; in those cases set the parameter to match the actual operational need, document the rationale, and tighten where workloads do not legitimately use the wider set.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Once allow:hd is set, the VM cannot boot from a rescue ISO, recovery media, or network PXE without first widening the parameter back out; teams that manage VMs through PXE-driven re-imaging will need to script the temporary widen before each re-image. Leaving the default allow:all keeps the VM vulnerable to attacker-supplied boot sources whenever the primary boot volume becomes unavailable.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must require encryption for Fault Tolerance.
Discussion:
vSphere Fault Tolerance maintains a real-time shadow copy of a protected VM on a second host, so that if the primary host fails the secondary VM takes over with no observable interruption. To keep the two copies in lockstep, the primary host streams a continuous FT log to the secondary host containing every memory page change and CPU instruction the primary executes. That FT log traffic carries everything sensitive the protected VM is doing in memory: credentials being parsed, in-flight requests, decrypted database pages, secret material. Anyone with access to the network the FT log traverses could read it.

FT encryption applies AES to the FT log to close that read-on-the-wire path. The default mode ftEncryptionOpportunistic enables encryption when both hosts support it and silently falls back to plaintext when they do not, which on any modern host with AES-NI almost always means encryption but does not require it. The recommended mode ftEncryptionRequired makes encryption mandatory: FT will not enable for a VM if encryption cannot be negotiated, removing the silent plaintext fallback path.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: This audit applies only to VMs with Fault Tolerance enabled (typically a small minority of the fleet). In clusters where every host supports FT encryption, the change has no operational impact because the FT pair already encrypts the log in transit under the opportunistic default. In a cluster with hosts that lack FT-encryption support, setting ftEncryptionRequired blocks FT from starting on affected VMs; verify hardware support across the cluster before applying.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must prevent unauthorized removal, connection, and modification of devices.
Discussion:
By default, an unprivileged user or process inside a guest VM can connect or disconnect certain virtual devices, including the virtual network adapter, virtual CD/DVD, and similar guest-visible hardware. A user could disconnect the vNIC to interrupt monitoring or break a session, eject a mounted ISO to invalidate a forensic snapshot, or mount and dismount removable devices repeatedly to disrupt the guest. None of these require guest-administrator privileges; the in-guest device connection control is exposed through the standard guest tooling APIs to any logged-in user.

Setting isolation.device.connectable.disable to TRUE removes that in-guest path. Operators with vCenter-level VM-edit privilege can still attach, detach, and reconfigure devices through the normal management surface; the control governs only the in-guest user pathway. The setting ships at TRUE on every modern VM hardware version, so this row primarily exists to audit that the default has not been changed. Setting it to FALSE is the only configuration that opens the in-guest path and is rarely desirable on a production workload.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing this setting to its secure default does not affect workload behavior; the vCenter management plane retains full device control. Explicitly setting the parameter to FALSE permits any in-guest user or process to connect and disconnect virtual devices on its own VM, which is the unauthorized-modification path the control is intended to close.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must have console drag and drop operations deactivated.
Discussion:
Drag-and-drop is a guest-tools feature that lets a user move files and content between the guest VM's console session and their local desktop in either direction. The bidirectional nature combines the two threat paths covered by the copy and paste sibling controls: a user with remote console access can drag sensitive files out of the guest to their workstation, or drag malicious content (binaries, scripts, payloads) into the guest console without leaving network-side audit traces. Console copy, console paste, and copy/paste through the VMware Tools GUI agent are governed by their own sibling controls.

Drag-and-drop ships disabled by default on every modern VM hardware version, so this row primarily exists to audit that the default has not been changed. Explicitly setting the advanced parameter to TRUE produces the same behavior as leaving it absent; both satisfy the baseline. Setting it to FALSE is the only configuration that enables the data path, and is almost never desirable on a production workload.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing this setting to its secure default does not affect workload behavior. Explicitly setting the parameter to FALSE re-enables file and content transfer in both directions between the guest console and the operator's local desktop, which is the data-flow path the control is intended to close.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must limit console sharing.
Discussion:
The RemoteDisplay.maxConnections setting caps the number of concurrent remote console sessions a VM accepts. Default -1 means unlimited: any user with VM-console-interact permission in vCenter can attach to the console of a VM that already has an active session, observe in real time whatever the current session is doing, and act in parallel. For a VM whose console is being used to perform privileged administration (logging into a guest, configuring an appliance, troubleshooting a service), a concurrent observer can shoulder-surf credentials, screen contents, and key presses without any indication to the operator that they are there.

Setting the value to 1 allows only one console session at a time. Subsequent connection attempts are refused until the existing session disconnects, removing the silent-observer path. VMware Cloud Director, which proxies console access on behalf of tenants, may require a value higher than 1; for VMs exposed to it, set the value to the number of legitimate concurrent sessions needed and document the exception.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Limiting concurrent console sessions to one can produce a usability problem if a previous session was disconnected uncleanly and still holds the console slot, preventing legitimate operators from reconnecting until the stale session times out. VMware Cloud Director, which proxies console access on behalf of tenants, may require a value greater than 1; setting 1 on a VM exposed to it can block its console proxy. Leaving the default -1 is what the control exists to close.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must limit informational messages from the virtual machine to the VMX file.
Discussion:
VMware Tools running inside a guest can write name-value pairs into the VM's VMX configuration file through the setInfo interface. The VMX file lives on the datastore alongside the VM's other files; if a misbehaving or malicious in-guest process repeatedly writes large values, the VMX can grow unbounded and consume datastore capacity used by other VMs on the same datastore. The tools.setInfo.sizeLimit parameter caps the VMX at 1 MB (1048576 bytes), which is enough for normal use and small enough to bound any single VM's contribution to datastore use through this interface.

This row primarily exists to audit that the default has not been raised or removed. Explicitly setting the parameter to 1048576 produces the same behavior as leaving it absent; both satisfy the baseline. Most workloads have no need to write into the VMX file from inside the guest at all, and even those that do rarely approach the 1 MB ceiling.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing this setting to its default does not affect workload behavior. Lowering the cap could prevent VMware Tools or guest agents that legitimately use the setInfo interface from completing their writes; raising the cap permits a misbehaving or malicious guest process to consume more datastore space through the VMX file.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must enable diagnostic logging.
Discussion:
Each VM has its own vmware.log file that the ESX hypervisor writes alongside the VM's other files on the datastore. The log captures the events the hypervisor sees for that VM: power on/off, suspend, snapshot operations, vMotion migrations, clone operations, hardware reconfigurations, VMware Tools status changes, time-sync events, system failures, and similar lifecycle activity. These logs provide a forensic record when investigating a VM availability incident or a suspected security event involving the VM's lifecycle.

VM-level logging ships enabled by default and this row primarily exists to audit that the default has not been turned off. Per-VM log volume is bounded by the rotation and retention settings governed by sibling controls (vm-9.log-rotation-size, vm-9.log-retention), so leaving the flag on is essentially free in terms of datastore use. Disabling it stops the per-VM vmware.log file from being written.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing this setting to its enabled default does not affect workload behavior; the per-VM log volume is bounded by the rotation and retention settings on sibling controls. Disabling VM logging stops the per-VM vmware.log file from being written.
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
$VMview = Get-VM -Name $VM | Get-View
$spec = New-Object VMware.Vim.VirtualMachineConfigSpec
$spec.Flags = New-Object VMware.Vim.VirtualMachineFlagInfo
$spec.Flags.EnableLogging = $true
$VMview.ReconfigVM_Task($spec)

Description/Title: Virtual machines must limit the number of retained diagnostic logs.
Discussion:
When per-VM logging is enabled (governed by sibling vm-9.log-enable), the ESX hypervisor writes the VM's events to vmware.log and rotates the file when it reaches the size limit set by vm-9.log-rotation-size. The log.keepOld parameter caps how many rotated copies are retained per VM. Each rotated copy is renamed vmware-N.log and the oldest is discarded once the cap is reached. The default 10 is a balance between holding enough lifecycle history for routine forensic review and bounding the per-VM datastore footprint.

This row primarily exists to audit that the default has not been raised or lowered. Setting it explicitly to 10 produces the same behavior as leaving it absent; both satisfy the baseline. The total per-VM log footprint is approximately log.keepOld * (rotation size), so changes here have a proportional effect on datastore space used per VM.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing this setting to its default does not affect workload behavior. Lowering the value shortens the on-host forensic history available for a VM; raising it increases the per-VM datastore footprint roughly linearly.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must limit the size of diagnostic logs.
Discussion:
When per-VM logging is enabled (governed by sibling vm-9.log-enable), the ESX hypervisor writes the VM's events to vmware.log. The log.rotateSize parameter sets the file size at which the hypervisor rotates the current vmware.log and starts a new one. The default is 2048000 bytes (2 MB).

This row primarily exists to audit that the default has not been raised or lowered. Setting it explicitly to 2048000 produces the same behavior as leaving it absent; both satisfy the baseline. The total per-VM log footprint is approximately (rotation size) * log.keepOld (governed by sibling vm-9.log-retention), so changes here interact directly with the retention count.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing this setting to its default does not affect workload behavior. Lowering the value rotates more frequently and produces smaller, more numerous log files; raising it produces fewer, larger log files.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must limit PCI device passthrough functionality.
Discussion:
VMDirectPath I/O (PCI passthrough) assigns a physical PCI function directly to a VM, bypassing the hypervisor for I/O on that function. Legitimate uses include dedicated GPUs (compute, graphics, AI/ML), high-performance NICs (SmartNICs, DPUs), and storage HBAs assigned to virtualized storage or backup appliances. The trade-off is that passthrough breaks vSphere features that depend on the hypervisor handling device access: vMotion, DRS load balancing, and vSphere HA restart cannot move the VM while the passthrough device is held by it.

Passthrough also gives the VM direct access to the underlying PCI function. The strength of guest-to-host isolation through the device depends on the IOMMU and the device's own integrity, both of which become trust-critical when passthrough is in use. Limit passthrough to VMs that genuinely require it, walk the VM fleet to inventory which VMs have passthrough devices attached, confirm each assignment corresponds to a documented business need, and remove any unexpected attachments.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing this control on VMs that have no passthrough devices does not affect workload behavior. Removing a legitimate passthrough device (for example, a GPU assigned to a VDI or AI workload) disconnects the device from the VM and breaks the workload that depends on it; verify a passthrough attachment is unexpected before removing it.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must not use independent, nonpersistent disks.
Discussion:
A VMware virtual disk normally operates in persistent mode: every write the guest makes is committed to the underlying VMDK and survives power cycles. Independent nonpersistent mode discards every write when the VM is powered off or reverted; the disk effectively resets to a known clean state on every reboot. The security risk is that an attacker who compromises a VM running in independent nonpersistent mode can install tools, exfiltrate data, or perform other malicious activity, then reboot the VM to wipe the forensic evidence of what they did. Without persistent disk state, the post-incident investigation has nothing local to examine.

Walk the VM fleet for hard disks configured in independent nonpersistent mode and confirm each one corresponds to a legitimate use case (read-only application presentation kiosks, certain testing or sandbox patterns, ephemeral compute workloads). Where the disk mode is unjustified, change it to persistent. Even where independent nonpersistent disks are legitimate, forward activity within those VMs to a remote log collector so that a forensic record exists outside the VM.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing this control on VMs that have no independent nonpersistent disks does not affect workload behavior. Changing a disk's mode from independent nonpersistent to persistent makes guest writes survive reboot, which is the security goal but can alter the operational characteristics of workloads that depended on the reset-on-reboot behavior (kiosks, transient sandbox tests, certain VDI floating-pool patterns); confirm the intent of the existing mode before changing it.
PowerCLI Command Assessment: Get-VM -Name $VM | Get-HardDisk | Select-Object Parent, Name, Persistence

Description/Title: Virtual machines must remove unnecessary virtual hardware.
Discussion:
VMs accumulate virtual hardware over time. Default templates often include floppy, serial, parallel, and CD/DVD device emulation that few modern guest workloads use. Hardware also arrives through migrations from older platforms, through clone-from-template operations that copy stale device lists, and through deliberate operator additions that outlive their reason. Each device adds a small surface that the hypervisor must emulate and the guest exposes; floppy and CD/DVD drives in particular can be used to introduce or extract content out-of-band by attaching ISOs or floppy images.

Walk each production VM's hardware list and remove any device the workload does not need. The categories most commonly unnecessary are floppy drives, serial ports, parallel ports, USB controllers (where no USB pass-through or VMRC USB redirection is needed), HD Audio devices, and CD/DVD drives that are not actively being used for a software install or maintenance window. Removing a CD/DVD drive permanently can interfere with VMware Tools installation, so prefer disconnecting the drive (leaving it present but with no media attached) over removing the device entirely on VMs that may need it later. Take a snapshot of the VM before making hardware changes so that any change that turns out to break the workload can be reverted along with the snapshot.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing VMs that already have no unnecessary devices does not affect workload behavior. Removing a CD/DVD drive can interfere with VMware Tools installation and patching workflows; prefer disconnecting the drive (no media attached) over removing it on VMs that may need it later. Removing the USB / XHCI controller can interrupt console keyboard and mouse input for guest OSes that bind those input devices to it. Some devices (serial, parallel, floppy) require the VM to be powered off before removal.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must not be able to obtain host information from the hypervisor.
Discussion:
The VMware Guest Library (vmGuestLib) is an API that VMware Tools exposes to the guest operating system for querying information about the VM's environment. The host-information subset of this API returns details about the underlying ESX host the VM is running on: host CPU model, clock speed and core count, host memory size, hostname, and similar identifying data. When this subset is disabled (tools.guestlib.enableHostInfo = FALSE), the guest sees only its own allocated resources, not the host's properties.

The risk of leaving host information available to the guest is that an attacker who compromises a VM gains a fingerprint of the underlying host that narrows their uncertainty about the platform (hardware identity, host name, cluster size). Default FALSE removes that fingerprinting path. This row primarily exists to audit that the default has not been changed. A small number of legitimate workloads (host-aware licensing checks, certain performance monitoring agents) need the host-info path; for those, the setting is enabled deliberately and the row becomes a per-VM exemption review.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing this setting to its secure default does not affect workload behavior for typical guest workloads. Explicitly setting the parameter to TRUE re-enables host-information queries for the guest, which some legitimate workloads (host-aware licensing, certain performance monitoring) require; on those VMs the change is intentional and this row becomes a per-VM exemption.

Description/Title: The guest OS must enable Secure Boot.
Discussion:
Secure Boot is a UEFI firmware feature that validates the cryptographic signature of every component in the early boot chain (firmware, boot loader, kernel, and signed drivers) before allowing it to execute. If any component is missing a valid signature, the firmware refuses to boot. Modern guest operating systems (current Windows Server, current Linux distributions, ESX itself) ship with the keys and tools to participate in this chain. Secure Boot mitigates persistent malware that hooks the boot path (boot kits, kernel rootkits) by refusing to load unsigned or tampered components at startup.

A VM uses Secure Boot only if the VM is configured with EFI firmware (not legacy BIOS) and the Secure Boot option is enabled in VM Options. Enable Secure Boot at VM creation when the guest OS supports it. Turning Secure Boot on after the guest is installed often requires reinstalling or reconfiguring the guest bootloader to a signed configuration; consult the guest OS documentation for the procedure.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Enabling Secure Boot on a VM that is not already configured for EFI + Secure Boot can prevent the VM from booting if the guest bootloader was not installed with a valid signature; this is the change the Discussion's "may involve more process than just checking the box" warning covers. Disabling Secure Boot on a VM that already relies on it removes the boot-chain integrity check, returning the VM to the unsigned-boot-component exposure the control was set to close.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must restrict sharing of memory pages between VMs.
Discussion:
Transparent Page Sharing (TPS) is an ESX memory-efficiency feature that deduplicates identical 4 KB memory pages across VMs on the same host, so a single physical page is mapped into multiple VMs that happen to hold identical content. TPS is also a documented side-channel risk: an attacker in one VM can detect that another VM holds particular content by timing how long writes to specific pages take, and use that signal to leak information about a co-tenant's memory contents.

Transparent Page Sharing has practical limits in normal operation that narrow this surface. ESX only deduplicates small (4 KB) pages, and modern hosts back guest memory with large (2 MB) pages by default. Large pages are broken down to small pages and considered for sharing only when the host comes under memory pressure, so on a host that is not memory-constrained, TPS does not deduplicate at all regardless of the salting configuration.

ESX disables inter-VM TPS by default through host-level salting: each VM receives a distinct salt, and only pages that share both salt and content qualify for deduplication, which means a VM with a default salt does not share pages with other VMs. The per-VM sched.mem.pshare.salt advanced parameter is an override that, if set, allows a VM to opt back in by sharing a salt with another VM. The recommended state is for this parameter to be absent (Not Present), so each VM relies on the host-level salting and stays isolated from inter-VM TPS. Audit each VM to confirm this advanced parameter has not been added.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing this setting to its absent default does not affect workload behavior; ESX continues to apply per-VM salting at the host level. Adding the sched.mem.pshare.salt advanced parameter to a VM is the only configuration that opens the inter-VM TPS path, which is rarely desirable on a multi-tenant or security-sensitive host.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must use a minimum supported virtual hardware version.
Discussion:
Each VM is configured with a virtual hardware version (vmx-N) that determines which virtual devices, CPU features, and platform capabilities the hypervisor exposes to the guest. Newer hardware versions add features the guest can use, including platform-security features (virtual TPM, memory protection options, newer CPU instruction sets) that older versions do not expose, and they also retire obsolete emulated devices. A guest running on an old hardware version cannot use the newer features even when the underlying ESX host supports them.

Set a minimum floor of vmx-17: no virtual machine should run on a hardware version older than this. Versions below the floor predate the platform-security features above and continue to present long-obsolete emulated devices to the guest, enlarging the attack surface for no functional benefit. Audit the fleet for VMs below the floor and raise them to at least vmx-17.

Beyond the floor, upgrade each VM to the highest hardware version it can use, bounded by operational reality rather than pushed to the newest version blindly. Before upgrading, verify that every environment the VM might run in supports the target version: not only the primary cluster, but also disaster recovery sites, secondary datacenters, and any standby clusters the VM might be restored to. A VM upgraded to a version newer than a recovery environment supports cannot run there until that environment is also upgraded, which can stall a recovery during a real incident. Test hardware-version upgrades on non-production VMs first, and take a snapshot before the upgrade so the change can be reverted along with the snapshot if the guest does not boot cleanly. Do not change the hardware version of VMware-supplied virtual appliances (vCenter, NSX, Operations); their configurations are managed by the vendor and unsupported changes can disrupt the appliance.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Virtual machines below the vmx-17 floor are the priority to remediate. Upgrading hardware version exposes the guest to newer virtual devices and CPU features; some guest operating systems will require a driver update or boot adjustment, and a small number of legacy guest operating systems will not boot at all on newer hardware versions. Take a snapshot before the change so it can be rolled back along with the snapshot if the guest does not boot cleanly. A VM upgraded to a version newer than the cluster it migrates to or recovers into cannot start in that environment.
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
# Upgrade the VM to the latest hardware version its host supports (the current maximum), via a
# VirtualMachineConfigSpec reconfigure. First verify every environment the VM may run in (DR,
# secondary, standby) supports that version. Snapshot first; do not run against VMware-supplied
# appliances (vCenter, NSX, Operations).
$vmObj = Get-VM -Name $VM
$eb = Get-View (Get-View $vmObj.VMHost.ExtensionData.Parent).EnvironmentBrowser
$max = ($eb.QueryConfigOptionDescriptor() | Sort-Object { [int]($_.Key -replace 'vmx-','') })[-1].Key
$spec = New-Object VMware.Vim.VirtualMachineConfigSpec
$spec.Version = $max   # to pin a lower version for a DR-constrained VM, set e.g. "vmx-19"
$vmObj.ExtensionData.ReconfigVM_Task($spec)

Description/Title: Virtual machines must require encryption for vMotion.
Discussion:
vMotion migrates a running VM between ESX hosts with no observable downtime by streaming the VM's working memory and CPU register state from the source host to the destination host over the vMotion network, then atomically switching execution to the new host. That stream carries everything the VM is doing in memory at that instant: credentials being parsed, in-flight requests, decrypted database pages, secret material. Anyone with access to the vMotion network can read it.

vMotion encryption applies 256-bit AES-GCM to the migration stream to close that read-on-the-wire path. The default mode opportunistic enables encryption when both source and destination support it and silently falls back to plaintext when they do not; on any modern host with AES-NI this almost always means encryption but does not require it. The recommended mode required makes encryption mandatory: vMotion will fail rather than migrate to a destination host that cannot negotiate an encrypted channel, removing the silent plaintext fallback path.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: This audit applies to most VMs in a cluster, since vMotion is a universally-available migration path. In clusters where every host supports encrypted vMotion (any modern VCF environment), the change has no operational impact because the migration already uses encryption under the opportunistic default. In a cluster with hosts that lack encrypted-vMotion support, setting required blocks vMotion to those hosts until they are upgraded; verify cluster homogeneity before applying.

Description/Title: Virtual machines must be configured to lock when the last console connection is closed.
Discussion:
The VMware Remote Console (VMRC) is a console connection to a VM's display, typically used by administrators to interact with the guest as if at a physical screen. After a VMRC user disconnects, the underlying guest OS session remains logged in unless the guest itself locks the screen. An administrator who has authenticated into a Windows guest through VMRC, then closes the console without locking, leaves the desktop session active and accessible to anyone who reconnects to that VM's console with VM-console-interact permission in vCenter.

Setting tools.guest.desktop.autolock to TRUE causes VMware Tools to invoke the Windows lock-workstation API when the last VMRC user disconnects, effectively pressing Win+L on the guest's behalf. This setting applies only to Windows VMs with VMware Tools installed and running; it has no effect on Linux or other non-Windows guests, which provide their own in-guest session-locking mechanisms. Audit Windows VMs to confirm the setting is in place; leave non-Windows VMs out of scope for this row.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Setting this control to TRUE causes the guest Windows desktop to lock the moment the last VMRC user disconnects, which is the security goal. The operational caveat is for Windows VMs that legitimately host a kiosk-style or service-display workflow where the desktop must remain unlocked for an external viewer; for those rare cases, leave the setting at the default and document the exception. Non-Windows VMs are unaffected by this setting and need no action.

Description/Title: vSAN automatic policy management must remain disabled.
Discussion:
vSAN Automatic Policy Management is a vSAN ESA feature that adjusts the default storage policy based on changes in cluster topology (host count, fault domains, and similar). The side effect is that storage policy can change without an operator explicitly approving the new configuration, which makes capacity planning, performance tuning, and compliance review harder.

In VCF 9.1, Auto-RAID supersedes Automatic Policy Management. Auto-RAID selects the RAID layout for each object based on cluster size and policy requirements, removing the need for a separate process to retarget policy as the cluster scales. Leave Automatic Policy Management disabled and rely on Auto-RAID and explicitly-chosen storage policies for placement decisions. Audit each vSAN ESA cluster to confirm Automatic Policy Management is not enabled.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing this control to its disabled default does not affect cluster operations. Enabling Automatic Policy Management allows vSAN to change the default storage policy without explicit operator approval; in 9.1, this also overlaps with Auto-RAID's layout selection and creates redundant or conflicting policy-change paths.
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
$clusterObj = Get-Cluster -Name $Cluster
$cfgSys = Get-VsanView -Id "VsanVcClusterConfigSystem-vsan-cluster-config-system"
$spec = New-Object VMware.Vsan.Views.VimVsanReconfigSpec
$spec.Modify = $true
$spec.VsanEsaConfig = New-Object VMware.Vsan.Views.VsanEsaConfig
$spec.VsanEsaConfig.DatastoreDefaultPolicySelectionConfig = New-Object VMware.Vsan.Views.VsanDatastoreDefaultPolicySelectionConfig
$spec.VsanEsaConfig.DatastoreDefaultPolicySelectionConfig.Enabled = $false
$cfgSys.VsanClusterReconfig($clusterObj.ExtensionData.MoRef, $spec)

Description/Title: vSAN ESA automatic RAID management must remain enabled.
Discussion:
vSAN protects each stored object by spreading redundant copies or parity across hosts, using either mirroring (RAID-1) or erasure coding (RAID-5/6). The RAID layout an object can use depends on how many hosts the cluster has, because erasure coding needs a minimum host count to place its data and parity fragments on separate hosts. How many host or disk failures an object can survive is its failure tolerance. When a cluster grows or shrinks, a RAID layout that was valid before can become a poor fit, leaving objects unable to achieve their intended failure tolerance or unable to rebuild efficiently after a failure.

vSAN ESA's automatic RAID management lets the cluster select and maintain an appropriate RAID layout for the current cluster size and the policy assigned to each object, rather than requiring an operator to curate RAID levels by hand. Keeping it enabled means stored data continues to meet an appropriate level of redundancy as the cluster changes, which is an availability and recoverability property: data running below its intended redundancy is more exposed to loss from a subsequent failure. The feature ships enabled and adds no workload resource cost, so the recommendation is to confirm it stays on. It is the 9.1 successor to automatic policy management (vsan-9.auto-policy-management), which is correspondingly left disabled.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling automatic RAID management shifts responsibility for choosing and maintaining RAID layouts back to manually authored storage policies; a layout that no longer matches the cluster's host count can leave objects unable to satisfy their failure tolerance or slow to rebuild after a failure. Leaving it enabled, the default, adds no workload resource cost.
Setting Location: UI: Cluster > Configure > vSAN > Services > vSAN ESA Options
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
$clusterObj = Get-Cluster -Name $Cluster
$cfgSys = Get-VsanView -Id "VsanVcClusterConfigSystem-vsan-cluster-config-system"
$spec = New-Object VMware.Vsan.Views.VimVsanReconfigSpec
$spec.Modify = $true
$spec.VsanEsaConfig = New-Object VMware.Vsan.Views.VsanEsaConfig
$spec.VsanEsaConfig.AutoRAIDConfig = New-Object VMware.Vsan.Views.VsanAutoRAIDConfig
$spec.VsanEsaConfig.AutoRAIDConfig.AssumeAutoManagedRAID = $true
$cfgSys.VsanClusterReconfig($clusterObj.ExtensionData.MoRef, $spec)

Description/Title: vSAN must enable automatic data rebalancing.
Discussion:
vSAN distributes each object across the storage devices in the cluster according to its storage policy. Over time, capacity utilization across individual disks and hosts can drift apart: maintenance evacuations, host additions, policy reconfigurations, and routine workload churn all leave the cluster with some disks more full than others. If a single device fills past the rebalance threshold while the cluster as a whole has free space, write performance to objects on the affected host degrades, and the disk can hit a fully-allocated condition that prevents new object placement on that device.

vSAN Automatic Rebalance watches per-disk utilization and moves objects in the background when imbalance crosses a configurable threshold. The rebalance work uses background I/O and does not require operator intervention. Enabling it on every vSAN cluster keeps capacity distribution converging on the cluster average without the operator having to schedule manual rebalance operations. Disabling it leaves an imbalanced cluster to remain imbalanced until an operator triggers manual rebalance through the UI.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Enabling Automatic Rebalance has no direct operational impact beyond background I/O activity during rebalance operations. Leaving it disabled allows capacity imbalance to accumulate over time, increasing the risk of a per-disk fully-allocated condition while the cluster as a whole still has free space; in a fleet without manual rebalance discipline, this can produce write failures on otherwise-healthy clusters.

Description/Title: vSAN must protect data at rest.
Discussion:
vSAN Data-at-Rest Encryption applies AES encryption to every block stored on the cluster's storage devices. When a host loses physical custody of a disk (theft, decommissioning, RMA return, drive failure return-to-vendor), the data on that disk cannot be read without the encryption key, which is held by a configured key provider. Encryption is configured per-cluster and applies to every object stored on the cluster regardless of storage policy.

Enabling Data-at-Rest Encryption requires a key provider; in VCF the Native Key Provider (NKP) is built-in and removes the need for an external Key Management Server (KMS), though external KMS is also supported. The encryption configuration can be changed while the cluster is operational: on vSAN OSA, enabling triggers a rolling reformat of disk groups; on vSAN ESA, the cluster rewrites stored objects to the new key. Both run in the background and do not require workloads to be powered off, though they take significant time to complete on populated clusters.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Enabling Data-at-Rest Encryption adds CPU work for encrypt/decrypt operations and can increase storage latency. The magnitude depends on workload I/O patterns and underlying hardware: modern CPUs with AES-NI handle the encryption work with minimal overhead, while older hardware can show measurable latency increase under heavy I/O. The enable operation itself runs in the background while workloads continue; on a heavily-populated cluster it takes significant time to complete.
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
$clusterObj = Get-Cluster -Name $Cluster
$cfgSys = Get-VsanView -Id "VsanVcClusterConfigSystem-vsan-cluster-config-system"
# Data-at-Rest Encryption requires a configured key provider (KMS); reference it by name.
$spec = New-Object VMware.Vsan.Views.VimVsanReconfigSpec
$spec.Modify = $true
$spec.DataEncryptionConfig = New-Object VMware.Vsan.Views.VsanDataEncryptionConfig
$spec.DataEncryptionConfig.EncryptionEnabled = $true
$spec.DataEncryptionConfig.KmsProviderId = New-Object VMware.Vim.KeyProviderId
$spec.DataEncryptionConfig.KmsProviderId.Id = "<key provider name>"
$cfgSys.VsanClusterReconfig($clusterObj.ExtensionData.MoRef, $spec)

Description/Title: vSAN must protect data in transit between hosts.
Discussion:
vSAN replicates and migrates objects between hosts in the cluster constantly: writes from any host go to every host that holds a replica of the object, repair traffic shuffles components after a failure, and rebalance operations move objects between disks. All of that traffic crosses the vSAN network. Without in-transit encryption, an attacker with access to that network segment could read object content directly off the wire and observe replication patterns.

On vSAN ESA, enabling Data-at-Rest Encryption automatically enables Data-in-Transit Encryption using AES-256 with forward secrecy; the two are coupled. Traffic between data hosts and any witness host is also encrypted. The rekey interval is configurable when in-transit is enabled directly; the default interval is appropriate for most environments. Audit each vSAN ESA cluster to confirm in-transit encryption is enabled (which it will be if Data-at-Rest is enabled per vsan-9.encryption-rest). This control governs local data-in-transit encryption between hosts within the vSAN cluster; in a disaggregated vSAN Storage Cluster deployment, the cross-cluster compute-to-storage traffic is protected separately by vsan-9.storage-cluster-traffic-protection.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: When Data-at-Rest is enabled on a vSAN ESA cluster, Data-in-Transit is enabled automatically at no additional configuration cost. If Data-at-Rest is disabled, enabling Data-in-Transit on its own adds CPU work for encrypt/decrypt of replica and rebalance traffic; the magnitude depends on workload write patterns and underlying hardware.
Installation Default Value: Enabled if using vSAN Data-at-Rest Protections

Description/Title: vSAN must protect data in transit between hosts.
Discussion:
vSAN replicates and migrates objects between hosts in the cluster constantly: writes from any host go to every host that holds a replica of the object, repair traffic shuffles components after a failure, and rebalance operations move objects between disks. All of that traffic crosses the vSAN network. Without in-transit encryption, an attacker with access to that network segment could read object content directly off the wire and observe replication patterns.

On vSAN OSA, Data-in-Transit Encryption is a separate cluster setting from Data-at-Rest Encryption; it must be explicitly enabled. The encryption uses AES-256 with forward secrecy, and traffic between data hosts and any witness host is also encrypted. The rekey interval is configurable; the default interval is appropriate for most environments. Enable Data-in-Transit Encryption on every vSAN OSA cluster. This control governs local data-in-transit encryption between hosts within the vSAN cluster; in a disaggregated vSAN Storage Cluster deployment, the cross-cluster compute-to-storage traffic is protected separately by vsan-9.storage-cluster-traffic-protection.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Enabling Data-in-Transit Encryption on a vSAN OSA cluster adds CPU work for encrypt/decrypt of replica, repair, and rebalance traffic. The magnitude depends on workload write patterns and underlying hardware: modern CPUs with AES-NI handle the encryption work with minimal overhead, while older hardware can show measurable latency increase under heavy I/O. Leaving Data-in-Transit Encryption disabled keeps vSAN inter-host traffic readable to anyone with access to the vSAN network segment.

Description/Title: NFS file shares on vSAN File Services must be configured to restrict access.
Discussion:
vSAN File Services lets a vSAN cluster present NFS file shares directly from the cluster's distributed storage. When a new NFS share is created, it ships with No Access (no client is permitted), which is a safe starting state but renders the share unusable. To make the share serve clients, an operator must configure access. The vSAN file share access modes are No Access, Read Only (any client can read), Read/Write (any client can read and write), and Customize Net Access (an explicit per-client or per-network allowlist with specified read or read/write permission).

Use Customize Net Access to enumerate which clients or networks are permitted to mount the share and what permission they hold (read or read/write). Avoid the unrestricted Read Only and Read/Write modes for production shares: those modes grant their permission to every NFS client that can reach the cluster's file services interface, expanding the share's exposure to anything on the same network segment. This control applies only when vSAN File Services with NFS shares is in use; clusters without it can skip the audit.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Configuring a curated Customize Net Access policy restricts the share to the listed clients. NFS clients not on the allowlist that previously mounted via an unrestricted mode will lose access; review existing client usage before tightening. Leaving an NFS share at an unrestricted mode (Read Only or Read/Write) keeps the share open to every reachable client.

Description/Title: SMB file shares on vSAN File Services must require protocol encryption.
Discussion:
SMB Protocol Encryption is an SMB 3.0+ transport-layer feature that encrypts every byte of an SMB session, not only the authentication handshake. It defends against wire sniffing of file content, directory listings, and metadata between an SMB client and the vSAN File Services server. Without protocol encryption, anyone with access to the network segment carrying the SMB session can read the file traffic in clear.

vSAN File Services SMB shares ship with Protocol Encryption disabled. Enable it on each SMB share to require encrypted sessions. Clients that support SMB 3.0 or newer (current Windows, Samba 4.0+, current macOS, current Linux SMB clients) connect normally with encryption negotiated; clients restricted to SMB 1 or SMB 2 cannot connect to an encryption-required share. This control applies only when vSAN File Services with SMB shares is in use; clusters without it can skip the audit.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Enabling Protocol Encryption on an SMB share has no operational impact on modern SMB 3.0+ clients, which negotiate encryption transparently. Clients restricted to SMB 1 or SMB 2 will fail to connect to an encryption-required share; inventory connecting clients and confirm SMB 3.0+ support before enabling on shares with mixed-version client populations. Leaving Protocol Encryption disabled keeps SMB session content readable to anyone with access to the network path.

Description/Title: vSAN must not provision storage when storage policy requirements cannot be met.
Discussion:
Every vSAN storage policy defines requirements that the cluster must meet when provisioning the objects assigned to that policy: how many host failures the object can tolerate, how many disk stripes the data is spread across, and similar placement and redundancy attributes. Normally, when a new object is being provisioned and the cluster cannot satisfy the policy (because of insufficient hosts, capacity, or fault domains), vSAN refuses the provisioning request, surfacing the constraint violation to the operator.

The "Force Provisioning" option on a storage policy overrides that refusal: when set, vSAN creates the object even though one or more policy requirements cannot be met, marking the object as non-compliant. The original intent was to support cluster-bootstrapping and outage-recovery cases where partial provisioning is preferable to no provisioning. In normal operation, leaving Force Provisioning enabled lets workloads quietly land in non-compliant states; the operator believes the policy is in force, but the redundancy or performance attributes the policy was meant to enforce may not actually be present. Keep Force Provisioning disabled on every production storage policy.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing this setting to its disabled default does not affect cluster operations. Enabling Force Provisioning on a storage policy allows objects to be created in non-compliant states, hiding policy violations from routine inspection and removing the operator's ability to trust that the policy's stated redundancy and performance attributes are actually in place.
Setting Location: UI: Policies and Profiles, VM Storage Policies

Description/Title: Disable or proxy the vSAN Health Check Hardware Compatibility List (HCL) download.
Discussion:
vSAN Health Check can reach out to Broadcom over the internet to download the Hardware Compatibility List (HCL), the database it uses to confirm that a cluster's storage controllers, drives, and firmware are on the supported list. By default this runs as a direct outbound connection from vCenter.

vCenter sits in the management plane, and an outbound internet path from it is available whether or not the HCL check needs it; reducing unnecessary egress from the management plane is a standard hardening step. Where the HCL check is not needed, disable the download and rely on the offline HCL copy bundled with vCenter. Where it is needed, route it through an organization-approved forward proxy, an intermediary that brokers and can log the outbound request, so the connection is controlled rather than direct. This control applies only to vCenter instances that have one or more vSAN-enabled clusters.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling the download means hardware compliance is checked against the offline HCL copy bundled with vCenter, which may lag the current Broadcom HCL, so newly certified or de-certified hardware may not be reflected until the copy is updated. Leaving the direct download enabled keeps an outbound internet path open from the management plane.
Baseline Suggested Value: Disabled, or Enabled with an organization-approved proxy
Setting Location: UI: vSphere Client > Hosts and Clusters > [vCenter] > Configure > vSAN > Internet Connectivity
PowerCLI Command Assessment:
$vsanphview = Get-VsanView -Id VsanPhoneHomeSystem-vsan-phonehome-system
$vsanphview.QueryVsanCloudHealthStatus()
# If InternetConnectivity is true, also verify a custom proxy:
$vsanhealthview = Get-VsanView -Id VsanVcClusterHealthSystem-vsan-cluster-health-system
$vsanhealthview.VsanHealthQueryVsanProxyConfig()
PowerCLI Command Remediation: N/A (No PowerCLI remediation available; configure via the vSphere Client.)

Description/Title: vSAN iSCSI target must enable bidirectional/mutual CHAP authentication.
Discussion:
The vSAN iSCSI Target Service lets a vSAN cluster present block storage to iSCSI initiators (typically physical hosts, non-VCF workloads, or other clusters that need vSAN-backed LUNs). iSCSI offers three authentication modes: None (no authentication, initiators identified only by network address), One-Way CHAP (the target authenticates the initiator with a shared secret), and Mutual CHAP (both the initiator and the target authenticate each other with separate secrets).

Without Mutual CHAP, an attacker who can reach the iSCSI target network can impersonate either side of the connection. Impersonating the initiator gets unauthorized access to LUNs; impersonating the target intercepts the initiator's reads and writes. Mutual CHAP closes both of these impersonation paths. CHAP is an authentication mechanism only; it does not encrypt iSCSI traffic on the wire. Protect the iSCSI network segment with IPsec or comparable wire-level protection when confidentiality of iSCSI data in flight is required.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Enabling Mutual CHAP requires every iSCSI initiator that connects to the target to be configured with both an outgoing credential (used to authenticate to the target) and an incoming credential the target presents (used to verify the target). Initiators previously connecting with One-Way CHAP or no authentication will fail to mount LUNs until they are reconfigured with the mutual credentials. Plan the credential rollout in coordination with the initiator side before enabling on a production target.

Description/Title: vSAN must disable managed disk claims.
Discussion:
When vSAN Managed Disk Claim is enabled, ESX hosts automatically claim any compatible local storage device they see and add it to the cluster's vSAN datastore. The intent is operational convenience: bring up a new host, and its disks become cluster capacity without operator intervention. The risk is that the auto-claim runs without distinguishing between disks the operator intended for vSAN and disks present for other reasons (replacement disks staged but not yet planned in, disks left over from a prior workload, drives in a state the operator has not yet inspected). An auto-claim of an unintended disk can wipe its existing contents or commit capacity to vSAN that the operator did not approve.

Leave Managed Disk Claim disabled in normal operation and claim disks manually when capacity additions are planned. Enabling it temporarily during a known capacity-add window, with active monitoring, is a reasonable workflow; leaving it on permanently is not.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Disabling Managed Disk Claim requires operators to claim disks manually when adding capacity to the cluster, which is a small operational overhead at the time of each capacity addition. Leaving it enabled can lead to unintended disks being absorbed into the cluster, with potential data loss on those disks and unplanned capacity changes to vSAN.
PowerCLI Command Remediation:
$clusterObj = Get-Cluster -Name $Cluster
$cfgSys = Get-VsanView -Id "VsanVcClusterConfigSystem-vsan-cluster-config-system"
$spec = New-Object VMware.Vsan.Views.VimVsanReconfigSpec
$spec.Modify = $true
$spec.VsanEsaConfig = New-Object VMware.Vsan.Views.VsanEsaConfig
$spec.VsanEsaConfig.HclDiskClaimEnabled = $false
$cfgSys.VsanClusterReconfig($clusterObj.ExtensionData.MoRef, $spec)

Description/Title: Isolate vSAN iSCSI Target traffic on dedicated VMkernel interfaces.
Discussion:
The vSAN iSCSI Target Service exposes block storage from a vSAN cluster to initiators that live outside the cluster's management plane: physical hosts, non-VCF workloads, or other clusters. That makes vSAN iSCSI Target traffic the one piece of vSAN traffic that legitimately crosses cluster boundaries. The other vSAN traffic types (cluster internal communications, replication, vMotion, management) should stay inside the cluster's private fabrics.

Configure the iSCSI Target Service on its own VMkernel network interfaces and place those interfaces on a network segment dedicated to iSCSI Target. Apply perimeter controls on that segment using Distributed Port Group Traffic Filtering and Marking, NSX Distributed Firewall, or external network security controls, so the iSCSI initiator hosts can reach only the target service and not the rest of the cluster's management plane. Isolating iSCSI Target traffic in this way also makes capacity planning and performance troubleshooting easier, since iSCSI-related traffic is not mixed with internal cluster traffic.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Configuring dedicated VMkernel interfaces for iSCSI Target traffic requires a one-time network design step (additional VMkernel ports, dedicated port groups, perimeter rules). Once in place, there is no ongoing operational cost. Skipping the isolation lets iSCSI initiator clients reach the same network paths the cluster uses for its internal management plane, expanding the attack surface that any one compromised initiator presents.

Description/Title: vSAN must calculate object checksums to protect data integrity.
Discussion:
vSAN uses object-level checksumming to detect data corruption in flight or at rest. On every read and write, vSAN computes a checksum of the data and compares it against the stored checksum; a mismatch indicates corruption, after which vSAN attempts to repair the affected component by reading the data from another healthy replica and rewriting the corrupted copy. Without checksumming, silent data corruption (a bit flip on a disk surface, a memory error during a write, a controller bug) can persist undetected and propagate to backups and replicas.

Object checksumming is governed by the per-policy attribute "Disable object checksum." Keep this attribute at its default value (not set), which leaves checksumming enabled. On vSAN ESA, object checksums are always computed and cannot be disabled regardless of policy. On vSAN OSA, the policy attribute is the audit point: verify each storage policy in use has not set "Disable object checksum" to true.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Auditing this control on vSAN ESA clusters is automatically satisfied (ESA always computes checksums). On vSAN OSA, leaving checksumming enabled adds a small constant CPU cost per I/O for hash computation; modern hardware absorbs this with minimal overhead. Disabling object checksums removes the ability to detect silent data corruption at the storage layer and is rarely desirable.
Setting Location: UI: Policies and Profiles, VM Storage Policies

Description/Title: vSAN must reserve space to complete internal maintenance operations.
Discussion:
vSAN performs internal operations that require temporary free space on the cluster: object rebuilds after a failure, policy reconfigurations that change the placement of existing data, and rebalance moves that shift objects between disks. When the cluster runs near capacity and one of these operations is triggered, vSAN may not have room to complete the operation, which can leave objects in degraded states or block the operation entirely.

Operations Reserve is a per-cluster capacity slack that vSAN sets aside for these internal operations. With Operations Reserve enabled, vSAN refuses workload provisioning that would consume the reserve, keeping the cluster's effective workload capacity below the physical maximum and giving the rebuild, rebalance, and reconfiguration paths capacity to run in. Enable Operations Reserve on every vSAN cluster. Host Reserve, a separate setting that reserves one host's worth of capacity for tolerating host failure, is a design choice the operator weighs against its per-cluster resource cost; the SCG does not mandate Host Reserve.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Enabling Operations Reserve reduces the cluster's workload-available capacity by the size of the reserve. The reduction is typically a small percentage of total cluster capacity, and it is what gives internal operations (rebuilds, policy changes, rebalances) capacity to run on a populated cluster. Leaving Operations Reserve disabled lets workloads consume the cluster up to the physical capacity, which improves headline capacity utilization but increases the risk that an internal operation cannot complete when needed, leaving objects in degraded states or blocking the operation.
Setting Location: UI: vSAN Configuration, Reservations and Alerts

Description/Title: Re-issue vSAN encryption Key Encryption Keys (KEKs) on a regular schedule.
Discussion:
The Key Encryption Key (KEK) for a vSAN encrypted datastore is issued by the Key Management Server (KMS) and wraps the on-host Disk Encryption Key (DEK). A shallow rekey rewraps the DEK with a new KEK without re-encrypting on-disk data. Performing this rekey on a regular cadence bounds the operational lifetime of a KEK and limits the useful window of any key material exposed by a KMS compromise. The rekey interval should be defined and documented by the organization as part of its key management procedure.

Not applicable to clusters without vSAN encryption in use.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Establishing a regular rekey has little operational cost: a shallow rekey rewraps the keys quickly without re-encrypting data or disrupting workloads. Leaving the KEK unrotated keeps it valid indefinitely, so any key material exposed through a KMS compromise stays useful for as long as that KEK remains in place.
Setting Location: UI: vSphere Client > Hosts and Clusters > [Cluster] > Configure > vSAN > Services > Data Services > Generate New Encryption Keys
PowerCLI Command Assessment: N/A (No PowerCLI available; this is an operational procedure verified by review of organization records.)
PowerCLI Command Remediation: N/A (No PowerCLI available; perform rekey via the vSphere Client.)

Description/Title: vSAN Storage Cluster compute-to-storage traffic must be encrypted or isolated.
Discussion:
vSAN Storage Cluster (formerly called vSAN Max) is a disaggregated storage architecture where a vSAN ESA or OSA cluster runs as dedicated storage and compute clusters mount its storage over a network connection. The compute-to-storage traffic includes all guest I/O for the workloads running on the compute side and metadata operations that drive the storage cluster's behavior. Without protection, anyone with access to the storage network can read application data, observe I/O patterns, and identify the workloads in flight.

Remote Data-in-Transit Encryption protects the cross-cluster compute-to-storage protocol using AES-256 with forward secrecy and TLS-mutual-authenticated key exchange. It is configured per remote mount, has a default 24-hour rekey interval (configurable from 30 minutes to 7 days), and can be enabled or disabled without interrupting traffic. It is separate from the local data-in-transit encryption that protects intra-cluster vSAN traffic (governed by vsan-9.encryption-transit-esa and vsan-9.encryption-transit-osa); both can be enabled simultaneously.

Protect vSAN Storage Cluster traffic by enabling Remote Data-in-Transit Encryption, by isolating the storage network on dedicated VLANs or physical interfaces, or by both as defense in depth. Encryption protects the traffic content directly; isolation reduces the population of hosts that can reach the protocol at all.
Potential Functional Impact if Default V: Enabling Remote Data-in-Transit Encryption is non-disruptive (the change does not interrupt existing traffic) and adds CPU work for encrypt/decrypt of compute-to-storage protocol traffic; modern hardware with AES-NI absorbs the cost with minimal observable impact. Network isolation requires a one-time network design step (dedicated VLANs or physical interfaces) and adds no ongoing runtime overhead. Skipping both leaves vSAN Storage Cluster compute-to-storage traffic readable to anyone with access to the network path.
